## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "Hermetic Storage" and state any two examples of hermetic storage systems used for food grains.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Hermetic Storage** is a method of preserving food grains in a sealed, airtight container where the atmospheric composition is modified by the natural respiration of the grain, insects, and microorganisms, creating a low-oxygen, high-CO₂ environment that is lethal to pests and inhibitory to mould growth.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Purdue Improved Cowpea Storage (PICS) triple-layer bags** – developed by Purdue University for cowpea storage in West Africa; now used for maize, rice, sorghum, and other grains.
2. **GrainPro Cocoons™** – large flexible PVC enclosures used at the community/village level for storing up to 300 tonnes of grain.
3. *(Optional third example)* **Super Grain Bags (SGB)** – single-layer high-density polyethylene bags used widely in South Asia for household-scale storage.

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):**
- Correct definition: 1.5 marks
- Two examples with brief description: 1.5 marks (0.75 each)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is "Safe Moisture Content" for cereals and why is it critical in traditional grain storage?**

**Model Answer:**

**Safe Moisture Content (SMC)** for cereals (rice, wheat, maize) is **12 – 14% (wet basis)**. It is the maximum moisture content at which grain can be stored safely for 6–12 months without significant mould growth, insect multiplication, or quality deterioration.

**Why it is critical:**
1. Above 14% moisture, **fungi** (e.g., *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*) actively grow and produce mycotoxins (e.g., aflatoxin B1) — a serious health hazard.
2. **Stored-grain insects** (weevils, grain borers) thrive in grain with moisture above 12%, with their metabolic rate roughly doubling for every 5°C rise in temperature.
3. In **traditional storage structures** (mud bins, earthen pots, bamboo baskets), there is **no active temperature or humidity control**, so the grain's initial moisture content is the **single most important parameter** determining storage success.
4. The **Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH)** corresponding to 14% moisture at 25°C is approximately **70%**, which is also the threshold for microbial growth (Aw = 0.70).

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):**
- Definition of SMC: 1 mark
- Numerical value: 0.5 marks
- Three critical points (any two): 1.5 marks (0.75 each)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Describe the **construction, working principle, and advantages of a Mud Bin (Kothi)** for grain storage. Discuss its limitations with reference to the humid climate of Kerala. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **construction and working principle of PICS triple bags**. Derive the **time required for oxygen concentration to drop below 5%** in a 50 kg bag of rice, given that the combined respiration rate of grain + insects + microbes is $1.5 \ \text{mL O}_2 / \text{kg} \cdot \text{day}$, bulk density is $750 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, and void fraction is 0.30. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Q.A (a):**

**Construction of Mud Bin (Kothi):**
A mud bin is constructed using the following materials and steps:
1. **Foundation:** A raised platform (30–45 cm above ground level) made of stone, brick, or compacted earth to prevent ground moisture and rodent access.
2. **Frame:** A cylindrical or rectangular frame is made using **bamboo strips or wooden poles** (typically 1.5–2.5 m in diameter, 2–3 m in height).
3. **Plastering:** The frame is coated on both sides with a mixture of **mud, cow-dung, and chopped straw** (in 3:2:1 ratio by volume). Multiple coats are applied, each allowed to dry for 2–3 days.
4. **Inner coating:** A **lime wash** (calcium hydroxide paste) is applied on the inner surface to raise pH and inhibit microbial growth.
5. **Roof:** A **conical thatched roof** (palm leaves or paddy straw) is placed on top to provide shade and allow ventilation.
6. **Lid:** A **wooden plank lid** sealed with a mud ring provides a tight closure.
7. **Rodent guard:** A **tin strip** (15 cm wide) is fixed around the plinth to prevent rodent climbing.

**Working Principle:**
- The mud wall acts as a **moisture buffer** due to its high specific heat capacity ($c_p \approx 900 \ \text{J/kg·K}$) and porosity.
- The cow-dung plaster provides **mild insect-repellent and anti-fungal properties**.
- The lime coating creates a **high-pH (pH $\sim 12$)** inner surface that deters insects and moulds.
- The thatched roof **reduces solar heat gain** and creates an air buffer above the grain.

**Advantages:**
1. **Low cost** (₹1–3 per kg of storage capacity).
2. **No external energy** required.
3. **Excellent moisture buffering** for grain stored at 12–14% moisture.
4. **Indigenous construction** using locally available materials.
5. **Long lifespan** (5–10 years with periodic re-plastering).
6. Suitable for **multiple grain types** (wheat, rice, maize, sorghum).

**Limitations in Kerala's Humid Climate:**
1. **High ambient humidity (75–95% RH)** during monsoon can cause **moisture absorption** through the porous mud walls, especially if the roof leaks.
2. The **high water table** in coastal Kerala prevents the use of **raised plinths** made from local soil without a stone/brick foundation, increasing cost.
3. **Mud-dissolving rain** can damage unprotected outer walls; requires **frequent re-plastering** (every 1–2 years).
4. **Fungal infestation** is higher in humid climates, especially for grains stored above 13% moisture.
5. **Termite attack** on the bamboo/wooden frame is a serious concern in Kerala.

---

#### **Model Answer to Q.A (b):**

**Construction and Working Principle of PICS Triple Bags:**

**Construction:**
PICS bags consist of **three layers** of plastic, each with a specific function:
- **Inner layer:** High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE) — provides the primary gas barrier.
- **Middle layer:** Another HDPE layer — provides a redundant gas barrier.
- **Outer layer:** Woven Polypropylene — provides mechanical strength and UV resistance.

The three layers are **sealed independently** using a heat-sealer or by knotting and folding. The bag is typically $50 \ \text{kg}$ or $100 \ \text{kg}$ capacity.

**Working Principle:**
Once sealed, the grain, insects, and microorganisms inside the bag **continue to respire**, consuming O₂ and releasing CO₂. Since the bag is **virtually airtight**, the O₂ concentration inside **decreases progressively** while CO₂ increases. When O₂ falls below 5% (after $\sim$ 7–14 days for moderately infested grain, longer for low infestation), most stored-product insects **die from asphyxiation**, and mould growth is inhibited due to the low water activity in anaerobic conditions.

**Derivation of Time for O₂ < 5%:**

**Step 1 — Determine the gas volume in the bag:**

$$
V_{\text{grain}} = \frac{m_{\text{grain}}}{\rho_{\text{bulk}}} = \frac{50}{750} = 0.0667 \ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{void}} = \epsilon \cdot V_{\text{grain}} = 0.30 \times 0.0667 = 0.0200 \ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{void}} = 20.0 \ \text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rate of O₂ consumption (in % per day):**

$$
\frac{dO_2}{dt} = -\frac{R_{\text{total}} \cdot m_{\text{grain}}}{V_{\text{void}}} \times \frac{1}{10}
$$

(The factor $1/10$ converts mL to % of 1 L.)

$$
\frac{dO_2}{dt} = -\frac{1.5 \times 50}{20.0 \times 10} = -\frac{75}{200} = -0.375 \ \%/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear approximation:**

$$
O_2(t) = 20.9 - 0.375 \cdot t
$$

Setting $O_2 = 5.0\%$:

$$
t = \frac{20.9 - 5.0}{0.375} = \frac{15.9}{0.375} = 42.4 \ \text{days}
$$

**Step 4 — Refined first-order kinetic model:**

$$
O_2(t) = 20.9 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}
$$

where $k = 0.375 / 20.9 = 0.01794 \ \text{day}^{-1}$.

$$
5.0 = 20.9 \cdot e^{-0.01794 \cdot t}
$$

$$
t = \frac{\ln(20.9/5.0)}{0.01794} = \frac{1.4294}{0.01794} = 79.7 \ \text{days}
$$

**Final Answer:** The oxygen concentration in the PICS bag will drop below 5% in approximately **42 days (linear model)** to **80 days (first-order model)**. The first-order model is more accurate and is the **preferred answer** in KTU exams.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Underground Pit Storage, Mud Bin (Kothi), and Bamboo Basket (Doko)** methods of grain storage with respect to construction, capacity, suitability, and limitations. Use a suitable table in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A farmer wants to store **2 tonnes of rice** in a cylindrical mud bin. The bulk density of rice is $600 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ and the void fraction of packed grain is 0.40. If the height of the bin must be **twice the diameter**, calculate the **internal diameter, height, and wall thickness** of the bin. Take wall thickness as 5% of the diameter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Q.B (a):**

**Comparative Analysis of Three Traditional Storage Methods**

| Parameter | **Underground Pit** | **Mud Bin (Kothi)** | **Bamboo Basket (Doko)** |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Construction** | Pit dug 1–3 m deep, lined with straw, sealed with mud lid | Raised cylindrical structure of bamboo frame plastered with mud–cow-dung–straw | Woven bamboo strips in cylindrical/conical shape |
| **Capacity** | 1 – 10 tonnes | 0.5 – 5 tonnes | 5 – 100 kg |
| **Working Principle** | Earth acts as thermal insulator; constant low temperature | Mud wall as moisture buffer; lime as disinfectant | Open weave allows continuous drying; poor pest barrier |
| **Airtightness** | High (sealed) | Moderate (porous) | Low (open weave) |
| **Moisture Control** | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (depends on ambient) |
| **Pest Exclusion** | Good (sealed) | Moderate | Poor |
| **Rodent Protection** | Excellent (underground) | Good (raised plinth + tin strip) | Poor |
| **Suitability in Kerala** | **Unsuitable** (high water table) | Good (with raised plinth) | Moderate |
| **Best Use** | Pulses, millets in arid regions | Cereals in semi-arid/sub-humid regions | Short-term storage of seeds/pulses in dry season |
| **Lifespan** | 5 – 15 years | 5 – 10 years | 1 – 3 years |
| **Main Limitation** | Water seepage during monsoon | Cracking of mud; re-plastering needed | Rapid pest reinfestation |

**Justification (4–5 sentences):**

The **underground pit** offers superior temperature stability and rodent protection due to its sub-soil location, but is **inherently unsuitable for Kerala** because of the high water table ($\geq 1 \ \text{m}$ during monsoon) and heavy annual rainfall ($\sim 3,000 \ \text{mm}$). The **mud bin** strikes the best balance of moisture buffering, pest exclusion, and affordability for Kerala's climate, provided the roof is leak-proof and the plinth is constructed of stone or brick. The **bamboo basket** is the **least protective** of the three and is recommended only for **short-term (1–2 months) storage** in dry seasons, primarily for seeds or pulses being prepared for sale.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Comprehensive table: 4 marks
- Justification paragraph: 2 marks
- Concluding recommendation: 1 mark

---

#### **Model Answer to Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- Mass of rice, $m = 2{,}000 \ \text{kg}$
- Bulk density, $\rho_b = 600 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Void fraction, $\epsilon = 0.40$
- Height-to-diameter ratio, $h = 2d$
- Wall thickness, $t = 0.05 \cdot d$

**Step 1 — Total volume of grain:**

$$
V_{\text{grain}} = \frac{m}{\rho_b} = \frac{2000}{600} = 3.333 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Internal volume of bin (accounting for voids):**

$$
V_{\text{bin}} = \frac{V_{\text{grain}}}{1 - \epsilon} = \frac{3.333}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{3.333}{0.60} = 5.556 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Cylindrical geometry:**

$$
V = \frac{\pi d^2 h}{4} = \frac{\pi d^2 (2d)}{4} = \frac{\pi d^3}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for diameter:**

$$
5.556 = \frac{\pi d^3}{2}
$$

$$
d^3 = \frac{5.556 \times 2}{\pi} = \frac{11.111}{3.1416} = 3.537
$$

$$
d = (3.537)^{1/3} = 1.523 \ \text{m} \approx 1.52 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute height:**

$$
h = 2d = 2 \times 1.52 = 3.05 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Wall thickness:**

$$
t = 0.05 \times d = 0.05 \times 1.52 = 0.076 \ \text{m} \approx 7.6 \ \text{cm}
$$

**Final Design Specification:**
- **Internal diameter:** 1.52 m
- **Internal height:** 3.05 m
- **Wall thickness:** 7.6 cm (mud–cow dung–straw mix)
- **Storage capacity:** 2 tonnes of rice
- **Outer diameter:** $1.52 + 2 \times 0.076 = 1.672 \ \text{m}$

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Step 1 (grain volume): 1 mark
- Step 2 (bin volume with void correction): 1 mark
- Step 3–4 (cylinder equation and d): 2 marks
- Step 5 (height): 1 mark
- Step 6 (wall thickness + final spec): 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the void fraction** is the most common mistake. Students write $V_{\text{bin}} = V_{\text{grain}}$ directly, which gives an undersized bin. **Always correct for void fraction** when designing grain storage structures.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** between bulk density and volume: bulk density in $\text{kg/m}^3$ requires volume in $\text{m}^3$, not litres.
> 3. In **comparison questions**, students often list only differences and forget **similarities** — both are required for full marks.
> 4. In **derivation questions**, the **kinetic model assumed** (linear vs. first-order) must be **explicitly stated**. Mark evaluators explicitly look for the kinetic assumption.
> 5. In **PICS bag questions**, students often forget to mention that the bag must be **stored in shade** — direct sunlight can degrade the plastic layers and increase permeability.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU examination.**

- ✅ **Safe moisture content** for cereals = **12–14%**; for oilseeds = **8–10%**; for spices = **10–12%**.
- ✅ **Three variables** controlling storage: **moisture, temperature, and atmospheric composition** (O₂, CO₂).
- ✅ **Traditional storage structures** in India include **mud bin (Kothi), earthen pot (Matka), bamboo basket (Doko), underground pit (Gudamba), thatched warehouse (Chaura), and grain silo (metal)**.
- ✅ **Mud bin advantages:** low cost, indigenous materials, moisture buffering, 5–10 year lifespan.
- ✅ **Mud bin limitations:** cracking, re-plastering, vulnerable to termites, not ideal for very humid climates.
- ✅ **Earthen pot advantage:** micro-porous clay regulates humidity; ideal for Kerala's climate.
- ✅ **Underground pit limitation:** NOT suitable for Kerala due to high water table.
- ✅ **PICS bag** is a **triple-layer hermetic storage** (HDPE + HDPE + woven PP) that creates a **self-fumigating** low-O₂ environment.
- ✅ **Hermetic principle:** O₂ depletion follows **first-order kinetics** $O_2(t) = 20.9 \cdot e^{-k t}$; lethal O₂ threshold = **<5%**.
- ✅ **Critical timing for PICS bags:** O₂ drops below 5% in **40–80 days** depending on insect load.
- ✅ **Henderson's Equation** is used to predict the **equilibrium moisture content** of grains at given $T$ and RH.
- ✅ **ERH threshold for microbial growth** = **70%** (corresponding to 14% moisture in cereals at 25°C).
- ✅ **Bulk density of rice** $\approx 600 \ \text{kg/m}^3$; **wheat** $\approx 750 \ \text{kg/m}^3$; **maize** $\approx 720 \ \text{kg/m}^3$.
- ✅ **Standard void fraction** for packed grain = **0.35 – 0.45**; use this in all bin volume calculations.
- ✅ **Metal silos** require **raised platforms (≥30 cm)** to prevent ground moisture; **white paint** to reflect solar heat.
- ✅ **Bamboo baskets** are **NOT airtight** — they are for **drying**, not long-term preservation.
- ✅ **Lime wash** in traditional bins raises surface pH to **~12**, inhibiting mould and insect oviposition.
- ✅ **Neem oil** (azadirachtin-based botanicals) is the most common **traditional insecticide** used as a grain protectant in India.
- ✅ **FAO estimates** post-harvest losses in traditional storage in India at **6–10%** for cereals and **15–25%** for pulses.
- ✅ **Kerala-specific context:** Spices (cardamom, pepper, turmeric, cloves) are traditionally stored in **cloth bags hung above kitchen hearths** — a unique hybrid of moisture control and smoke-mediated antimicrobial protection.
- ✅ **Modern improved structures** (Pusa bin, metal silo, PICS bag) combine **traditional simplicity with scientific optimization** — a key KTU 2024 module learning outcome.
- ✅ **Three exam-favourite derivations:** (i) O₂ depletion in hermetic bag, (ii) Bin volume calculation, (iii) Equilibrium moisture content via Henderson's equation.
- ✅ **Comparison matrix** (mud bin vs. earthen pot vs. bamboo basket vs. PICS bag vs. metal silo) is the **single most important table** to memorize for KTU Part B questions.

---

**End of Module 2 – Topic: Traditional and Small-Scale Storage Methods**
*PEFTT633 – Food Storage Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bag and Bulk Storage: Mud Bin, Drums, Gunny Bags, Method of Stacking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bag and Bulk Storage: Mud Bin, Drums, Gunny Bags, Method of Stacking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bag storage** refers to the preservation of food commodities (primarily grains, cereals, pulses, and oilseeds) in sealed or open woven containers such as **gunny bags (jute bags)**, **HDPE woven sacks**, and **PP (polypropylene) bags**, stacked systematically in godowns or warehouses.

**Bulk storage** denotes the containment of food grains in large, stationary structures where the commodity is stored as a continuous mass without individual packaging. Common bulk storage receptacles include **mud bins (earthen bins)**, **metal drums**, **plastic drums**, and **silos**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Food storage engineering emphasizes the principle that the *mode of storage is dictated by moisture content, storage duration, commodity type, and the required protection against biotic/abiotic factors.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine your kitchen:
- **Bulk storage** is like a **rice container (drum/canister)** at home — you pour grains in bulk, scoop out what you need, and the container is reusable.
- **Bag storage** is like **packed grocery bags** — each bag is a discrete unit, easy to count, move, and inspect individually.

**Mud bins** are the traditional "earthen pots" used by farmers for generations — built from clay-soil mixture, they leverage the natural **hygroscopic** and **insulating** properties of mud to maintain a cool, dry microclimate. Think of them as nature's "thermos flask" for grains.

### 1.3 Classification of Storage Receptacles

| Storage Type | Receptacle | Capacity Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Traditional Bulk | Mud Bin | 1 – 10 tonnes | Farm-level, short-term (3–6 months) |
| Modern Bulk | Metal/Plastic Drums | 50 – 200 kg | Small-scale, household, seed storage |
| Bag Storage | Gunny/Jute Bags | 50 – 100 kg | Warehouse stacking, commercial trade |
| Industrial Bulk | Silos (Steel/Concrete) | 100 – 10,000+ tonnes | FCI, long-term central storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Bag Weight:** A traditional Indian **gunny bag** holds **75 kg (old standard)** or **50 kg (current standard after 2012)** of foodgrains as per the Government of India grain handling norms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mud Bin (Earthen Storage Structure)

A **mud bin** is a cylindrical or rectangular structure constructed from a mixture of **clay soil, straw/cow dung, and water**, plastered on a raised earthen platform. It represents one of the oldest and most economical grain storage systems in rural India and the developing world.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Site Selection:** Choose an elevated, well-drained platform to prevent seepage of ground moisture and avoid waterlogging.
- **Step 2 — Foundation:** A circular or rectangular base is laid using compacted mud bricks or stones. Diameter typically ranges from **1.5 m to 3.0 m**.
- **Step 3 — Wall Construction:** Walls are raised in layers (15–20 cm per lift) using the mud-straw mixture, each layer allowed to sun-dry before the next is added. Total wall thickness: **10–15 cm**.
- **Step 4 — Plastering:** Inner surface is plastered with a smooth mud-cow dung slurry to minimize porosity and grain adhesion.
- **Step 5 — Roof:** A conical or domed thatched/mud roof with adequate overhang (minimum **30 cm**) is provided to prevent rain ingress.
- **Step 6 — Loading & Sealing:** Grains are sun-dried to safe moisture content (**≤ 12% for cereals**), filled into the bin, and the top sealed with a mud plaster.

**Why Mud Works:**
- **Low thermal conductivity** of mud (k ≈ 0.5 – 0.8 W/m·K) keeps the bin interior cool, reducing insect respiration and moisture migration.
- **Gas-tightness** when freshly plastered creates a **modified atmosphere** (high CO₂, low O₂) that suffocates storage pests.

### 2.2 Drums (Metal and Plastic)

**Metal Drums** are fabricated from **galvanized iron (GI)** or **mild steel (MS)** sheets, typically cylindrical with a capacity of **50–200 litres**. They are airtight when fitted with rubber-gasket lids, making them ideal for **seed storage** and **long-term hermetic storage**.

**Plastic Drums** are made of **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** or **PP (Polypropylene)**, lighter, corrosion-resistant, and cheaper than metal drums but with shorter UV-life expectancy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hermetic Storage Principle:** In a sealed drum, inter-grain respiration consumes O₂ and releases CO₂. Once O₂ drops below **2–5%** and CO₂ rises above **20%**, insects and aerobic molds are killed without the use of chemical fumigants.

### 2.3 Gunny Bags (Jute Bags)

**Gunny bags** are woven sacks made from **jute fibre (Corchorus capsularis / C. olitorius)**, traditionally used for packaging cereals, pulses, oilseeds, and sugar. They are **biodegradable**, **breathable**, and **reusable**.

**Standard Specifications (as per Indian Standards):**

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Material | Jute (Burlap) |
| Standard Capacity | 50 kg or 75 kg |
| Fabric Weight | 200 – 270 g/m² (tare weight ≈ 1 kg) |
| Moisture Permeability | High (breathable) |
| UV Resistance | Moderate |
| Reusability | 2–3 cycles (with fumigation between cycles) |

**HDPE/PP Woven Sacks** are the modern alternative — woven from high-density polyethylene or polypropylene tapes, these are **non-biodegradable** but offer superior **moisture resistance, tear strength, and pest-exclusion** properties.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bin Capacity (Volume-based) | $V = \pi \times r^2 \times h$ | m³ | Cylindrical mud bin |
| Bin Capacity (Mass-based) | $M = V \times \rho_{grain}$ | kg | $\rho_{grain}$ = bulk density of grain |
| Safe Storage Moisture (Cereals) | $M_{c} \leq 12\%$ | % wet basis | For wheat, rice, maize |
| Hermetic O₂ Threshold | $O_2 \leq 5\%$ | % by volume | Insect mortality level |
| Hermetic CO₂ Threshold | $CO_2 \geq 20\%$ | % by volume | Insect mortality level |
| Bulk Density of Wheat | $\rho \approx 750$ | kg/m³ | Typical |
| Bulk Density of Paddy | $\rho \approx 580$ | kg/m³ | Typical |
| Bulk Density of Maize | $\rho \approx 720$ | kg/m³ | Typical |
| Stacking Height Limit (Bag) | $H_{max} \leq 4.5$ | m | Standard warehouse norm |
| Stack Base Area per Bag | $A_{bag} = 0.4 \times 0.6$ | m² | Standard gunny bag flat dimensions |
| Heat Transfer (Conduction) | $Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | W | Through mud wall |
| Angle of Repose (Wheat) | $\theta \approx 27°$ | degrees | For conical heap formation |
| Space Utilization Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{V_{grain}}{V_{stack}} \times 100$ | % | Aim $\geq 65\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** The **angle of repose** determines how grain naturally piles. If grain is heaped at angles exceeding the angle of repose, lateral flow and avalanching occur — this is why bulk bins are designed with conical bottoms matching the angle of repose for **gravity discharge**.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Mud bins** are central to **rural food security** and **zero-energy storage** — no electricity or chemicals required.
- **Drums** enable **hermetic storage technology**, recognized by **FAO** as a low-cost alternative to chemical fumigation.
- **Gunny bags** integrate with the **Public Distribution System (PDS)** of India, where the **Food Corporation of India (FCI)** moves millions of tonnes of grain annually in bagged form.
- **Stacking methods** directly influence **structural load, ventilation, pest control, and fire safety** in warehouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacity Calculation of a Cylindrical Mud Bin

**Problem Statement:** A cylindrical mud bin has an internal diameter of **2.0 m** and a useful height of **1.5 m**. It is to be filled with wheat. Calculate the **volume capacity**, the **mass of wheat stored** (assuming bulk density = 750 kg/m³), and the **surface area of the inner wall** (to estimate plastering material required).

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 2.0$ m, hence radius $r = 1.0$ m
- Height $h = 1.5$ m
- Bulk density of wheat $\rho = 750$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Calculate the internal volume of the bin.**

The bin is a cylinder, so:

$$V = \pi \times r^2 \times h$$

Substituting values:

$$V = \pi \times (1.0)^2 \times 1.5$$

$$V = 3.1416 \times 1.0 \times 1.5$$

$$V = 4.7124 \; m^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate the mass of wheat that can be stored.**

$$M = V \times \rho$$

$$M = 4.7124 \times 750$$

$$M = 3534.3 \; kg \approx 3.53 \; tonnes$$

**Step 3: Calculate the lateral (curved) surface area of the inner wall.**

The plastering material covers the cylindrical wall:

$$A_{wall} = 2 \pi r h$$

$$A_{wall} = 2 \times \pi \times 1.0 \times 1.5$$

$$A_{wall} = 9.425 \; m^2$$

**Step 4: Account for the base (floor) area to find total inner surface for plastering.**

$$A_{base} = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (1.0)^2 = 3.1416 \; m^2$$

$$A_{total} = A_{wall} + A_{base} = 9.425 + 3.142 = 12.567 \; m^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 2 m × 1.5 m cylindrical mud bin can hold approximately **3.5 tonnes of wheat**, which is sufficient to feed a family of 5 for nearly **a full year** (assuming 200 kg/person/year). This demonstrates why mud bins remain relevant in rural India.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stacking Pattern Analysis: Comparison of Methods

**Problem Statement:** A godown has a floor area of **10 m × 8 m = 80 m²**. Standard gunny bags (each of size 0.6 m × 0.4 m when laid flat, height 0.25 m when filled) are stacked. Calculate the storage capacity under three stacking methods:

- **Method A:** Flat-on-flat, bags laid horizontally (0.6 m × 0.4 m footprint)
- **Method B:** End-on-end, bags stood on their narrow end (0.4 m × 0.25 m footprint)
- **Method C:** Pyramid/Tiered stacking (top layers reduced by 1 bag per side per tier)

Assume maximum stacking height $H_{max} = 4.5$ m and a safety aisle area of **20%** of the floor.

**Step 1: Calculate effective floor area (after aisle deduction).**

$$A_{eff} = A_{floor} \times (1 - 0.20)$$

$$A_{eff} = 80 \times 0.80 = 64 \; m^2$$

**Step 2: Method A — Horizontal Stacking**

Footprint per bag = 0.6 × 0.4 = 0.24 m²

Number of bags per layer:

$$N_{layer} = \frac{A_{eff}}{A_{footprint}} = \frac{64}{0.24} = 266.67 \approx 266 \; bags$$

Number of layers (bag height 0.25 m, max stack height 4.5 m):

$$n_{layers} = \frac{H_{max}}{h_{bag}} = \frac{4.5}{0.25} = 18 \; layers$$

Total bags (Method A):

$$N_A = 266 \times 18 = 4788 \; bags$$

Assuming 50 kg/bag:

$$W_A = 4788 \times 50 = 239{,}400 \; kg = 239.4 \; tonnes$$

**Step 3: Method B — Vertical (End-on-End) Stacking**

Footprint per bag = 0.4 × 0.25 = 0.10 m²

$$N_{layer} = \frac{64}{0.10} = 640 \; bags$$

Bag effective height contribution per layer ≈ 0.40 m (width becomes height):

$$n_{layers} = \frac{4.5}{0.40} = 11.25 \approx 11 \; layers$$

Total bags (Method B):

$$N_B = 640 \times 11 = 7040 \; bags$$

$$W_B = 7040 \times 50 = 352{,}000 \; kg = 352.0 \; tonnes$$

**Step 4: Method C — Pyramid/Tiered Stacking (3 bags per side on ground tier, 2 on next, 1 on top)**

This method is used for short-term storage where structural stability matters. Each tier reduces the stack size.

For a 3-2-1 pyramid stack:
- Tier 1: 3 × 3 = 9 bags
- Tier 2: 2 × 2 = 4 bags
- Tier 3: 1 × 1 = 1 bag
- **Total per pyramid = 14 bags**

Height of pyramid (3 tiers × 0.25 m) = 0.75 m

Number of pyramid units fitting in 80 m² (each pyramid base = 3 × 0.6 m = 1.8 m wide):

$$N_{pyramids} = \frac{80}{1.8 \times 1.2} \approx 37 \; pyramids$$

$$N_C = 37 \times 14 = 518 \; bags$$

$$W_C = 518 \times 50 = 25{,}900 \; kg = 25.9 \; tonnes$$

**Step 5: Comparative Summary Table**

| Parameter | Method A (Horizontal) | Method B (Vertical) | Method C (Pyramid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Bags | 4788 | 7040 | 518 |
| Total Weight (tonnes) | 239.4 | 352.0 | 25.9 |
| Space Utilization $\eta$ (%) | 64.5 | 95.0 | 30.0 |
| Stability | High | Moderate | Very High |
| Inspection Access | Easy | Moderate | Easy |
| Ventilation | Excellent | Good | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Verdict:** Method B (vertical) gives the **highest space utilization** but requires careful **bag handling and pallet support**. Method A is the **FCI-preferred** method for operational safety. Method C is used for **initial receipt** and **fumigation stacks**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Transfer Through Mud Wall

**Problem Statement:** A mud bin wall has thickness $L = 0.15$ m, thermal conductivity $k = 0.7$ W/m·K. The outside temperature is $T_o = 40°C$ and the grain temperature inside is $T_i = 25°C$. Calculate the **heat flux** per m² of wall area.

**Step 1: Apply Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (steady-state, 1-D):**

$$q = \frac{k \cdot \Delta T}{L}$$

where $\Delta T = T_o - T_i$.

**Step 2: Substitute values:**

$$q = \frac{0.7 \times (40 - 25)}{0.15}$$

$$q = \frac{0.7 \times 15}{0.15}$$

$$q = \frac{10.5}{0.15}$$

$$q = 70 \; W/m^2$$

**Step 3: Compare with a metal drum (k = 50 W/m·K, L = 0.002 m):**

$$q_{metal} = \frac{50 \times 15}{0.002} = 375{,}000 \; W/m^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The mud wall has **~5350 times lower heat flux** than a metal drum of the same ΔT. This is precisely why mud bins keep grain cool and insect metabolism suppressed — a **passive thermal management** system built into the structure itself.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Assessment

| Parameter | Mud Bin | Metal Drum | Plastic Drum | Gunny Bag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Cost | Very Low | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| Thermal Insulation | Excellent | Poor | Moderate | Moderate |
| Hermetic Seal | Moderate (after fresh plaster) | Excellent (with gasket) | Excellent | Poor |
| Pest Resistance | High (gas-tight) | Very High | Very High | Low–Moderate |
| Moisture Barrier | Moderate | High | High | Low |
| Lifespan (years) | 3–5 | 10–20 | 5–10 | 1–3 (reusable) |
| Capacity Range | 0.5–10 t | 50–200 kg | 50–200 kg | 50–100 kg |
| Best Use | Rural farm storage | Seed storage, hermetic | Household, small trader | PDS, FCI warehouses |
| Construction Skill | Local artisan | Industrial fabrication | Industrial fabrication | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mud Bin Cross-Sectional View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Conical Thatched Roof] --> B[Mud Plastered Cylindrical Wall]
    B --> C[Raised Earthen Platform]
    C --> D[Compacted Stone Foundation]
    B --> E[Sun-Dried Grain Column]
    E --> F[Grain Bulk]
    F --> G[Bottom Drainage Gravel Layer]
    G --> D
    A --> H[Roof Overhang for Rain Protection]
```

**Description of Architecture:**
- The mud bin uses a **conical thatched roof** with a minimum overhang of **30 cm** to deflect rain.
- The **cylindrical wall** is built in successive 15–20 cm lifts of mud-straw mixture, sun-dried between layers.
- A **raised platform** (15–30 cm above ground) prevents capillary moisture rise.
- A **gravel drainage layer** at the base provides moisture buffering and prevents direct grain-soil contact.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hermetic Drum Storage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["INPUT"]
        A1[Dried Grain MC 12 percent] --> A2[Clean and Sort]
    end
    
    subgraph SEAL["SEALING"]
        A2 --> B1[Load into Drum]
        B1 --> B2[Place Rubber Gasket Lid]
        B2 --> B3[Clamp and Seal Hermetically]
    end
    
    subgraph STOR["STORAGE PHASE"]
        B3 --> C1[Grain Respiration consumes O2]
        C1 --> C2[CO2 Concentration rises]
        C2 --> C3{O2 below 5 percent}
        C3 -->|Yes| C4[Insect Mortality Achieved]
        C3 -->|No| C1
    end
    
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT"]
        C4 --> D1[Pest Free Grain Ready for Use]
    end
```

**Operating Logic:** The drum relies on **inter-grain respiration** (aerobic metabolism of the grain itself and any microorganisms present) to deplete O₂ and elevate CO₂. Once the modified atmosphere threshold is crossed, insects are asphyxiated — no chemical fumigant is needed.

---

### 4.3 Bag Stacking Methods — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph M1["METHOD A HORIZONTAL"]
        A1[Bag Flat 0.6 by 0.4 m] --> A2[Stack 18 Layers High]
        A2 --> A3[Capacity 239 tonnes per 80 sqm]
    end
    
    subgraph M2["METHOD B VERTICAL"]
        B1[Bag on End 0.4 by 0.25 m] --> B2[Stack 11 Layers High]
        B2 --> B3[Capacity 352 tonnes per 80 sqm]
    end
    
    subgraph M3["METHOD C PYRAMID"]
        C1[Tier 1 3 by 3 Bags] --> C2[Tier 2 2 by 2 Bags]
        C2 --> C3[Tier 3 1 by 1 Bag]
        C3 --> C4[Capacity 26 tonnes per 80 sqm]
    end
    
    subgraph DEC["DECISION LOGIC"]
        D1{Duration and Use?}
        D1 -->|Long term FCI| M1
        D1 -->|Maximum throughput| M2
        D1 -->|Fumigation inspection| M3
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Block Flow — Choice of Storage System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Food Grain to be Stored] --> Q1{Storage Duration}
    Q1 -->|Short 3 months| Q2{Quantity}
    Q1 -->|Long 6 to 12 months| Q3{Quantity}
    
    Q2 -->|Less than 1 tonne| R1[Mud Bin Recommended]
    Q2 -->|Greater than 1 tonne| R2[Gunny Bag Warehouse]
    
    Q3 -->|Less than 500 kg| R3[Hermetic Drum]
    Q3 -->|Greater than 500 kg| R4[Silo or Bag Warehouse]
    
    R1 --> END[Optimal Storage Solution]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
    R4 --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a "mud bin" and list any two advantages of mud bin storage for food grains.**

**Model Answer:**

A **mud bin** is a traditional cylindrical or rectangular bulk storage structure constructed from a mixture of **clay soil, straw (or cow dung), and water**, raised on a platform and plastered to store food grains in bulk.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Low capital cost** — constructed from locally available materials by village artisans, no industrial input required.
2. **Excellent thermal insulation** — low thermal conductivity of mud keeps the interior cool, suppressing insect activity and reducing moisture migration.
3. (Bonus point) **Eco-friendly and biodegradable** — no chemical fumigation needed when sealed properly.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the principle of hermetic storage in metal drums?**

**Model Answer:**

The principle of **hermetic storage** is the creation of an **airtight environment** in a sealed drum where the **inter-grain respiration** of the grain and microorganisms consumes oxygen (O₂) and releases carbon dioxide (CO₂). Once O₂ falls below **5%** and CO₂ rises above **20%**, storage insects and aerobic molds are killed by **asphyxiation**, eliminating the need for chemical fumigants like phosphine or methyl bromide.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation, Module 2 questions carry internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **construction procedure and design features of a mud bin** for storing food grains. What is the typical capacity range and what factors govern the safe storage of grains in mud bins?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical mud bin has an internal diameter of **2.4 m** and a height of **1.8 m**. Calculate:
- (i) The volume of the bin
- (ii) The mass of wheat it can hold (bulk density = 750 kg/m³)
- (iii) The lateral inner surface area requiring plastering

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The construction of a mud bin follows these steps:

1. **Site Selection:** An elevated, well-drained site away from flood-prone areas.
2. **Foundation:** Compacted stone/brick base, raised **15–30 cm** above ground level to prevent moisture rise.
3. **Wall Construction:** Built in **lifts of 15–20 cm** using a mud-straw-cow dung mixture (typical ratio 3:1:1 by volume). Each lift is sun-dried for 2–3 days.
4. **Wall Thickness:** **10–15 cm** at the base, tapering to **8–10 cm** at the top.
5. **Plastering:** Inner surface finished with a **smooth mud-cow dung slurry** to fill pores.
6. **Roof:** Conical/domed thatched roof with **30–45 cm overhang** to keep rain off the walls.
7. **Loading and Sealing:** Grains dried to **≤ 12% moisture** before filling. Top sealed with mud plaster.

**Typical Capacity:** **0.5 to 10 tonnes** depending on dimensions.

**Governing Factors for Safe Storage:**
- **Moisture content of grain** (≤ 12% w.b. for cereals)
- **Cleanliness** (free from broken grains, weed seeds, foreign matter)
- **Sun-drying** before storage
- **Sealing integrity** (gas-tightness)
- **Pest-free initial loading**
- **Site drainage** around the bin

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given:** $D = 2.4$ m, $h = 1.8$ m, $\rho = 750$ kg/m³

**(i) Volume of bin:**

$$r = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{2.4}{2} = 1.2 \; m$$

$$V = \pi r^2 h = \pi \times (1.2)^2 \times 1.8$$

$$V = 3.1416 \times 1.44 \times 1.8 = 8.143 \; m^3$$

**(ii) Mass of wheat:**

$$M = V \times \rho = 8.143 \times 750 = 6107 \; kg \approx 6.11 \; tonnes$$

**(iii) Lateral surface area:**

$$A = 2 \pi r h = 2 \times \pi \times 1.2 \times 1.8 = 13.57 \; m^2$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **types of gunny bags** used for food grain storage. Compare **jute gunny bags with HDPE/PP woven sacks** in terms of material properties, moisture permeability, reusability, and suitability for long-term storage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A warehouse of floor area **15 m × 10 m** is used to store wheat in standard 50 kg gunny bags (footprint 0.6 m × 0.4 m, bag height 0.25 m). Using the **horizontal stacking method** with a maximum stack height of **4.5 m** and **15% aisle area**, calculate:
- (i) Number of bags per layer
- (ii) Number of layers
- (iii) Total weight of wheat stored
- (iv) Space utilization efficiency if a bag is approximated as a cuboid

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Types of Gunny Bags:**
- **Traditional Jute (Burlap) Bags** — woven from jute fibre, biodegradable, breathable, tare weight ~1 kg.
- **HDPE Woven Sacks** — woven from high-density polyethylene tapes, moisture-resistant, non-biodegradable.
- **PP (Polypropylene) Woven Sacks** — similar to HDPE but with slightly different mechanical properties.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Jute Gunny Bag | HDPE/PP Woven Sack |
|---|---|---|
| Material | Natural jute fibre | Synthetic polymer tapes |
| Moisture Permeability | High (breathable) | Very Low |
| Tear Strength | Moderate | High |
| Reusability | 2–3 cycles | 3–5 cycles |
| Biodegradability | Yes (eco-friendly) | No (pollution concern) |
| UV Resistance | Moderate | Moderate to High (with UV stabilizer) |
| Insect Resistance | Low (porous) | High (tight weave) |
| Long-term Suitability | Moderate | Excellent |
| Cost per Bag | ₹ 25–40 | ₹ 20–35 |

**Conclusion:** For **long-term commercial storage**, HDPE/PP woven sacks are preferred due to better moisture barrier and pest exclusion. Jute bags are preferred for **breathable storage** of seeds and for **eco-friendly operations**.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:** Floor area = 15 × 10 = 150 m²; Aisle deduction = 15%; $H_{max} = 4.5$ m; bag footprint = 0.6 × 0.4 = 0.24 m²; bag height = 0.25 m; bag weight = 50 kg.

**(i) Number of bags per layer:**

$$A_{eff} = 150 \times (1 - 0.15) = 127.5 \; m^2$$

$$N_{layer} = \frac{127.5}{0.24} = 531.25 \approx 531 \; bags/layer$$

**(ii) Number of layers:**

$$n_{layers} = \frac{4.5}{0.25} = 18 \; layers$$

**(iii) Total weight of wheat stored:**

$$N_{total} = 531 \times 18 = 9558 \; bags$$

$$W_{total} = 9558 \times 50 = 477{,}900 \; kg \approx 477.9 \; tonnes$$

**(iv) Space utilization efficiency:**

Volume occupied by bags per layer = $531 \times (0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.25) = 531 \times 0.06 = 31.86 \; m^3$

Total volume occupied = $31.86 \times 18 = 573.48 \; m^3$

Effective stacking volume = $127.5 \times 4.5 = 573.75 \; m^3$

$$\eta = \frac{573.48}{573.75} \times 100 \approx 99.96\%$$

(Note: When measured against total warehouse volume including roof space, $\eta$ reduces to ~30–40% — the above calculation shows within-stack packing efficiency.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Penalty (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently mix up **wet basis (w.b.)** and **dry basis (d.b.)** moisture content. KTU expects the **wet basis** convention unless specified otherwise.
> 2. **Bulk Density Confusion (–1 mark):** Do not confuse bulk density ($\rho \approx 750$ kg/m³ for wheat) with **true density** (~1350 kg/m³). Bulk density accounts for the **voids between grains** and is the correct value for bin capacity calculations.
> 3. **Stack Height Violation (–1 to –2 marks):** Exceeding the 4.5 m limit without pallet racking support is a major safety violation; do not propose higher stacks in exam answers.
> 4. **Hermetic Threshold Mix-up (–1 mark):** The correct insect mortality thresholds are **O₂ < 5%** AND **CO₂ > 20%** — stating only one is incomplete and loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping Plastering Area Calculation:** Always include **lateral wall + base + roof** when asked for total plastering material — only quoting the curved wall area is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mud Bin** = Traditional bulk storage using **clay-straw-cow dung mixture**; capacity **0.5–10 tonnes**; relies on **thermal insulation and modified atmosphere** for pest control.
- **Metal Drum** = Airtight **galvanized/mild steel** container; ideal for **hermetic storage** of seeds and high-value grains; capacity **50–200 kg**.
- **Plastic Drum (HDPE/PP)** = Lighter, corrosion-resistant alternative to metal; used for **household and small-trader** storage.
- **Gunny Bag** = Woven sack of **jute fibre**; standard capacity **50 kg (modern) / 75 kg (traditional)**; breathable and biodegradable.
- **HDPE/PP Woven Sack** = Modern synthetic alternative; superior **moisture and pest resistance**; preferred for FCI/PDS operations.
- **Stacking Method A (Horizontal):** Bags laid flat, max 18 layers, **highest stability and inspection access** — FCI-preferred.
- **Stacking Method B (Vertical):** Bags on end, max ~11 layers, **highest space utilization (~95%)** but requires pallet support.
- **Stacking Method C (Pyramid):** 3-2-1 tiered, used for **fumigation and short-term inspection** — lowest capacity but maximum access.
- **Hermetic Threshold:** **O₂ < 5%** and **CO₂ > 20%** for insect mortality.
- **Safe Storage Moisture:** **≤ 12% (w.b.)** for cereals (wheat, rice, maize).
- **Thermal Conductivity of Mud:** **0.5–0.8 W/m·K** — gives ~5000× better insulation than metal — this is the key to passive cooling.
- **Angle of Repose (Wheat):** **~27°** — governs conical bottom design for gravity discharge in silos.
- **Standard Bag Footprint:** **0.6 m × 0.4 m** (horizontal) or **0.4 m × 0.25 m** (vertical).
- **Maximum Stack Height:** **4.5 m** (Indian warehouse norm) — exceeding this requires engineered racking.
- **Bulk Densities to Memorize:** Wheat = **750 kg/m³**, Paddy = **580 kg/m³**, Maize = **720 kg/m³**.
- **Engineering Takeaway:** Choice of storage (mud bin / drum / bag) is governed by the **4S Principle — Scale, Span, Safety, and Shelf-life** of the commodity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small-Scale Storage Structures: Brick, Concrete Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Small-Scale Storage Structures: Brick, Concrete Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Small-Scale Storage Structures — Formal Definition

**Small-scale storage structures** are purpose-built, on-farm or community-level containment units engineered to store agricultural produce (grains, pulses, oilseeds, spices, perishables) in capacities ranging typically from **1 tonne to 500 tonnes**. They are constructed using locally available, durable, and economically viable materials such as burnt clay bricks, reinforced cement concrete (RCC), plain cement concrete (PCC), ferrocement, stone masonry, and stabilized earth.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for *Food Storage Engineering*, small-scale storage structures are classified under **"improved / scientific storage"** as opposed to traditional mud bins, bamboo baskets (*kuppi*), and earthen pots, because they incorporate engineered design principles: load-bearing calculations, moisture barriers, rodent-proofing, and aeration control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:**
> *"Small-scale storage structures — Brick silos, Brick godowns, RCC bins, Ferrocement bins, Pre-cast concrete bins, Metal bins, Underground (pit) storage, Bamboo & wooden structures — Constructional details, design principles, suitability for different commodities, advantages and limitations."*

---

## 1.2 Brick Storage Structures

A **brick storage structure** is a small-capacity containment facility whose load-bearing walls, flooring, and roof are constructed using **burnt clay bricks** bonded with cement mortar (or lime mortar in older constructions). Brick structures are sub-classified into:

| Sub-Type | Typical Capacity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Brick Godown (Warehouse)** | 50 – 500 tonnes | Multi-commodity bag storage |
| **Brick Silo (Circular)** | 10 – 100 tonnes | Bulk grain (wheat, maize, paddy) |
| **Brick Bin (Square/Rectangular)** | 1 – 25 tonnes | Farm-level seed/feed storage |
| **Brick-Cum-Mud Bin (Improved Pusa Bin)** | 1.5 – 5 tonnes | Village-level seed storage |

**Key Material Constants (Burnt Clay Brick, IS 1077):**

- **Compressive strength:** **3.5 – 10.5 N/mm²** (common: 3.5 MPa Class)
- **Density:** **1700 – 1920 kg/m³**
- **Standard brick size:** **190 mm × 90 mm × 90 mm** (with 10 mm mortar)
- **Water absorption:** **< 15%** by weight
- **Thermal conductivity:** **0.6 – 0.8 W/m·K**

---

## 1.3 Concrete Storage Structures

A **concrete storage structure** uses **cement concrete** (a mixture of cement, fine aggregate, sand, coarse aggregate, and water) as the primary load-bearing and protective material. Concrete structures are categorized by their reinforcement level and construction methodology:

| Sub-Type | Reinforcement | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain Cement Concrete (PCC) Bins** | None | Small floor-grade bins, plinth beams |
| **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) Silos** | Steel rebar + mesh | Medium-capacity cylindrical silos |
| **Pre-cast Concrete Bins** | Pre-tensioned panels | Modular farm bins (easy assembly) |
| **Ferrocement Bins** | Wire mesh + cement mortar | Low-cost cylindrical/domed bins |
| **Hollow Concrete Block Bins** | Cavity-filled with RCC | Affordable on-farm godowns |

**Standard Concrete Mix Grades (IS 456:2000):**

- **M20 (1 : 1.5 : 3)** — general RCC work
- **M25 (1 : 1 : 2)** — silos, water-retaining structures
- **M30 and above** — large-diameter silos, dynamic load zones

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Anchor:** Concrete is approximately **10× stronger in compression** than in tension — that is why steel reinforcement is mandatory in walls that experience hoop (tensile) stresses from grain lateral pressure.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like this — the "Suitcase Engineering" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine you are packing clothes for a long trip. A **mud bin** is like stuffing everything loosely into a cardboard box — cheap, but the box collapses if stacked. A **brick bin** is like a hard-shell suitcase — solid, sturdy, weather-resistant, but heavy and slow to build. A **RCC silo** is like a reinforced hard-shell suitcase with steel ribs inside — it can handle enormous pressure from all sides without cracking. A **ferrocement bin** is like a lightweight fabric suitcase with thin plastic-coated layers — surprisingly strong for its weight, and cheap to make. Each has its place; the choice depends on what you store, for how long, and your budget.

### Why This Matters in Food Storage Engineering

- **Post-harvest losses** in India range from **5–16%** for cereals due to improper storage (ICAR data).
- Small-scale engineered structures can reduce losses to **< 2%**.
- They form the **first mile of the cold-chain / dry-chain infrastructure**, especially for farmers with 1–10 tonne surplus.

---

## 1.5 Categorical Tree of Small-Scale Storage Structures

```
SMALL-SCALE STORAGE STRUCTURES
├── MASONRY (BRICK / STONE)
│   ├── Brick Godown (Rectangular)
│   ├── Brick Silo (Circular)
│   ├── Improved Pusa Bin
│   └── Brick-Cum-Mud Bin
├── CONCRETE
│   ├── PCC Bins (Plain)
│   ├── RCC Silos (Reinforced)
│   ├── Pre-cast Concrete Bins
│   ├── Ferrocement Bins
│   └── Hollow Block Bins
├── METAL
│   ├── GI Sheet Bins
│   └── Aluminum Silos
├── NATURAL / TRADITIONAL (for context)
│   ├── Bamboo Bins
│   └── Underground Pits
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of Brick and Concrete Storage Structures

The design of any grain storage structure must simultaneously satisfy **four engineering objectives**:

1. **Structural integrity** — resist lateral grain pressure, roof load, wind, seismic forces.
2. **Hermetic / controlled microenvironment** — prevent moisture ingress, insect entry, rodent access.
3. **Operational efficiency** — allow easy loading, unloading, aeration, and inspection.
4. **Economic viability** — life-cycle cost within 3–5× payback of stored commodity value.

---

## 2.2 Loads Acting on a Storage Structure

A small-scale silo or godown experiences the following loads (per IS 875, IS 4995, and ASAE EP433 standards):

### (A) Dead Loads (DL)
Self-weight of walls, roof slab, columns, and permanent fixtures. For a brick wall of thickness *t* (m), height *h* (m), and unit weight *γ_brick* = **20 kN/m³**:

$$w_{wall} = t \times h \times \gamma_{brick}$$

### (B) Live Loads (LL)
Roof live load for inaccessible roofs = **1.5 kN/m²** (IS 875 Part 2).
Floor live load for grain storage = **10 – 25 kN/m³** depending on commodity.

### (C) Grain Lateral Pressure (Janssen's Equation)
This is the **single most important formula** in silo design. Traditional earth-pressure theories (Rankine) overestimate the pressure in deep bins. **Janssen (1895)** recognized that friction on the walls carries a portion of the vertical load, reducing bottom pressure asymptotically.

$$p_h = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu k} \left(1 - e^{-\mu k H / R}\right)$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $p_h$ | Horizontal (lateral) pressure at depth *H* (kN/m²) | — |
| $\gamma$ | Bulk density of stored grain (kN/m³) | 7.5 – 9.0 (wheat) |
| $R$ | Hydraulic radius = Area / Perimeter (m) | — |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction (grain ↔ wall) | 0.3 – 0.5 (concrete), 0.4 – 0.6 (brick) |
| $k$ | Lateral pressure ratio = tan²(45° − φ/2) | 0.4 – 0.6 |
| $\phi$ | Angle of internal friction (grain) | 25° – 35° |
| $H$ | Depth of grain (m) | — |

**Vertical wall friction:**

$$p_v = \gamma H - p_h$$

### (D) Thermal & Moisture Loads
Temperature variation (ΔT = 20–30 °C diurnal in tropical Kerala) induces thermal stresses of:

$$\sigma_{thermal} = E \alpha \Delta T$$

For concrete *E* = **25 GPa**, *α* = **10⁻⁵ /°C** ⇒ σ_thermal ≈ **5 – 7.5 MPa** (significant — expansion joints mandatory).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{cylinder} = \pi r^2 h$ | Silo capacity | m³ |
| 2 | $V_{cone/hopper} = \tfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$ | Bottom hopper volume | m³ |
| 3 | $V_{rect} = L \times B \times H$ | Godown capacity | m³ |
| 4 | $W = V \times \gamma_{bulk}$ | Stored grain weight (kN) | kN |
| 5 | $p_h = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu k}\left(1-e^{-\mu kH/R}\right)$ | Janssen lateral pressure | kN/m² |
| 6 | $p_v = \gamma H - p_h$ | Vertical wall friction stress | kN/m² |
| 7 | $T = p_h \cdot D / 2$ | Hoop tension in circular wall | kN/m |
| 8 | $M_{ring} = p_h \cdot D^2 / 16$ | Bending moment (simply supported) | kN·m |
| 9 | $A_{st} = T / \sigma_{st}$ | Steel area for hoop tension | mm² |
| 10 | $N_{bricks} = V_{wall} / V_{brick,mortar}$ | Brick quantity estimation | nos. |
| 11 | $V_{concrete} = L \times B \times t$ | Concrete volume | m³ |
| 12 | $\sigma_{c,allow} = 0.45 f_{ck}$ | Permissible concrete stress (IS 456) | MPa |
| 13 | $\sigma_{st,allow} = 0.87 f_y$ | Permissible steel stress (Fe415) | MPa |
| 14 | $C_c = 0.36 \cdot \dfrac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \cdot \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d} \cdot b \cdot d$ | Compressive force in RCC section | kN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In the formulas above, **never confuse** $\mu$ (wall friction coefficient) with Poisson's ratio (also sometimes denoted $\mu$). In Janssen's equation, $\mu$ is **strictly** the grain-to-wall friction coefficient.

---

## 2.4 Detailed Constructional Features

### 2.4.1 Brick Godown (Rectangular Warehouse)

**Sub-components:**

1. **Foundation:** Isolated RCC footings (depth ≥ 1.0 m below GL in Kerala laterite soil) or strip footing for load-bearing walls.
2. **Plinth beam:** RCC band at plinth level (150 mm × 200 mm) — provides DPC and lateral rigidity.
3. **Damp-Proof Course (DPC):** 40 mm thick 1:2:4 PCC with waterproofing compound at plinth level.
4. **Walls:** 230 mm (one-brick) or 345 mm (1.5-brick) thick, English bond brickwork in 1:6 cement mortar.
5. **Roof:** RCC slab (120 mm thick, M20 grade) with weathering course and thermal insulation.
6. **Floor:** 150 mm M15 PCC finished with epoxy or polyurethane coating; slope 1:80 toward drain.
7. **Ventilators:** Fixed louvered ventilators @ 1 per 12 m² of floor area, with **18-mesh SS wire netting** to exclude insects.
8. **Doors:** Steel rolling shutters (3 m × 3 m typical).
9. **Rat-proof band (RPB):** GI sheet 200 mm wide embedded 150 mm into wall @ 1.0 m above GL — prevents rodent climbing.
10. **Treatment:** Walls lime-washed internally, external acrylic emulsion.

### 2.4.2 Brick Silo (Circular)

- **Diameter:** 3–6 m typical for small-scale.
- **Wall thickness:** 230 mm (one-brick) up to 4 m height; 345 mm above.
- **Hopper bottom:** Steel cone (45° slope) or masonry pyramid.
- **Reinforcement:** None in pure brick; **hoop bands** of MS flat 50 × 6 mm @ 300 mm c/c are recommended for diameters > 4 m to resist hoop tension.
- **Roof:** Dome-shaped brick tile roof (safer for thrust) or flat RCC slab.

### 2.4.3 RCC Silo

- Wall thickness: **150 mm** for D ≤ 4 m, **200 mm** for D = 4–6 m.
- Two layers of **8 mm Ø @ 150 mm c/c** as hoop + vertical reinforcement.
- Concrete grade: **M25 minimum**, water-cement ratio **≤ 0.45**.
- Curing: **14 days minimum** (silos require extended curing to prevent shrinkage cracks).
- Joints: Construction joint with water-stop at every 6 m vertical height.

### 2.4.4 Ferrocement Bin

- Wall thickness: **25–40 mm** (key advantage).
- Layers: **4–6 layers** of welded wire mesh (chicken mesh + square mesh) embedded in **1:2 cement mortar**.
- Skeleton: Mild steel rods 8–10 mm Ø as vertical and horizontal skeletal frame.
- Suitable for capacities up to **10 tonnes**, diameter ≤ 3 m.

### 2.4.5 Pre-cast Concrete Bin

- Panels cast at factory, transported, bolted on-site.
- Joints sealed with bitumen or silicone.
- Advantage: faster construction, quality control at factory.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application |
|---|---|
| **FPOs / FPCs (Farmer Producer Organizations)** | 100-tonne RCC silos for collective grain banking |
| **PACS (Primary Agricultural Cooperative Societies)** | Brick godowns under NABARD's Warehouse Infrastructure Fund |
| **Seed Production Hubs (Kerala State Seed Farm)** | Ferrocement bins for breeder/foundation seed |
| **Custom Hiring Centers (CHC)** | Mobile pre-cast bins for rent |
| **Disaster-prone regions (Kerala floods)** | Elevated RCC silos with 1 m stilts to prevent water damage |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Janssen's Lateral Pressure Equation (Detailed)

This is a **KTU-favorite derivation** — frequently asked for 7 marks in Part B.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Setup

Consider a thin horizontal slice of grain of thickness *dz* at depth *z* below the top surface, in a vertical cylindrical silo of radius *R* (hydraulic radius for a circular section = D/4).

Forces acting on the slice:

1. **Vertical pressure** *p_v* acting downward on top of slice.
2. **Vertical pressure** *(p_v + dp_v)* acting upward on bottom of slice.
3. **Weight of slice** = *γ · A · dz* (downward).
4. **Wall friction** = *μ · p_h · perimeter · dz* (upward, resisting downward motion).

Where *A* = cross-sectional area, *perimeter* = circumference for circular silo, *R = A / perimeter*.

### Step 2 — Equilibrium of the Slice (ΣF_v = 0)

$$p_v A = (p_v + dp_v) A + \gamma A dz - \mu p_h \cdot (\text{perimeter}) dz$$

Simplify:

$$0 = A \cdot dp_v + \gamma A dz - \mu p_h \cdot (\text{perimeter}) dz$$

Divide through by *A*:

$$\frac{dp_v}{dz} = \mu \cdot \frac{\text{perimeter}}{A} \cdot p_h - \gamma = \frac{\mu p_h}{R} - \gamma$$

### Step 3 — Lateral-Vertical Pressure Relationship

For granular material at failure, by Rankine's active state:

$$p_h = k \cdot p_v \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_v = \frac{p_h}{k}$$

Where $k = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$ (lateral pressure ratio, NOT the Rankine active coefficient for soil which uses the same form but with $\phi$ = soil friction angle — same expression, different physical meaning here).

Substitute:

$$\frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{dp_h}{dz} = \frac{\mu p_h}{R} - \gamma$$

$$\frac{dp_h}{dz} = \frac{\mu k}{R} p_h - \gamma k$$

### Step 4 — Solve the First-Order Linear ODE

This is of form $\dfrac{dp_h}{dz} - a p_h = -b$, with $a = \mu k / R$ and $b = \gamma k$.

Standard solution with boundary condition $p_h(0) = 0$ (free surface):

$$p_h = \frac{b}{a} \left(1 - e^{-a z}\right) = \frac{\gamma k \cdot R}{\mu k}\left(1 - e^{-\mu k z / R}\right)$$

$$\boxed{p_h = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\mu k H / R}\right)}$$

### Step 5 — Physical Interpretation

- As $H \to \infty$: $p_h \to \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu}$ (asymptotic saturation).
- This explains why **deep silos do not have proportionally larger bottom pressure** — friction on walls "absorbs" the load.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Capacity of a Small Brick Godown

**Problem (KTU style):** A brick godown has internal dimensions **8 m × 5 m × 3.5 m** (L × B × H). Wheat is stored in bags stacked up to **3.0 m height**. Each bag weighs **50 kg** with dimensions **0.6 m × 0.4 m × 0.25 m**. Determine:

(a) Number of bags stored.
(b) Total weight of wheat stored.
(c) Floor area utilization.

### Solution

**(a) Number of bags:**

Volume occupied by each bag:

$$V_{bag} = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.25 = 0.06 \text{ m}^3$$

Useful storage volume = $8 \times 5 \times 3.0 = 120 \text{ m}^3$

Number of bags:

$$N = \frac{120}{0.06} = 2000 \text{ bags}$$

**(b) Total weight:**

$$W = 2000 \times 50 = 100{,}000 \text{ kg} = 100 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(c) Floor area utilization:**

Floor area = $8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ m}^2$

Stacking density:

$$\rho_{stack} = \frac{2000}{40} = 50 \text{ bags/m}^2$$

This is acceptable (typical: 35–55 bags/m²).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — RCC Silo Hoop Tension

**Problem:** Design the hoop reinforcement for a cylindrical RCC silo:
- Diameter *D* = 4 m
- Stored grain: wheat, $\gamma = 8 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Wall friction $\mu = 0.4$
- Lateral pressure ratio $k = 0.5$
- Height of grain $H = 6 \text{ m}$

Use **Fe415 steel**, **M25 concrete**, wall thickness = 150 mm.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Hydraulic radius:**

$$R = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Janssen lateral pressure at H = 6 m:**

Exponent:

$$\frac{\mu k H}{R} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.5 \times 6}{1.0} = 1.2$$

$$e^{-1.2} = 0.3012$$

$$p_h = \frac{8 \times 1.0}{0.4} (1 - 0.3012) = 20 \times 0.6988 = 13.98 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Hoop tension per unit height:**

$$T = \frac{p_h \cdot D}{2} = \frac{13.98 \times 4}{2} = 27.96 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Steel area required per metre height:**

Permissible stress in Fe415 = $0.87 f_y = 0.87 \times 415 = 361 \text{ MPa}$

$$A_{st} = \frac{T \times 10^3}{361} = \frac{27960}{361} = 77.5 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Choose bar diameter and spacing:**

Try **8 mm Ø bars**: area per bar = $\pi/4 \times 8^2 = 50.27 \text{ mm}^2$

Spacing:

$$s = \frac{1000 \times 50.27}{77.5} \times \frac{1}{1000} \text{ m} \Rightarrow \frac{50270}{77.5} = 648 \text{ mm}$$

Provide **8 mm Ø @ 300 mm c/c** (conservative, ensures crack control).

**Step 6 — Check minimum steel (IS 456):**

For RCC walls, $A_{st,min} = 0.12\%$ of gross cross-section = $0.0012 \times 1000 \times 150 = 180 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$

Steel provided = $\dfrac{1000}{300} \times 50.27 = 167.6 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$ — slightly less, so provide **8 mm Ø @ 250 mm c/c**.

**Final: Provide 8 mm Ø HYSD bars @ 250 mm c/c as hoop reinforcement.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Brick Quantity Estimation

**Problem:** Estimate the number of bricks required for a brick godown wall:
- Wall length: 30 m (perimeter, two sides + two gable ends)
- Wall height: 3.5 m
- Wall thickness: 230 mm (one-brick)

Assume 10 mm mortar joints; nominal brick size 200 × 100 × 100 mm.

### Solution

**Volume of wall:**

$$V_{wall} = 30 \times 3.5 \times 0.23 = 24.15 \text{ m}^3$$

**Volume of one brick with mortar:**

$$V_{brick+mortar} = 0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.10 = 0.002 \text{ m}^3$$

**Number of bricks (theoretical):**

$$N = \frac{24.15}{0.002} = 12{,}075 \text{ bricks}$$

**Add 5% wastage:**

$$N_{actual} = 12{,}075 \times 1.05 = 12{,}679 \approx 12{,}700 \text{ bricks}$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Brick vs. Concrete)

| Parameter | Brick Structure | RCC Structure | Ferrocement Structure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material cost (per m³ wall)** | ₹4,000 – 5,500 | ₹7,000 – 9,000 | ₹2,500 – 3,500 |
| **Construction speed** | Slow (skilled mason) | Medium (formwork) | Fast (semi-skilled) |
| **Skill required** | High (masonry) | Medium | Low – Medium |
| **Lifespan** | 30–50 years | 50–80 years | 15–25 years |
| **Moisture resistance** | Moderate (needs DPC) | High | Moderate |
| **Rodent resistance** | High (with RPB) | Very high | Moderate (thin wall) |
| **Crack resistance** | Low (rigid joints) | Good (reinforced) | Excellent (mesh arrests cracks) |
| **Best for capacity** | 50–500 t godowns | 50–5000 t silos | 1–10 t farm bins |
| **Seismic performance** | Moderate (with bands) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Suitability for Kerala (humid)** | Good if ventilated | Excellent | Good (must coat outside) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEFTT633]
**Define a brick godown. List any four components of a brick godown used for grain storage.**

**Model Answer:**

A **brick godown** is a rectangular storage warehouse constructed with burnt clay brick walls, RCC roof, and PCC flooring, used for storing bagged or bulk grains at capacities ranging from 50 to 500 tonnes.

**Four essential components:**

1. **Foundation** — RCC isolated footings or strip footing.
2. **Plinth Beam and DPC** — 150 × 200 mm RCC beam with 40 mm thick Damp-Proof Course.
3. **Load-bearing walls** — 230 mm one-brick in cement mortar 1:6.
4. **RCC roof slab** — 120 mm thick M20 grade with weathering course.
5. (Bonus) **Ventilators** with 18-mesh SS wire net.
6. (Bonus) **Rat-proof band** — GI sheet 200 mm wide.

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEFTT633]
**What is a ferrocement bin? Mention its advantages over RCC bins.**

**Model Answer:**

A **ferrocement bin** is a small-capacity (1–10 tonne) cylindrical or domed storage bin constructed by applying **1:2 cement mortar over a skeletal framework of mild steel rods and 4–6 layers of welded wire mesh** (chicken mesh + square mesh), resulting in a wall thickness of only **25–40 mm**.

**Advantages over RCC:**

1. **Lower cost** — ~50% cheaper per m³ of wall.
2. **No formwork required** — saves labor and time.
3. **Better crack-resistance** — closely spaced mesh arrests micro-cracks.
4. **Lightweight** — easier to transport and assemble.
5. **Constructible by semi-skilled labor** — village-level feasibility.
6. **Lower cement consumption** (~30% less).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **constructional details of a brick godown** for grain storage. Discuss the role of **DPC and rat-proof band**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical **RCC silo** has internal diameter **5 m** and stores wheat up to a height of **8 m**. Calculate the **hoop tension** at mid-height and design the **hoop reinforcement** using **Fe415** steel and **M25** concrete. Wall thickness = 175 mm. Take $\gamma = 8 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.4$, $k = 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Brick Godown — Constructional Details:**

1. **Site selection:** Elevated ground, away from flood-prone areas, accessible by road.
2. **Excavation & Foundation:** Trenches dug to 1.0–1.2 m depth; PCC 1:4:8 bed; RCC isolated footings for columns (size: 1.2 m × 1.2 m × 0.3 m) and strip footing for walls (width 0.6 m).
3. **Plinth Beam:** RCC 200 × 250 mm with 4 nos. 12 mm Ø longitudinal bars and 8 mm Ø stirrups @ 150 mm c/c. Plinth height ≥ 450 mm above GL.
4. **DPC (Damp-Proof Course):** 40 mm thick 1:2:4 PCC with **2% waterproofing compound** (e.g., Conplast or Pidiproof) laid over the entire plinth beam. Prevents capillary rise of moisture into walls.

**Role of DPC:**
- Prevents **rising dampness** which would otherwise increase grain moisture content and cause fungal/mycotoxin growth.
- Acts as a horizontal barrier, blocking capillary suction of groundwater.

5. **Walls:** One-brick (230 mm) in **English bond**, cement mortar 1:6. Lime-washed internally.
6. **Rat-Proof Band (RPB):** GI sheet 200 mm wide, **embedded 150 mm into the wall** and projecting **50 mm outward**, fixed at **1.0 m above GL** all around the perimeter.

**Role of Rat-Proof Band:**
- Prevents rats from climbing up the rough brick surface to enter the godown.
- Smooth GI surface is impossible for rodents to grip.
- Mandated by **Food Corporation of India (FCI) godown norms**.

7. **Roof:** 120 mm RCC slab M20 with 100 mm brick tile coba waterproofing on top.
8. **Floor:** 150 mm M15 PCC, finished with 2 mm epoxy coating; slope 1:80 toward drainage.
9. **Ventilators:** 0.45 m × 0.30 m fixed louvered, with 18-mesh SS wire netting; placed @ 1 per 12 m² floor area.
10. **Doors:** Steel rolling shutters 3 m × 3 m on at least two opposite walls for cross-ventilation.

**[Sketch — refer to SECTION 4.5 mermaid diagram above]**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** D = 5 m, H = 8 m (mid-height = 4 m), $\gamma = 8 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.4$, $k = 0.5$, Fe415, M25.

**Step 1 — Hydraulic radius:**

$$R = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{5}{4} = 1.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Lateral pressure at mid-height (H = 4 m):**

Exponent:

$$\frac{\mu k H}{R} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.5 \times 4}{1.25} = 0.64$$

$$e^{-0.64} = 0.5273$$

$$p_h = \frac{8 \times 1.25}{0.4}(1 - 0.5273) = 25 \times 0.4727 = 11.82 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Hoop tension per unit height:**

$$T = \frac{p_h \cdot D}{2} = \frac{11.82 \times 5}{2} = 29.55 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Required steel area:**

$\sigma_{st} = 0.87 \times 415 = 361 \text{ MPa}$

$$A_{st} = \frac{29.55 \times 10^3}{361} = 81.86 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum steel (IS 456 for RCC wall = 0.12%):**

$$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 175 = 210 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$$

Steel provided governs = 210 mm²/m.

**Step 6 — Choose 8 mm Ø bars (area = 50.27 mm²):**

$$s = \frac{1000 \times 50.27}{210} = 239 \text{ mm}$$

**Provide 8 mm Ø HYSD bars @ 230 mm c/c as hoop reinforcement on both faces (inner and outer), with vertical distribution steel of 8 mm Ø @ 300 mm c/c.**

---

### **Question B (Alternative to Question A)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** Compare **brick silos, RCC silos, and ferrocement bins** in terms of construction, cost, capacity, and suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brick godown of internal dimensions **10 m × 6 m × 4 m** is to be constructed. Estimate the **quantity of bricks and cement** required for 230 mm thick walls of height 3.5 m. Assume brick size 200 × 100 × 100 mm with 10 mm mortar. Cement mortar ratio 1:6. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Brick Silo | RCC Silo | Ferrocement Bin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 10–100 t | 50–5000 t | 1–10 t |
| **Diameter range** | 3–6 m | 3–15 m | 1–3 m |
| **Wall thickness** | 230–345 mm | 150–250 mm | 25–40 mm |
| **Reinforcement** | Optional MS hoop bands | Mandatory double-mesh | Mesh + skeletal rods |
| **Skilled labor** | Mason (high skill) | Concrete + steel (medium) | Semi-skilled (low) |
| **Formwork** | Not required (masonry) | Required (costly) | Not required |
| **Construction time** | 30–45 days | 45–90 days | 7–15 days |
| **Cost per tonne stored** | ₹2,500–4,000 | ₹3,500–6,000 | ₹1,500–2,500 |
| **Lifespan** | 30–50 years | 50–80 years | 15–25 years |
| **Best use** | Cooperative godowns | Large FPOs / FCI | Small/marginal farmers |
| **Suitability in Kerala** | Good (humidity OK if ventilated) | Excellent | Good (with exterior coat) |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Perimeter of walls (internal dimensions + 2 × wall thickness on each side):**

External L = 10 + 2 × 0.23 = 10.46 m
External B = 6 + 2 × 0.23 = 6.46 m
External perimeter = 2 × (10.46 + 6.46) = 33.84 m

**Step 2 — Wall volume:**

$V_{wall} = 33.84 \times 3.5 \times 0.23 = 27.24 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 3 — Number of bricks:**

Volume of one brick with mortar = $0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.10 = 0.002 \text{ m}^3$

$N = \dfrac{27.24}{0.002} = 13{,}620$ bricks

Add 5% wastage: **~14,300 bricks**

**Step 4 — Mortar volume:**

Wet mortar volume = 27.24 − (13,620 × 0.002) ≈ approximately **25%** of wall volume.

$$V_{mortar,wet} = 0.25 \times 27.24 = 6.81 \text{ m}^3$$

Dry volume = $1.33 \times$ wet volume (bulk factor):

$$V_{mortar,dry} = 1.33 \times 6.81 = 9.06 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Cement for 1:6 mortar:**

Total parts = 1 + 6 = 7
Cement volume (dry) = $\dfrac{9.06}{7} = 1.29 \text{ m}^3$

Density of cement = 1440 kg/m³
Mass of cement = $1.29 \times 1440 = 1858 \text{ kg}$

Number of 50-kg bags = $\dfrac{1858}{50} = 37.2 \approx$ **38 bags**

**Final answer:** ~14,300 bricks + 38 bags of cement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up hydraulic radius formula** for non-circular sections. For rectangular: $R = \dfrac{LB}{2(L+B)}$, for circular: $R = D/4$.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract volume of bricks from total wall volume** when calculating mortar (loses 2–3 marks).
> 3. **Not adding wastage percentage** (typically 5% for bricks, 2–3% for cement).
> 4. **Confusing hoop tension formula** $T = p_h D/2$ with bending moment — they look similar but are physically different.
> 5. **Forgetting DPC** in godown diagrams — a frequent omission that costs a mark.
> 6. **Writing mm instead of m, or kN instead of N** in units — strict ½ mark deduction per wrong unit in KTU valuation.
> 7. **Not providing spacing of bars** (e.g., "8 mm Ø @ 250 mm c/c") — design is incomplete without spacing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 2, Brick & Concrete Storage:**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Brick godown** — masonry warehouse for 50–500 t bag storage.
- [ ] **Brick silo** — circular masonry structure, typically 10–100 t capacity.
- [ ] **RCC silo** — reinforced concrete, mandatory for D > 4 m.
- [ ] **Ferrocement bin** — mortar + 4–6 layers of mesh, 25–40 mm wall, 1–10 t.
- [ ] **Improved Pusa Bin** — brick-cum-mud, 1.5–5 t capacity, ICAR design.

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] Janssen: $p_h = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\mu kH/R}\right)$
- [ ] Hoop tension: $T = p_h D / 2$
- [ ] Bending moment: $M = p_h D^2 / 16$
- [ ] Capacity: $W = V \times \gamma_{bulk}$
- [ ] Standard brick + mortar volume: 0.002 m³
- [ ] Concrete grade M20 → 1:1.5:3 mix ratio

### Constructional Must-Knows
- [ ] **DPC** thickness: **40 mm**, at plinth level.
- [ ] **Rat-proof band**: GI sheet **200 mm** wide, embedded **150 mm** into wall.
- [ ] **Ventilators**: 1 per **12 m²** floor area, with **18-mesh SS netting**.
- [ ] **Floor slope**: 1:80 toward drain.
- [ ] **Concrete curing**: minimum **14 days** for silos.
- [ ] **Min. hoop steel in RCC wall**: **0.12%** of gross cross-section.

### Design Constants
- [ ] Wheat bulk density $\gamma = 7.5 - 8.0 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- [ ] Paddy bulk density $\gamma = 5.5 - 6.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- [ ] Maize bulk density $\gamma = 7.0 - 7.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- [ ] Angle of internal friction (grain): 25°–35°
- [ ] Wall friction (concrete): $\mu = 0.3 - 0.4$
- [ ] Wall friction (brick): $\mu = 0.4 - 0.6$

### Codes & Standards
- [ ] IS 1077 — Burnt clay bricks specification
- [ ] IS 456:2000 — Plain and reinforced concrete code
- [ ] IS 875 — Loads on structures (Parts 1, 2, 3)
- [ ] IS 4995 — Criteria for design of RCC silos
- [ ] ASAE EP433 — Loadings on farm silos (international reference)

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Using Rankine earth-pressure theory for grain (use **Janssen**).
- ❌ Ignoring **moisture migration** in deep bins (Fick's law applies).
- ❌ Specifying M15 concrete for silo walls (use **M25 minimum**).
- ❌ Omitting construction joints (cause **shrinkage cracks**).
- ❌ Placing bins on **low-lying land** in Kerala (monsoon flooding risk).

### Last-Minute Numerical Problem Templates
- **Capacity** = Volume × bulk density
- **Hoop steel** = (Hoop tension / 0.87 × 415) × 10³ mm²/m
- **Brick quantity** = (Wall volume / 0.002) × 1.05 (with 5% wastage)
- **Cement bags** = [(Wall volume × 0.25 × 1.33) / 7] × 1440 / 50

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a **neat, labelled cross-section** of the structure first before writing the description — in KTU valuation, a clean diagram can earn you **2–3 marks even if the description is incomplete**. Use a ruler, label every component, and include dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
