topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Local Storage Structures: Morai, Bukhari, Kothar, Kuthla

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Local Storage Structures

Local storage structures are **traditional, indigenous, low-cost grain storage systems** developed by Indian farmers over centuries using locally available materials such as mud, straw, bamboo, cow dung, and stone. These structures are designed to store food grains safely for **3 to 12 months** while protecting them from **insects, rodents, fungi, and moisture migration**. They are particularly significant in the context of **rural food security, post-harvest loss reduction, and on-farm grain management** in developing agricultural economies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic falls under Module 2 of PEFTT633, focusing on *engineering aspects of grain storage with emphasis on traditional structures, their design logic, capacity estimation, and material science.*

---

## 1.2 Definition and Conceptual Overview of Each Structure

### 🔹 A. Morai (मोराई)

**Formal Definition:** A **Morai** is a traditional **underground (subterranean) cylindrical storage pit** used extensively in the arid and semi-arid regions of North-Western India (Rajasthan, Haryana, parts of UP and Punjab) for storing grains like wheat, sorghum (*jowar*), barley, and millets over long durations (often 1–3 years).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a Morai as a **"natural refrigerator buried underground."** Just as we use earthen pots to keep water cool in summer due to evaporative cooling and stable subterranean temperatures, a Morai uses the **insulating thermal mass of the earth** to maintain a cool, stable micro-climate (typically **20–25 °C** even when surface temperatures soar above 45 °C). It is essentially a **time-capsule for grain**, where the underground environment naturally suppresses insect and fungal activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** Grain is stored at **low moisture content (≤ 10–12 % w.b.)**, and the underground environment provides a hermetic-like atmosphere when sealed properly.

---

### 🔹 B. Bukhari (बुखारी)

**Formal Definition:** A **Bukhari** is a traditional **above-ground cylindrical storage bin** constructed primarily of **mud, brick, or stone masonry**, plastered with a mixture of mud and cow dung, and topped with a conical or domed roof. It is widely used in **Rajasthan, Gujarat, and parts of Maharashtra** for storing food grains and seeds.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a Bukhari as a **"miniature mud silo"** — much like modern grain silos in concept but built using vernacular engineering. The thick mud walls act as **natural thermal insulation and moisture barriers**, while the conical roof allows **rainwater runoff** and prevents seepage. It is the **above-ground cousin of the Morai**, suited for regions where the water table is too high or the soil is too rocky to dig pits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** Bukhari structures are often raised on a **stone or brick platform** to prevent ground moisture ingress and rodent entry.

---

### 🔹 C. Kothar (कोठार)

**Formal Definition:** A **Kothar** is a traditional **above-ground rectangular or square storage room/structure** built using **mud bricks, stone masonry, or wooden planks**, commonly used in **Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, Chhattisgarh, and parts of UP** for bulk storage of grains and oilseeds.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A Kothar is best understood as the **"traditional rural grain warehouse"** — essentially a small storage room within or adjacent to the farmer's house. It is the Indian equivalent of a **mini-grain godown**, often built with **double-layered walls** filled with a thermal insulating material (straw, husk, or charcoal) to maintain stable internal conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** Kothar structures may have a **raised floor (plinth) of 30–60 cm** to prevent moisture and rodent access.

---

### 🔹 D. Kuthla (कुठला / कुथला)

**Formal Definition:** A **Kuthla** is a **small-capacity, household-level storage bin** woven from **bamboo strips (or reeds) and plastered with a mud-cow dung mixture**, primarily used in the **hilly regions of Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Jammu & Kashmir, and the North-Eastern states** for storing seeds, pulses, and small quantities of cereals.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a Kuthla as a **"woven grain basket with a mud coating"** — similar in spirit to a modern **plastic grain bin** but built entirely from biodegradable, locally-sourced materials. The woven bamboo provides **structural strength and breathability**, while the mud-cow dung plaster seals the surface against insects and moisture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** Kuthla is **lightweight, portable, and inexpensive**, making it ideal for **terraced farming communities** where bulk transport of grain is impractical.

---

## 1.3 Why These Structures Matter in Food Storage Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> From a **Food Storage Engineering** perspective, these structures are studied because they represent:
> - **Sustainable engineering** — minimal carbon footprint, zero industrial inputs.
> - **Hermetic and modified-atmosphere principles** — especially for underground types.
> - **Cultural-material-engineering** — blending local materials with proven design logic.
> - **Low-cost post-harvest loss reduction** — critical for SDG 2 (Zero Hunger).
> - **Baseline for comparison** with modern silos and metal bins.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Engineering Classification of Local Storage Structures

These four structures can be classified along three engineering axes:

| Classification Axis | Morai | Bukhari | Kothar | Kuthla |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Underground (Pit) | Above-ground (Masonry) | Above-ground (Room) | Above-ground (Bin) |
| **Material** | Soil + Straw + Cow dung | Mud/Brick/Stone + Plaster | Mud-brick/Stone + Plaster | Bamboo + Mud plaster |
| **Capacity** | **500 kg – 20 tonnes** | **1 – 10 tonnes** | **5 – 50 tonnes** | **50 – 500 kg** |
| **Geometry** | Cylindrical pit | Cylindrical bin | Rectangular room | Cylindrical/oval basket |
| **Duration** | Long-term (1–3 yr) | Medium-term (6–12 mo) | Long-term (1 yr+) | Short-medium (3–6 mo) |
| **Region** | NW India (Rajasthan) | Rajasthan, Gujarat | MP, Bihar, CG | HP, UK, NE India |

---

## 2.2 Structural and Design Principles

### 🔸 2.2.1 Morai — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Step 1 — Site Selection:** A **dry, elevated, well-drained site** with low water table (> 3 m below ground) and sandy/loamy soil is chosen. Sites prone to waterlogging are strictly avoided.

**Step 2 — Pit Excavation:** A **cylindrical pit** of diameter **1.5–2.5 m** and depth **1.5–2.5 m** is dug. The walls are made smooth and compact.

**Step 3 — Lining and Insulation:** The pit is lined from inside with a thick layer of **wheat straw or paddy straw**, followed by a **cow-dung and mud plaster** (typically 5–10 cm thick). The straw acts as a **thermal insulator** and the dung-mud plaster acts as a **moisture barrier**.

**Step 4 — Filling:** Grains at **safe moisture content (≤ 12 % w.b.)** are filled in layers, with each layer compacted manually to remove air pockets.

**Step 5 — Sealing:** The top is covered with straw bundles, a wooden lid, and finally a **heap of earth (15–30 cm thick)** to make it airtight and water-resistant.

**Why it works (Engineering Logic):**
- **Temperature stability:** Earth's thermal inertia keeps interior at **18–25 °C** year-round.
- **Low O₂, high CO₂ atmosphere:** Respiration of grain and microorganisms gradually depletes O₂ and raises CO₂, suppressing insect survival.
- **Protection from light, rodents, and external humidity.**

---

### 🔸 2.2.2 Bukhari — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Step 1 — Foundation:** A circular **stone or brick foundation** (plinth height 30–60 cm) is laid to prevent ground moisture and rodent entry.

**Step 2 — Wall Construction:** Walls are built of **mud bricks or stone masonry**, typically **30–45 cm thick**, plastered externally and internally with mud-cow dung mix.

**Step 3 — Roof:** A **conical/domed mud roof** is constructed to shed rainwater. A small **opening/door at the top** allows grain filling and removal.

**Step 4 — Optional Loading Chute:** A side opening at the bottom for grain discharge.

**Why it works (Engineering Logic):**
- Thick walls provide **thermal buffering** (lag time 8–12 hours).
- Conical roof ensures **zero water pooling**.
- Raised plinth ensures **zero capillary moisture rise**.
- Cow-dung plaster provides **antibacterial and insect-repellent properties**.

---

### 🔸 2.2.3 Kothar — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Step 1 — Construction:** Built as a **dedicated rectangular room**, usually **3 m × 3 m × 3 m** (or larger), with **double-walled construction** having an insulating cavity filled with **paddy husk, straw, or charcoal**.

**Step 2 — Flooring:** Floor is raised **30–60 cm** above ground level, plastered with **bitumen or cow-dung mix**, and sometimes overlaid with wooden planks.

**Step 3 — Door and Ventilation:** A small, tight-fitting **wooden door** with metal lining is provided. Optional high-level ventilation slits covered with **perforated metal sheets** allow minimal air exchange.

**Step 4 — Roof:** Sloped or flat mud-tile roof with adequate overhang.

**Why it works (Engineering Logic):**
- Double-wall insulation reduces diurnal temperature swing inside.
- Raised floor provides **moisture barrier**.
- Tight door limits **insect and rodent entry**.
- Suitable for **bulk storage and stacking of bags** inside.

---

### 🔸 2.2.4 Kuthla — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Step 1 — Bamboo Framework:** A cylindrical or oval frame is woven using **bamboo strips (or reeds)** with a mesh size of 2–5 mm.

**Step 2 — Plastering:** Both inner and outer surfaces are plastered with a thick layer of **mud + cow dung + rice husk** mix. Some variants use **lime plaster** for added durability.

**Step 3 — Base and Lid:** The base is fitted with a **wooden or stone disc**. The top has a **removable lid** for grain access.

**Step 4 — Optional Support:** The Kuthla may be hung from roof beams or placed on a wooden stool for additional protection from pests.

**Why it works (Engineering Logic):**
- Woven bamboo provides **high tensile strength with low weight**.
- Mud plaster seals the structure and provides **moisture buffering**.
- Lime (if used) provides **antifungal and antibacterial protection**.
- Easy to **inspect, repair, and relocate**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU ESE — Memorize These!**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cylindrical Capacity (Morai)** | $V_{cyl} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h$ | Volume of cylindrical pit, $m^3$ |
| 2 | **Grain Mass (Capacity)** | $M_{grain} = V \cdot \rho_{bulk}$ | $V$ in $m^3$, $\rho_{bulk}$ in $kg/m^3$, $M$ in $kg$ |
| 3 | **Bulk Density (Wheat)** | $\rho_{bulk} \approx 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Standard reference value |
| 4 | **Bulk Density (Rice, milled)** | $\rho_{bulk} \approx 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Standard reference value |
| 5 | **Bulk Density (Sorghum)** | $\rho_{bulk} \approx 720 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Standard reference value |
| 6 | **Rectangular Capacity (Kothar)** | $V_{rect} = L \cdot B \cdot H$ | Volume of storage room, $m^3$ |
| 7 | **Useful Capacity** | $V_{useful} = V_{total} \cdot \eta_{fill}$ | $\eta_{fill}$ = packing/filling efficiency (typically **0.60 – 0.75**) |
| 8 | **Surface Area (Cylinder, lateral)** | $A_{lat} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot r \cdot h$ | $m^2$ |
| 9 | **Surface Area (incl. both ends)** | $A_{total} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot r \cdot h + 2 \cdot \pi \cdot r^2$ | $m^2$ |
| 10 | **Moisture Content (w.b.)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight, in % |
| 11 | **Equilibrium Moisture Content** | $EMC = f(T, RH)$ | Function of temperature and relative humidity |
| 12 | **Storage Duration Estimate** | $t_{safe} \propto \dfrac{1}{T \cdot MC}$ | Lower T and MC = longer safe storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Bulk Densities to Remember:**
> - **Wheat:** **780 kg/m³** | **Paddy:** **580 kg/m³** | **Milled Rice:** **750 kg/m³** | **Sorghum:** **720 kg/m³** | **Maize:** **720 kg/m³** | **Bajra:** **760 kg/m³**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study these in a B.Tech Food Storage Engineering course?**
> - **Design Reference:** Modern hermetic storage (e.g., PICS bags, metal silos) borrow design logic from these traditional structures.
> - **Capacity Planning:** Farmers and FPOs (Farmer Producer Organizations) still use these, requiring engineering input for sizing.
> - **Loss Estimation:** ICAR, FAO, and NCCD use data from these structures to estimate national post-harvest losses.
> - **Climate-Resilient Storage:** As climate change increases ambient temperatures, the **passive cooling principle** of Morai and Bukhari becomes more relevant.
> - **Sustainable Development:** These structures embody **circular economy** and **zero-waste** engineering principles.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Capacity Calculation of a Morai (Underground Cylindrical Pit)

**Given Data:**
- Diameter of Morai pit, $d = 2.0 \text{ m}$
- Depth of pit, $h = 2.0 \text{ m}$
- Grain stored: Wheat
- Bulk density of wheat, $\rho_{bulk} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Filling efficiency, $\eta_{fill} = 0.70$ (accounting for straw lining and air gaps)

**Find:** Storage capacity of the Morai in **kilograms**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Determine the radius.**

$$r = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{2.0 \text{ m}}{2} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the gross cylindrical volume.**

The volume of a cylinder is given by:

$$V_{gross} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h$$

Substituting the values:

$$V_{gross} = \pi \cdot (1.0)^2 \cdot 2.0$$

$$V_{gross} = \pi \cdot 1.0 \cdot 2.0$$

$$V_{gross} = 2\pi \text{ m}^3$$

Evaluating numerically:

$$V_{gross} = 2 \times 3.1416 = 6.2832 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the useful (net) volume.**

The straw lining, mud plaster, and inevitable air gaps reduce the usable volume. Applying the filling efficiency:

$$V_{useful} = V_{gross} \cdot \eta_{fill}$$

$$V_{useful} = 6.2832 \times 0.70$$

$$V_{useful} = 4.3982 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the mass of grain stored.**

$$M_{grain} = V_{useful} \cdot \rho_{bulk}$$

$$M_{grain} = 4.3982 \times 780 \text{ kg}$$

$$M_{grain} = 3430.6 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Express the final answer with appropriate units.**

$$\boxed{M_{grain} \approx 3431 \text{ kg} \approx 3.43 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Capacity of a Bukhari (Above-Ground Cylindrical Bin)

**Given Data:**
- Internal diameter, $d = 1.8 \text{ m}$
- Internal height, $h = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- Grain stored: Sorghum (*jowar*)
- Bulk density of sorghum, $\rho_{bulk} = 720 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Filling efficiency, $\eta_{fill} = 0.80$ (above-ground, less wastage)

**Find:** Storage capacity of the Bukhari.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the radius.**

$$r = \frac{1.8}{2} = 0.9 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute gross volume.**

$$V_{gross} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h = \pi \cdot (0.9)^2 \cdot 2.5$$

$$V_{gross} = \pi \cdot 0.81 \cdot 2.5 = 2.025\pi$$

$$V_{gross} = 2.025 \times 3.1416 = 6.3617 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute useful volume.**

$$V_{useful} = 6.3617 \times 0.80 = 5.0894 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute grain mass.**

$$M_{grain} = 5.0894 \times 720 = 3664.3 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{M_{grain} \approx 3664 \text{ kg} \approx 3.66 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Capacity of a Kothar (Rectangular Room)

**Given Data:**
- Internal length, $L = 4.0 \text{ m}$
- Internal breadth, $B = 3.0 \text{ m}$
- Internal height, $H = 3.0 \text{ m}$
- Grain stored: Wheat in bags
- Bag dimensions: $0.6 \text{ m} \times 0.4 \text{ m} \times 0.3 \text{ m}$ (50 kg/bag)

**Find:** Number of 50-kg bags that can be stored.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute total internal volume.**

$$V_{total} = L \cdot B \cdot H = 4.0 \times 3.0 \times 3.0 = 36.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute volume occupied per bag.**

$$V_{bag} = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.072 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply stacking and aisle efficiency.**

For a small Kothar, accounting for stacking pattern (≈ 0.85) and aisle/access space (≈ 0.85):

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.85 \times 0.85 = 0.7225$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective usable volume.**

$$V_{useful} = 36.0 \times 0.7225 = 26.01 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Compute number of bags.**

$$N_{bags} = \frac{V_{useful}}{V_{bag}} = \frac{26.01}{0.072}$$

$$N_{bags} = 361.25$$

Since a partial bag is not possible:

$$\boxed{N_{bags} = 361 \text{ bags} \approx 18{,}050 \text{ kg} \approx 18.05 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

## 3.4 Problem 4 — Capacity of a Kuthla (Small Bamboo Bin)

**Given Data:**
- Average internal diameter, $d = 0.8 \text{ m}$
- Internal height, $h = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- Grain stored: Mixed pulses
- Bulk density of pulses (average), $\rho_{bulk} = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Filling efficiency, $\eta_{fill} = 0.75$

**Find:** Storage capacity of the Kuthla.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute radius.**

$$r = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute gross volume.**

$$V_{gross} = \pi \cdot (0.4)^2 \cdot 1.0 = \pi \cdot 0.16 = 0.5027 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute useful volume.**

$$V_{useful} = 0.5027 \times 0.75 = 0.3770 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute grain mass.**

$$M_{grain} = 0.3770 \times 800 = 301.6 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{M_{grain} \approx 302 \text{ kg}}$$

This is consistent with the **typical 50–500 kg** range of Kuthla structures.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: Define the term "Morai" as used in traditional grain storage. Mention its typical location, construction material, and storage capacity range.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Morai** is a traditional **underground cylindrical storage pit** used for long-term storage of food grains such as wheat, sorghum, and barley in the arid and semi-arid regions of North-Western India (especially Rajasthan and Haryana).

- **Location:** Underground, dug into dry, well-drained soil.
- **Construction Material:** Compacted earth walls, lined internally with **wheat straw and plastered with a mud-cow dung mixture**.
- **Storage Capacity Range:** **Approximately 500 kg to 20 tonnes**, depending on pit dimensions.

**Sealing:** The top is sealed with straw bundles, a wooden lid, and a thick earthen mound to ensure near-hermetic conditions and protection from rain, rodents, and insects.

*(Word Count Target for Answer: ~100 words)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between Bukhari and Kothar with respect to (i) shape, (ii) typical capacity, and (iii) primary construction material.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bukhari | Kothar |
|---|---|---|
| **Shape** | Cylindrical | Rectangular / Square |
| **Typical Capacity** | 1 – 10 tonnes | 5 – 50 tonnes |
| **Primary Material** | Mud bricks or stone masonry, plastered with mud-cow dung | Mud bricks or stone masonry with **double-wall construction** and straw/husk insulation |

**Conclusion:** While both are above-ground masonry storage structures, the Bukhari is a smaller cylindrical bin, whereas the Kothar is a larger rectangular room-like warehouse.

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the construction, working principle, and engineering advantages of a **Morai** as a traditional grain storage structure. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A Morai is constructed with a cylindrical pit of **diameter 2.5 m and depth 2.0 m**. Wheat (bulk density 780 kg/m³) is to be stored. Assuming a filling efficiency of 0.70, calculate the mass of wheat that can be stored in this Morai. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Construction & Working of Morai**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
A Morai is an underground cylindrical pit used for storing grains in arid regions of India, particularly Rajasthan, Haryana, and parts of Punjab. It is a low-cost, traditional structure suitable for long-term storage (1–3 years).

**2. Construction Steps (3 Marks):**
- **Site Selection:** Elevated, dry, well-drained site with low water table.
- **Excavation:** Cylindrical pit of typical dimensions — diameter 1.5–2.5 m, depth 1.5–2.5 m.
- **Wall Preparation:** Walls are compacted; lined with straw (5–10 cm thick) and plastered with mud-cow dung mix.
- **Filling:** Grain at safe moisture content (≤ 12% w.b.) is filled in compacted layers.
- **Sealing:** Top covered with straw bundle, wooden lid, and earthen mound (15–30 cm thick).

**3. Working Principle (2 Marks):**
- **Thermal Insulation:** Earth's thermal mass maintains interior temperature at 18–25 °C, suppressing insect and fungal activity.
- **Modified Atmosphere:** Respiration depletes O₂ and raises CO₂, creating a near-hermetic atmosphere.
- **Moisture Barrier:** Straw lining and mud plaster prevent external moisture ingress.

**4. Engineering Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Very low cost; zero-energy passive cooling; excellent long-term storage; protection from rodents, insects, and theft.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Capacity Calculation**

**Given:**
- Diameter, $d = 2.5 \text{ m}$, hence $r = 1.25 \text{ m}$
- Depth, $h = 2.0 \text{ m}$
- Bulk density, $\rho_{bulk} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Filling efficiency, $\eta_{fill} = 0.70$

**Step 1: Gross Volume**

$$V_{gross} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h = \pi \cdot (1.25)^2 \cdot 2.0$$

$$V_{gross} = \pi \cdot 1.5625 \cdot 2.0 = 3.125\pi$$

$$V_{gross} = 3.125 \times 3.1416 = 9.8175 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Useful Volume**

$$V_{useful} = V_{gross} \cdot \eta_{fill} = 9.8175 \times 0.70 = 6.8723 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Mass of Wheat**

$$M_{grain} = V_{useful} \cdot \rho_{bulk} = 6.8723 \times 780$$

$$M_{grain} = 5360.4 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{M_{grain} \approx 5360 \text{ kg} \approx 5.36 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the design, construction, and engineering significance of **Bukhari** and **Kuthla** as local storage structures. Compare their materials, capacity, and suitable regions. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A Kothar with internal dimensions **4 m × 3 m × 3 m** is used to store bagged wheat. Each bag has dimensions **0.6 m × 0.4 m × 0.3 m** and contains 50 kg of wheat. Assuming an overall packing-and-access efficiency of 0.72, estimate the **total mass of wheat stored** in the Kothar. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Bukhari and Kuthla Compared**

**1. Bukhari (3 Marks):**

- **Design:** Above-ground cylindrical masonry bin with a conical mud roof and a raised stone plinth.
- **Construction:** Built of mud bricks or stone masonry (wall thickness 30–45 cm), plastered internally and externally with mud-cow dung mixture.
- **Capacity:** Typically **1 – 10 tonnes.**
- **Region:** Rajasthan, Gujarat, parts of Maharashtra.
- **Engineering Significance:** Thick walls provide thermal buffering; conical roof sheds rainwater; raised plinth blocks capillary moisture and rodent entry.

**2. Kuthla (3 Marks):**

- **Design:** Small, woven, cylindrical/oval household bin.
- **Construction:** Bamboo-strip framework plastered with mud-cow dung-rice husk mix; removable wooden lid.
- **Capacity:** Typically **50 – 500 kg.**
- **Region:** Hilly regions — Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Jammu & Kashmir, North-Eastern states.
- **Engineering Significance:** Lightweight, portable, low-cost, breathable yet sealed — ideal for seed and pulse storage at the household level.

**3. Comparison (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Bukhari | Kuthla |
|---|---|---|
| Material | Mud brick / stone | Bamboo + mud plaster |
| Capacity | 1 – 10 t | 50 – 500 kg |
| Skill to build | High | Low |
| Lifespan | 8 – 15 yr | 2 – 5 yr |

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Kothar Bag Stacking Calculation**

**Given:**
- $L = 4.0 \text{ m}$, $B = 3.0 \text{ m}$, $H = 3.0 \text{ m}$
- Bag volume: $V_{bag} = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.072 \text{ m}^3$
- Overall efficiency, $\eta_{overall} = 0.72$
- Mass per bag, $m_{bag} = 50 \text{ kg}$

**Step 1: Total Internal Volume**

$$V_{total} = 4.0 \times 3.0 \times 3.0 = 36.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Useful Stacking Volume**

$$V_{useful} = 36.0 \times 0.72 = 25.92 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Number of Bags**

$$N_{bags} = \frac{V_{useful}}{V_{bag}} = \frac{25.92}{0.072} = 360$$

**Step 4: Total Mass Stored**

$$M_{total} = N_{bags} \times m_{bag} = 360 \times 50$$

$$\boxed{M_{total} = 18{,}000 \text{ kg} = 18 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to **convert units** (e.g., cm to m) when calculating volumes — always convert to **SI units (m)** before substitution.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to apply the **filling efficiency (η)** leads to overestimation by 20–40%. This is a guaranteed **loss of 1–2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** For Kothar problems, students often confuse **bulk grain storage** with **bag stacking**. Read the question carefully — the formula differs.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing **"Morai is a silo"** or **"Bukhari is a pit"** is **factually wrong** and will cost full marks on definition questions.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not specifying **moisture content limits** (≤ 12% w.b.) for safe storage in Morai answers — examiners expect this detail.
> - **Tip:** Always end your answer with a **brief conclusion or engineering significance statement** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for this routinely.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam!**

- ✅ **Morai = Underground + Cylindrical + Hermetic + 500 kg–20 t + Rajasthan/Haryana**
- ✅ **Bukhari = Above-ground + Cylindrical + Mud-brick + Conical roof + 1–10 t + Rajasthan/Gujarat**
- ✅ **Kothar = Above-ground + Rectangular + Double-wall insulated + 5–50 t + MP/Bihar/CG**
- ✅ **Kuthla = Above-ground + Bamboo-woven + Mud-plastered + Portable + 50–500 kg + HP/UK/NE India**
- ✅ **Cylindrical Volume Formula:** $V = \pi r^2 h$
- ✅ **Rectangular Volume Formula:** $V = L \times B \times H$
- ✅ **Grain Mass Formula:** $M = V_{useful} \times \rho_{bulk}$
- ✅ **Standard Bulk Densities to Memorize:**
  - Wheat = **780 kg/m³** | Sorghum = **720 kg/m³** | Rice (milled) = **750 kg/m³**
  - Maize = **720 kg/m³** | Bajra = **760 kg/m³** | Paddy = **580 kg/m³**
- ✅ **Typical Filling Efficiencies:** Morai (0.65–0.75), Bukhari (0.75–0.85), Kothar bagged (0.70–0.75), Kuthla (0.70–0.80)
- ✅ **Safe Moisture Content for Storage:** **≤ 12% (w.b.)** for cereals; **≤ 10%** for oilseeds and pulses
- ✅ **Underground Temperature Benefit:** **18–25 °C** stable interior vs. 45 °C+ surface in summer
- ✅ **Key Engineering Principles:** Passive thermal insulation, modified atmosphere, capillary moisture barrier, rodent-proof design
- ✅ **Difference Between Bukhari and Morai:** Same region, same materials — but Morai is **below ground**, Bukhari is **above ground**.
- ✅ **Difference Between Kothar and Kuthla:** Kothar is a **large permanent room**; Kuthla is a **small portable bin**.
- ✅ **Most Exam-Relevant Numerical:** Capacity calculation of cylindrical pit/bin using $M = \pi r^2 h \cdot \eta_{fill} \cdot \rho_{bulk}$
- ✅ **One-Line Definition Style (for 3-mark Qs):** *What + Where + Material + Capacity* = **full marks**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** If a question asks *"Compare any two local storage structures,"* always use a **table format with at least 4–5 parameters**. KTU examiners award **1 extra mark** for neat tabular presentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improved Storage Structures: Pusa Bin, Brick and Cement Bin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Improved Storage Structures: Pusa Bin, Brick and Cement Bin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Improved Storage Structures — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Improved Storage Structures** are scientifically engineered, low-cost on-farm grain storage systems that incorporate moisture barriers, rodent-proofing, insect-proofing, and controlled aeration features into traditional storage designs. They serve as the critical intermediate technology between primitive mud/cane bins and large-scale metallic silos, primarily engineered to reduce **post-harvest losses (5–30%)** caused by biotic (insects, rodents, fungi) and abiotic (moisture, temperature) factors.

The two structures mandated for study under the KTU 2024 PEFTT633 Module 2 syllabus are:

| Structure | Material Class | Typical Capacity | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pusa Bin** | Mud + Polyethylene Liner | **1.5 – 3 quintals** | Small & marginal farmers |
| **Brick & Cement Bin** | Brick masonry + Cement plaster | **5 – 100 quintals** | Medium to large farmers / FPOs |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a "Thermos for Grains"**
> Imagine a steel almirah (wardrobe) you use at home to keep your clothes safe from dust, moisture, and insects. Now, scale that idea up to a rural farmyard, replace the wood with mud or brick, and add a thin plastic "raincoat" lining on the inside walls. That is exactly what an **Improved Storage Structure** is — a **moisture-proof, insect-proof, rodent-proof, scientifically sealed "almirah"** for grain.

**Pusa Bin = The Mud Thermos with a Plastic Raincoat**
- The **mud walls** act as thermal mass (they keep the inside cool even in hot afternoons).
- The **polyethylene sheet** acts as a "raincoat" — blocking the moisture that would otherwise migrate from the outer mud into the grain.
- The **bottom outlet** is like a tap — letting grain out without letting insects, rats, or rain inside.

**Brick & Cement Bin = The Brick Fort**
- A more permanent, heavy-duty construction — like building a small concrete bunker for grain.
- It is designed for **larger volumes, longer storage durations, and multiple loading/unloading cycles**.
- Aeration vents and a damp-proof course (DPC) at the base prevent ground moisture creep.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)**
> - **Pusa Bin Capacity:** **1.5 to 3 quintals** (150 – 300 kg). Larger versions: up to **5 quintals**.
> - **Pusa Bin Polythene Thickness:** **200 – 250 micron** (LDPE/LLDPE film).
> - **Pusa Bin Diameter:** Standard **1.0 m to 1.2 m** (cylindrical body).
> - **Pusa Bin Height:** Approximately **1.5 m** of grain depth.
> - **Brick & Cement Bin Capacity:** **5 to 100 quintals** depending on chamber size.
> - **Standard Brick Size (Modular):** **190 mm × 90 mm × 90 mm** (or 230×110×75 mm non-modular).
> - **Cement Plaster Thickness:** **12 mm** (internal walls) and **15 mm** (external/exposed).
> - **Moisture Safe Storage Limit:** Grain moisture content must be below **12%** (cereals) and **8%** (oilseeds).
> - **Design Storage Period:** Pusa Bin = **3 to 6 months**; Brick Bin = **6 to 12 months**.

---

### 1.4 Necessity for Improved Structures

The transition from **traditional** to **improved** storage is driven by quantifiable losses:

> [!WARNING]
> **Quantitative Loss Data (Standard Reference)**
> - Traditional open mud bins: **15 – 30% loss** in 6 months.
> - Pusa Bin: Reduces losses to **< 2%** in 6 months.
> - Brick & Cement Bin: Reduces losses to **< 1%** in 12 months.
> - Source of losses: Insects (45%), Rodents (25%), Moisture/Mould (20%), Spillage/Pilferage (10%).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Pusa Bin — Engineering Breakdown

The Pusa Bin (also called **"Pusa Kothar"** or **"Pusa Improved Bin"**) was designed by the **Indian Agricultural Research Institute (IARI), Pusa, New Delhi** in the 1960s. It is a cylindrical, low-cost, on-farm storage structure.

#### 2.1.1 Construction Logic — Step by Step

1. **Site Selection:** A **raised, well-drained, plinth-protected** circular platform is prepared. The plinth height is kept at least **45 cm above ground level** to prevent seepage and flooding.
2. **Floor Construction:** A circular brick-flat-soling floor of diameter **1.0 m** is laid and finished with **1:3 cement mortar** (1 part cement : 3 parts sand) to make it damp-proof and smooth.
3. **Wall Construction (Outer Mud Wall):**
    - The cylindrical mud wall is built using **mud-clay mixed with rice husk / wheat straw** (acts as anti-crack binder).
    - Wall thickness: **5 – 7.5 cm**.
    - The wall acts as **thermal mass**, keeping the bin interior cool.
4. **Polyethylene Liner Installation (The Critical Step):**
    - A single, continuous sheet of **200–250 micron LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene)** film is wrapped on the **inner face** of the mud wall.
    - This liner acts as the **moisture vapour barrier** — the key innovation of the Pusa Bin.
    - Joints are heat-sealed or carefully lapped (no pinholes allowed).
5. **Top Cover / Roof:** A domed or conical thatch/metal sheet cover protects from rain and direct sun.
6. **Bottom Outlet / Tap:** A metallic (GI/aluminium) draw-out spout with a sliding cover is fitted near the base. It allows grain dispensing without opening the lid, maintaining the hermetic seal.
7. **Filling & Sealing:** Grain (dried to < 12% moisture) is filled to the top. The lid is sealed with adhesive tape or mud to create a **near-hermetic (semi-airtight)** atmosphere, which suppresses insect respiration.

#### 2.1.2 Why Each Component Exists — The Engineering "Why"

| Component | Engineering Function | Counter-Attack Target |
|---|---|---|
| Mud wall | Thermal insulation, low cost | Heat-driven insect breeding |
| Polythene liner | Vapour barrier | Moisture migration from soil/walls |
| Elevated plinth | Flood protection, rodent barrier | Rats, ground water |
| Bottom tap | Hermetic dispensing | Re-infestation during removal |
| Dome roof | Rain protection, no condensation | Mould, water damage |
| Sealed lid | Creates low-oxygen environment | Insect reproduction |

---

### 2.2 The Brick & Cement Bin — Engineering Breakdown

The Brick & Cement Bin is a **permanent, large-capacity, modular** storage structure suitable for **Farmers' Producer Organizations (FPOs), warehouses, and rural godowns**.

#### 2.2.1 Construction Logic — Step by Step

1. **Foundation:** A **rubble masonry or PCC (Plain Cement Concrete) foundation** of depth **60–90 cm** is laid. Below the floor, a **Damp-Proof Course (DPC)** of **40 mm thick 1:1.5:3 concrete** is laid with hot bitumen coating.
2. **Floor:** A **1:2:4 reinforced concrete floor** (100–150 mm thick) is laid with a smooth finish and slope (1 in 60) towards a floor drain/door.
3. **Walls (Load-Bearing Brick Masonry):**
    - **Single brick wall (230 mm)** for smaller bins, **double brick wall (340–460 mm)** for larger bins.
    - Built with **1:6 cement mortar**, internal surface plastered with **1:3 cement mortar** (12 mm thick) and external with **1:4 mortar** (15 mm).
    - Plaster surface is given a **neem oil + wax polish** to make it insect-repellent and dust-proof.
4. **Roof:** A **flat RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete) slab** of **100–120 mm thickness** with waterproofing treatment (bitumen + china mosaic tiles) is provided. A **false ceiling** of thermocol/insulating board can be added to prevent heat ingress.
5. **Doors & Windows:**
    - **One main door** (0.9 m × 1.8 m) for filling/cleaning, fitted with a metal shutter and rubber gasket.
    - **One small dispensing outlet** at the base.
    - Optional **ventilator windows (0.3 m × 0.3 m)** with fine wire mesh (30-mesh) for cross-ventilation.
6. **Aeration System:** Wall-mounted **aeration ducts** (PVC/perforated metal pipes) at floor level allow air circulation without exposing grain to rain.
7. **Capacity Modularity:** The bin can be a **single chamber** or **multi-chamber** (2, 4, or 6 chambers side by side), allowing **multiple crop storage in isolation**.

#### 2.2.2 Why Each Component Exists — The Engineering "Why"

| Component | Engineering Function | Counter-Attack Target |
|---|---|---|
| DPC + raised floor | Prevents capillary moisture rise | Mould, caking |
| Thick brick walls | Thermal insulation, structural load | Heat, external impact |
| Cement plaster | Seals pores in brickwork | Insect harborage in cracks |
| Neem oil coating | Natural insect repellent (azadirachtin) | Storage pests |
| Wire-mesh ventilators | Cross-ventilation without insect entry | Heat build-up, moth entry |
| RCC roof | Weather-tight, rodent-proof ceiling | Rain, rats, solar heat |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CRITICAL — Master these equations for any numerical/comparison question.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylindrical Bin Volume | $V_c = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h$ | $r$ = radius (m), $h$ = height (m), $V_c$ in m³ | Pusa Bin capacity |
| 2 | Cuboidal Bin Volume | $V_{cb} = l \cdot b \cdot h$ | $l, b, h$ in m | Brick & Cement Bin (rectangular) |
| 3 | Grain Mass Storage | $M = V \cdot \rho_b$ | $\rho_b$ = bulk density (kg/m³) | Wheat $\approx$ **780 kg/m³**, Rice $\approx$ **580 kg/m³**, Maize $\approx$ **720 kg/m³** |
| 4 | Plinth Area (Cylindrical) | $A_p = \pi \cdot r^2$ | $r$ = outer radius (m) | Material estimation |
| 5 | Wall Volume (Cylinder) | $V_w = \pi \cdot (r_o^2 - r_i^2) \cdot h$ | $r_o, r_i$ = outer & inner radii | Mud wall material |
| 6 | Number of Bricks | $N = \dfrac{V_{wall}}{V_{brick}} \cdot$ wastage factor | Wastage $\approx$ **5%** | Brick bin estimation |
| 7 | Mortar Volume | $V_m = V_{wall} - (N \times V_{brick})$ | in m³ | Cement/sand estimation |
| 8 | Dry Mortar for 1 m³ Wet Mortar | $D_m = 1.30 \times V_m$ | Factor = **1.30** (bulking) | Mix design |
| 9 | Cement Quantity | $W_c = \dfrac{D_m \times 1}{1 + n}$ | for 1:$n$ mortar ratio | Cement bags |
| 10 | Sand Quantity | $W_s = \dfrac{D_m \times n}{1 + n}$ | in m³ | Sand estimation |
| 11 | Loss Reduction Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{L_t - L_i}{L_t} \times 100$ | $L_t$ = traditional loss %, $L_i$ = improved loss % | Comparison questions |
| 12 | Moisture Migration Rate (Fick's Law) | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $D$ = diffusivity, $C$ = concentration | Concept (rare) |
| 13 | Hermetic O₂ Depletion Time | $t = \dfrac{V \times 0.21}{R_{insect} \times N}$ | Simplified empirical | Concept question |
| 14 | Cement Bag Volume | $1 \text{ bag} = 50 \text{ kg} = 0.0347 \text{ m}^3$ | Standard | Material estimation |
| 15 | Aeration Rate | $Q = A_v \times v_a$ | $A_v$ = vent area, $v_a$ = air velocity (m/s) | Brick bin vent design |

**Standard Bulk Densities (Memorize):**

| Grain | $\rho_b$ (kg/m³) |
|---|---|
| Wheat | **780** |
| Paddy (rough rice) | **580** |
| Milled rice | **750** |
| Maize | **720** |
| Sorghum | **780** |
| Pulses (chickpea) | **770** |
| Groundnut (shelled) | **650** |

---

### 2.4 Comparative Engineering Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asks comparison questions. Memorize this matrix.**

| Feature | Traditional Mud Bin | Pusa Bin | Brick & Cement Bin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 1 – 2 q | 1.5 – 3 q | 5 – 100 q |
| **Moisture Protection** | Poor | **Excellent** (PE liner) | Excellent (DPC + plaster) |
| **Insect Protection** | Poor | **Good** (hermetic) | Very Good (sealed + repellent) |
| **Rodent Protection** | Poor | Good (raised plinth) | **Excellent** (masonry) |
| **Life Span** | 1 – 2 years | 4 – 6 years | **20 – 30 years** |
| **Cost (per quintal)** | Low | Low | **High initial, low recurring** |
| **Storage Duration** | < 3 months | 6 – 9 months | **12 – 24 months** |
| **Capital Investment** | Negligible | Low (₹ 500 – 1500) | High (₹ 30,000 – 200,000) |
| **Suitability** | Subsistence | Small farmers | FPO / commercial |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Pusa Bin Capacity & Material Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Numerical — Practice this exact format.**
> **Problem:** Design a Pusa Bin for storing **3 quintals (300 kg) of wheat.** Given: Bulk density of wheat $\rho_b = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$, standard internal diameter $d = 1.0 \text{ m}$, wall thickness $t = 0.075 \text{ m}$. Calculate: (a) Required grain depth. (b) Total bin height including 15 cm headspace. (c) Polythene sheet length and area required. (d) Plinth area.

#### Part (a) — Grain Depth Calculation

**Step 1: Write the mass-volume relation.**

$$
M = V_c \cdot \rho_b
$$

**Step 2: Substitute cylindrical volume formula.**

$$
M = (\pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h_g) \cdot \rho_b
$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to solve for grain depth $h_g$.**

$$
h_g = \frac{M}{\pi \cdot r^2 \cdot \rho_b}
$$

**Step 4: Plug in numerical values.**
- $M = 300$ kg
- $r = 0.5$ m (since $d = 1.0$ m)
- $\rho_b = 780$ kg/m³

$$
h_g = \frac{300}{\pi \cdot (0.5)^2 \cdot 780}
$$

$$
h_g = \frac{300}{\pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot 780}
$$

$$
h_g = \frac{300}{3.1416 \times 0.25 \times 780}
$$

$$
h_g = \frac{300}{612.61}
$$

$$
h_g = 0.4898 \text{ m} \approx 49 \text{ cm}
$$

#### Part (b) — Total Bin Height

$$
h_{total} = h_g + h_{headspace}
$$

$$
h_{total} = 0.49 + 0.15
$$

$$
h_{total} = 0.64 \text{ m} = 64 \text{ cm}
$$

> **Note:** If $h_g$ exceeds the practical limit (≥ 1.5 m, leading to high wall pressure and compaction), the bin diameter must be increased. For a 3 q bin, 1.0 m diameter is appropriate.

#### Part (c) — Polythene Liner Requirement

**Step 1: Liner is wrapped inside the cylindrical wall + covers the floor + extends up for a top fold.**

Liner height on wall (extend to full bin height for moisture barrier):
$$
H_{liner} = h_{total} + 0.15 \text{ m (top fold-over)}
$$
$$
H_{liner} = 0.64 + 0.15 = 0.79 \text{ m} \approx 80 \text{ cm}
$$

Liner length (circumference of inner wall):
$$
L_{liner} = \pi \cdot d_{inner} = \pi \cdot 1.0 = 3.1416 \text{ m} \approx 3.15 \text{ m}
$$

Plus overlap for sealing = + 0.10 m
$$
L_{total} = 3.15 + 0.10 = 3.25 \text{ m}
$$

Liner floor (circular):
$$
A_{floor} = \pi \cdot r^2 = \pi \cdot (0.5)^2 = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2
$$

Liner wall area:
$$
A_{wall} = L_{liner} \times H_{liner} = 3.15 \times 0.80 = 2.52 \text{ m}^2
$$

Total Liner Area:
$$
A_{total} = A_{floor} + A_{wall}
$$
$$
A_{total} = 0.7854 + 2.52 = 3.3054 \text{ m}^2 \approx 3.31 \text{ m}^2
$$

> **Purchased size recommendation:** Buy a **1.0 m wide × 4.0 m long** polythene sheet (sufficient to cover floor + walls with overlaps).

#### Part (d) — Plinth Area

$$
A_p = \pi \cdot r_o^2
$$

Outer radius:
$$
r_o = r_i + t = 0.5 + 0.075 = 0.575 \text{ m}
$$

Plinth area:
$$
A_p = \pi \cdot (0.575)^2 = \pi \cdot 0.3306 = 1.0385 \text{ m}^2 \approx 1.04 \text{ m}^2
$$

#### Summary Table — Pusa Bin Design Output

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Grain depth | **0.49 m (49 cm)** |
| Total bin height | **0.64 m (64 cm)** |
| Polythene area required | **3.31 m²** |
| Recommended polythene size | **1.0 m × 4.0 m** (200 micron LDPE) |
| Plinth area | **1.04 m²** |
| Polythene mass (at 200 µm, ρ ≈ 920 kg/m³) | $0.0002 \times 3.31 \times 920 = 0.609$ kg |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Brick & Cement Bin Material Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Material Estimation Numerical**
> **Problem:** A **Brick & Cement Bin** is to be built with the following specifications: Outer dimensions = **2.5 m (L) × 2.5 m (W) × 2.0 m (H)**. Wall thickness = **230 mm (single brick)**. Floor thickness = **150 mm PCC (1:2:4)**. Internal plaster = **12 mm (1:3 cement mortar)**. Calculate: (a) Internal storage volume. (b) Capacity in quintals for wheat ($\rho_b = 780$ kg/m³). (c) Number of bricks required. (d) Cement & sand for plaster.

#### Part (a) — Internal Volume

Internal dimensions:
$$
L_i = 2.5 - 2 \times 0.230 = 2.5 - 0.46 = 2.04 \text{ m}
$$

$$
W_i = 2.04 \text{ m (symmetrical)}
$$

Internal height (assuming roof slab 120 mm):
$$
H_i = 2.0 - 0.120 = 1.88 \text{ m}
$$

Internal volume:
$$
V_i = L_i \times W_i \times H_i = 2.04 \times 2.04 \times 1.88
$$

$$
V_i = 7.823 \text{ m}^3
$$

#### Part (b) — Storage Capacity for Wheat

$$
M = V_i \times \rho_b = 7.823 \times 780
$$

$$
M = 6101.94 \text{ kg} = 61.02 \text{ quintals}
$$

#### Part (c) — Number of Bricks for Walls

Wall volume (total of 4 walls, subtracting corners):
$$
V_{wall} = (L_o + W_o) \times 2 \times t \times H_o
$$

$$
V_{wall} = (2.5 + 2.5) \times 2 \times 0.230 \times 2.0
$$

$$
V_{wall} = 5.0 \times 2 \times 0.230 \times 2.0
$$

$$
V_{wall} = 4.60 \text{ m}^3
$$

Volume of one standard brick (with mortar, nominal size 200×100×100 mm):
$$
V_{brick} = 0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.10 = 0.002 \text{ m}^3
$$

Number of bricks (with 5% wastage):
$$
N = \frac{V_{wall}}{V_{brick}} \times 1.05
$$

$$
N = \frac{4.60}{0.002} \times 1.05
$$

$$
N = 2300 \times 1.05 = 2415 \text{ bricks}
$$

#### Part (d) — Cement & Sand for Internal Plaster

Internal surface area to be plastered (4 walls + ceiling, excluding floor):

Wall area:
$$
A_{walls} = (L_i + W_i) \times 2 \times H_i = (2.04 + 2.04) \times 2 \times 1.88
$$
$$
A_{walls} = 4.08 \times 2 \times 1.88 = 15.34 \text{ m}^2
$$

Ceiling (RCC underside) area:
$$
A_{ceiling} = L_i \times W_i = 2.04 \times 2.04 = 4.1616 \text{ m}^2
$$

Total plastered area:
$$
A_{total} = 15.34 + 4.16 = 19.50 \text{ m}^2
$$

Wet mortar volume (12 mm thick):
$$
V_{wet} = A_{total} \times 0.012 = 19.50 \times 0.012 = 0.234 \text{ m}^3
$$

Dry mortar volume (×1.30 bulking):
$$
V_{dry} = 0.234 \times 1.30 = 0.3042 \text{ m}^3
$$

For 1:3 mortar (1 part cement : 3 parts sand), sum ratio = 4:
Cement volume:
$$
V_{cement} = \frac{V_{dry} \times 1}{4} = \frac{0.3042}{4} = 0.0761 \text{ m}^3
$$

Sand volume:
$$
V_{sand} = \frac{V_{dry} \times 3}{4} = \frac{0.3042 \times 3}{4} = 0.2282 \text{ m}^3
$$

Convert cement volume to mass (cement bulk density ≈ 1440 kg/m³):
$$
M_{cement} = 0.0761 \times 1440 = 109.6 \text{ kg}
$$

Number of cement bags (1 bag = 50 kg):
$$
N_{bags} = \frac{109.6}{50} = 2.19 \approx 3 \text{ bags (rounded up)}
$$

#### Summary Table — Brick & Cement Bin Design Output

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Internal storage volume | **7.82 m³** |
| Wheat storage capacity | **61 quintals (6.1 tonnes)** |
| Number of bricks | **2,415** (with 5% wastage) |
| Plastered area (internal) | **19.50 m²** |
| Cement required (plaster only) | **109.6 kg ≈ 3 bags** |
| Sand required (plaster only) | **0.23 m³** |

---

### 3.3 Loss Reduction Efficiency Calculation (Analytical)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick formula for "How much loss is prevented" type questions.**

**Problem:** A farmer traditionally loses 25% of stored grain. With a Pusa Bin, losses drop to 3%. What is the loss reduction efficiency?

$$
\eta = \frac{L_t - L_i}{L_t} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{25 - 3}{25} \times 100 = \frac{22}{25} \times 100 = 88\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The Pusa Bin **prevents 88% of the losses** experienced in traditional storage — a powerful justification for technology adoption.

If 10 quintals stored, traditional loss = **2.5 q**; Pusa loss = **0.3 q**; Savings = **2.2 q per cycle**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Reminder

> [!NOTE]
> Per protocol: all Mermaid node IDs use the `nodeX` pattern (alphanumeric + letter prefix). No reserved keywords, no markdown formatting inside quoted labels. Labels are clean uppercase or Title Case only.

### 4.2 Pusa Bin — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    siteA["SITE SELECTION"] --> siteB["RAISED PLINTH 45 CM ABOVE GROUND"]
    siteB --> siteC["BRICK FLAT SOLING CIRCULAR DIA 1.0 M"]
    siteC --> siteD["CEMENT MORTAR 1:3 FLOOR FINISH"]
    siteD --> siteE["MUD WALL CONSTRUCTION THICKNESS 7.5 CM"]
    siteE --> siteF["POLYTHENE LINER 200 MICRON LDPE INSTALLATION"]
    siteF --> siteG["GRAIN FILLING MOISTURE LESS THAN 12 PERCENT"]
    siteG --> siteH["HERMETIC LID SEAL WITH TAPE OR MUD"]
    siteH --> siteI["STORAGE PERIOD 6 TO 9 MONTHS"]
    siteI --> siteJ["DRAWING GRAIN VIA BOTTOM TAP"]

    moA["MOISTURE BARRIER FUNCTION"] -.-> siteF
    inB["INSECT HERMETIC CONTROL"] -.-> siteH
    roC["RODENT PROTECTION"] -.-> siteB
    thD["THERMAL INSULATION"] -.-> siteE
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = the linear construction sequence.
- **Dashed arrows (`.->`)** = engineering function attribution (i.e., which component achieves which protective goal).

### 4.3 Brick & Cement Bin — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    foA["FOUNDATION PCC DEPTH 60 TO 90 CM"] --> foB["DAMP PROOF COURSE 40 MM BITUMEN COATED"]
    foB --> foC["RCC FLOOR 100 TO 150 MM 1:2:4 SLOPE 1 IN 60"]
    foC --> waA["BRICK WALLS 230 MM 1:6 MORTAR"]
    waA --> waB["INTERNAL PLASTER 12 MM 1:3 CEMENT MORTAR"]
    waB --> waC["NEEM OIL COATING INSECT REPELLENT"]
    waC --> roA["RCC ROOF SLAB 120 MM WATERPROOFING"]
    roA --> roB["MAIN DOOR METAL SHUTTER 0.9 X 1.8 M"]
    roB --> veA["VENTILATOR WINDOWS 30 MESH WIRE GAUZE"]
    veA --> aeA["AERATION DUCTS AT FLOOR LEVEL"]
    aeA --> fiA["FILLING VIA TOP DOOR"]
    fiA --> diA["DISPENSING VIA BOTTOM OUTLET"]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Component Flow — Pusa vs Brick Bin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pusa["PUSA BIN SUBSYSTEM"] --> p1["Mud Wall Subsystem"]
    pusa --> p2["Polythene Liner Subsystem"]
    pusa --> p3["Bottom Tap Subsystem"]
    pusa --> p4["Dome Roof Subsystem"]

    brick["BRICK CEMENT BIN SUBSYSTEM"] --> b1["Masonry Wall Subsystem"]
    brick --> b2["DPC and Plaster Subsystem"]
    brick --> b3["RCC Roof Subsystem"]
    brick --> b4["Aeration Subsystem"]

    p1 --- sharedA["Thermal Insulation Function"]
    b1 --- sharedA
    p2 --- sharedB["Moisture Barrier Function"]
    b2 --- sharedB
    p3 --- sharedC["Insect Proof Sealing"]
    b4 --- sharedC
    p4 --- sharedD["Weather Protection"]
    b3 --- sharedD
```

### 4.5 Decision Logic — Which Bin to Choose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["FARMER STORAGE NEED"] --> q1{"QUANTITY BELOW 5 QUINTALS?"}
    q1 -- YES --> q2{"BUDGET LESS THAN 2000 INR?"}
    q1 -- NO --> q3{"BUDGET MORE THAN 30000 INR?"}
    q2 -- YES --> pusa["USE PUSA BIN"]
    q2 -- NO --> brick["USE BRICK CEMENT BIN"]
    q3 -- YES --> brick
    q3 -- NO --> coop["USE COOPERATIVE WAREHOUSE OR METALLIC SILO"]
```
