Affects long-term storage stability. **(b) Mass flow vs. Funnel flow:** | Aspect | Mass Flow | Funnel Flow | |---|---|---| | **Flow zone** | Entire cross-section moves | Only central channel moves | | **Stagnant zones** | None | Yes, along walls | | **Discharge sequence** | First-In, First-Out (FIFO) | First-In, Last-Out (FILO) | | **Quality preservation** | Excellent (no spoilage in stagnant zones) | Poor (stagnant zones spoil) | | **Aeration uniformity** | Good | Poor | | **Segregation** | Less | More | | **Hopper angle requirement** | Steep, $\theta < \theta_{crit}$ | Shallow, $\theta > \theta_{crit}$ allowed | | **Cost** | Higher (taller hopper) | Lower | **Hopper angle criterion for mass flow (conical hopper):** For a conical hopper, mass flow is achieved when the hopper half-angle $\theta$ satisfies: $$\theta \leq \theta_{crit} = 45° - \frac{\phi}{2}$$ where $\phi$ is the **effective angle of internal friction** of the bulk solid. For wheat with $\phi = 25°$: $$\theta_{crit} = 45° - 12.5° = 32.5°$$ So the conical hopper half-angle must be **≤ 32.5°** (i.e., a very steep hopper) to ensure mass flow. **Industry preference (food applications):** - **Mass flow silos** are preferred for **flour, sugar, milk powder, salt, and spices** — these are cohesive and deblend in funnel flow, leading to quality and safety issues. - **Funnel flow silos** are acceptable for **whole, free-flowing grains** like wheat, maize, soybeans, and barley — provided aeration is provided and storage time is moderate. - **Critical food safety note:** Stagnant zones in funnel flow silos are a major source of **mold, mycotoxins, and insect infestation** in food grains. Hence, modern food-grade silos increasingly use **mass flow** design even at higher capital cost. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:** > 1. **Forgetting to state the assumptions** of Janssen's equation (5+ marks lost in part-a derivations). > 2. **Confusing K and 1/K** — many students invert the Jaky formula. The correct form is $K = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$, **not** $(1+\sin\phi)/(1-\sin\phi)$. > 3. **Using the wrong sign in the exponent** of Janssen's equation. The exponent must be **negative** with depth $z$ in the positive direction. > 4. **Mixing up mass flow and funnel flow** definitions. Mass flow = FIFO; funnel flow = FILO. Examiners explicitly test this. > 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** (Pa vs. kPa) — partial mark loss is common. > 6. **Not converting degrees to radians** in the exponent for very precise numerical work (typically 0.5 mark lost). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Bulk material:** Discrete particles handled in large quantities; characterized by bulk density, particle density, angle of repose, internal friction, wall friction, EMC. - **Bulk storage:** Large-scale unpackaged containment of food materials in engineered structures (silos, bins, bunkers, warehouses). - **Silo:** Vertical cylindrical structure with H/D ≥ 1.5, designed for gravity discharge via a hopper. - **Janssen's equation (KEY EQUATION):** $\sigma_v(z) = \dfrac{\rho_b g D}{4\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\frac{4\mu K z}{D}}\right)$ — **memorize this verbatim** for KTU exams. - **Jaky's K:** $K = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$ — valid for at-rest, normally consolidated grain. - **Saturation pressure:** $\sigma_{v,\infty} = \rho_b g D / (4\mu K)$ — a maximum cap; floor pressure does **not** grow with depth indefinitely. - **Wall friction:** $q_w = \mu \sigma_h$ — this is what transfers load from grain to wall. - **Hoop tension:** $T = \sigma_h D / 2$ — drives silo wall thickness design. - **Mass flow vs Funnel flow:** Mass flow = FIFO, steep hopper, no stagnant zones (used for flour, sugar, milk powder). Funnel flow = FILO, shallow hopper, stagnant zones at walls (acceptable for whole grains like wheat, maize). - **Hopper angle for mass flow (conical):** $\theta \leq 45° - \phi/2$. - **Critical silo phenomena to recognize:** arching (bridging), ratholing (piping), quaking (silo honking), segregation, hot-spot formation, spontaneous combustion in oilseeds. - **Safe moisture contents:** Wheat ≤ 14% w.b., Rice ≤ 13% w.b., Maize ≤ 13% w.b. - **Standards to remember:** **ISO 11697** (silo loads), **Eurocode 1 Part 4 / EN 1991-4** (silo design), **AS 3774** (Australian silo loads). - **Eurocode K vs. Janssen K:** Eurocode 1 Part 4 uses an **empirical K** that depends on $\phi$, not the simple Jaky form. For Module-3 level, Jaky's K is sufficient. - **Numbers to remember:** $\rho_b$ wheat = 780 kg/m³; $\rho_p$ wheat = 1340 kg/m³; $\phi$ wheat ≈ 25°; $\phi_w$ wheat on steel ≈ 22°. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-line board-exam mantra:** *"In a silo, the walls hold the weight; the floor holds only the saturation pressure."* — Always use this framing in derivations to earn full marks.

topics.title: Types of Silos: Deep, Shallow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Silos: Deep, Shallow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **silo** is an enclosed bulk storage structure designed to hold granular, powdered, or particulate food materials (such as grains, flour, sugar, salt, animal feed, and seeds) under controlled environmental conditions, protecting them from moisture, pests, contamination, and spoilage.

Silos are broadly classified based on the **Height-to-Diameter (H/D) ratio** into two principal categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Deep vs. Shallow Silos**
> - **Deep Silo (Tall Silo):** A storage structure in which the **H/D ratio is greater than 1.5 (H/D > 1.5)**. Internal pressures are dominated by **arching and frictional mobilization** along the vertical walls. Pressure distribution follows the **Janssen Silo Theory**.
> - **Shallow Silo (Bin):** A storage structure in which the **H/D ratio is less than 1.5 (H/D < 1.5)**. Internal pressures are governed primarily by **gravitational hydrostatic loading**. Pressure distribution follows the **Rankine / Coulomb Earth Pressure analogy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a stack of soft sponge discs in a tall, narrow glass tube versus a wide, shallow baking tray:

- **Deep Silo** = Tall glass tube: As you keep adding sponge discs, the discs at the bottom are squeezed sideways **not just by gravity straight down, but also because friction grips the side walls**. Most of the weight is "absorbed" by the wall friction. The bottom discs feel much less vertical pressure than if they were in open air.

- **Shallow Silo** = Wide baking tray: Because the tray is short and wide, the side walls cannot "grab" enough material to relieve the bottom pressure. The bottom material feels almost the **full weight** of the column above it — similar to water in a shallow pool pressing on the floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a deep silo, the walls act as a structural partner, **carrying vertical load** through friction. In a shallow silo, the floor carries the **majority of the vertical load**, because wall friction is insufficient to relieve the pressure.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following standardized metrics are universally accepted in KTU-validated food storage engineering practice:

- **H/D ratio threshold:** **1.5** (IS 4995, DIN 1055, Eurocode 1 Part 4)
- **Bulk density of wheat:** ≈ **780 kg/m³**
- **Bulk density of rice:** ≈ **750 kg/m³**
- **Bulk density of maize:** ≈ **720 kg/m³**
- **Angle of internal friction (φ):** 25°–35° (for most food grains)
- **Coefficient of wall friction (μ):** 0.3–0.5 (steel silo with grain)
- **Lateral pressure ratio (K):** 0.4–0.6 (Janssen's K-value)
- **Standard design lateral pressure coefficient:** **K = 0.4** (for grain in steel silos as per Indian Standard IS 4995)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Deep Silos (H/D > 1.5)

1. **Vertical Load Transfer Mechanism:** When granular material is filled into a deep silo, vertical stress (σᵥ) does **not** increase linearly with depth as it would in a liquid. Instead, due to the mobilization of friction along the cylindrical wall, the vertical stress asymptotically approaches a **finite limiting value** (σᵥ∞).

2. **Frictional Mobilization:** The wall exerts an upward frictional shear stress (τw) opposing the downward movement tendency of the bulk solid. This friction **transfers part of the vertical load** from the bulk solid to the silo wall.

3. **Stress State (Active):** The bulk solid shifts from a "K₀" (at-rest) state to an "active" state as filling and discharge occur. Lateral pressure (σₕ) becomes a fraction K of the vertical pressure.

4. **Janssen Governing Equation (1895):** This is the foundational equation for deep silo pressure analysis. It assumes a horizontal slice element of bulk solid in equilibrium under gravity, wall friction, and internal pressure redistribution.

5. **Flow Behavior:** Deep silos can exhibit **funnel flow** (first-in/last-out, central core flows first) or **mass flow** (first-in/first-out, all material moves uniformly). Design choice depends on the food material's flow properties.

### 2.1.2 Shallow Silos / Bins (H/D < 1.5)

1. **Hydrostatic-Analogous Behavior:** Because the wall height is too short to mobilize significant friction, the bulk solid behaves almost like a dense fluid. Vertical stress increases **approximately linearly** with depth (σᵥ ≈ ρgh).

2. **Wall Friction is Negligible:** The frictional contribution to vertical load relief is small. Hence, **the floor bears nearly the full weight** of the stored material.

3. **Rankine / Coulomb Theory Applies:** Lateral pressure is calculated using the Rankine active earth pressure coefficient:
   - **K_a = (1 − sin φ) / (1 + sin φ)** = tan²(45° − φ/2)

4. **Application Suitability:** Shallow bins are used for **short-duration storage**, intermediate process buffers, and materials with good flowability where mass flow is achievable with shallow hoppers.

5. **Structural Simplicity:** Since lateral pressures are modest, shallow bins can be constructed with lighter-gauge walls and simpler foundations.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Deep Silo Formula | Shallow Silo Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Vertical Pressure (Lateral) | $\sigma_v = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K}\left(1 - e^{-4\mu K h / D}\right)$ | $\sigma_v = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | Lateral (Horizontal) Pressure | $\sigma_h = K \cdot \sigma_v$ | $\sigma_h = K_a \cdot \sigma_v$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 3 | Lateral Pressure Ratio | $K = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v}$ (Janssen K) | $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2) = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Wall Frictional Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \mu \cdot \sigma_h$ | $\tau_w \approx 0$ (often neglected) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 5 | Asymptotic (Maximum) Vertical Pressure | $\sigma_{v,\infty} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K}$ | Not applicable | Pa (N/m²) |
| 6 | Hopper Pressure (Conical bottom) | $\sigma_{vh} = \sigma_{v,\text{transition}} \cdot \dfrac{D}{D - h_{hop}}$ | $\sigma_{vh} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (h + h_{hop})$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 7 | Total Vertical Load on Silo Wall | $P_w = \int_0^H \pi D \cdot \tau_w \, dh$ | $P_w \approx 0$ | N (Newtons) |
| 8 | Critical H/D Transition | $\dfrac{H}{D} > 1.5$ → Deep | $\dfrac{H}{D} < 1.5$ → Shallow | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** Always verify the H/D ratio **after** considering any hopper or conical bottom extension. The transition between deep and shallow regimes directly affects which formula set is applied in KTU exam numerical problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deep Silos in Kerala's Coconut & Paddy Industry:** Used in KSEB rice warehouses, FCI godowns, and coconut copra storage facilities where **long-duration bulk storage** (3–12 months) is required.
- **Shallow Silos in Flour Mills & Sugar Refineries:** Common as day-buffer bins between cleaning, milling, and packaging sections where **rapid in-and-out flow** is critical.
- **International Relevance:** Eurocode 1 Part 4 (EN 1991-4) uses similar H/D classification for structural design of silos across the food, chemical, and cement industries.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Janssen's Equation for Vertical Pressure in a Deep Silo

### Setup
Consider a horizontal differential slice of bulk solid at depth $h$ inside a vertical cylindrical silo of internal diameter $D$. The slice has thickness $dh$. The bulk solid has bulk density $\rho$ and is in active failure state with lateral pressure ratio $K$ and wall friction coefficient $\mu$.

### Step-by-Step Equilibrium

**Step 1:** Write vertical force equilibrium on the differential slice of cross-sectional area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.

The forces acting on the slice are:
- Weight of slice (downward) = $\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot dh$
- Vertical pressure from material above on top of slice (downward) = $\sigma_v \cdot A$
- Vertical pressure from material below on bottom of slice (upward) = $\left(\sigma_v + d\sigma_v\right) \cdot A$
- Frictional force from wall (upward) = $\tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot dh$

**Step 2:** Apply the wall friction relationship. The wall frictional shear stress is given by:

$$\tau_w = \mu \cdot \sigma_h = \mu \cdot K \cdot \sigma_v$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the equilibrium equation (Sum of vertical forces = 0):

$$\left(\sigma_v + d\sigma_v\right) \cdot A = \sigma_v \cdot A + \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot dh - \mu \cdot K \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \pi D \cdot dh$$

**Step 4:** Simplify by cancelling $\sigma_v \cdot A$ from both sides:

$$A \cdot d\sigma_v = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot dh - \mu \cdot K \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \pi D \cdot dh$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$\frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot d\sigma_v = \rho \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot dh - \mu \cdot K \cdot \sigma_v \cdot \pi D \cdot dh$$

**Step 6:** Cancel the common factor $\pi D$ from both sides:

$$\frac{D}{4} \cdot d\sigma_v = \rho \cdot g \cdot \frac{D}{4} \cdot dh - \mu \cdot K \cdot \sigma_v \cdot dh$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange into a standard first-order linear ODE form:

$$\frac{d\sigma_v}{dh} + \frac{4 \mu K}{D} \cdot \sigma_v = \rho \cdot g$$

**Step 8:** Identify this as $\dfrac{d\sigma_v}{dh} + P \cdot \sigma_v = Q$ with $P = \dfrac{4\mu K}{D}$ and $Q = \rho g$.

The integrating factor is $e^{Ph} = e^{4\mu K h / D}$.

**Step 9:** Multiply both sides by the integrating factor:

$$e^{4\mu K h / D} \cdot \frac{d\sigma_v}{dh} + \frac{4 \mu K}{D} \cdot e^{4\mu K h / D} \cdot \sigma_v = \rho g \cdot e^{4\mu K h / D}$$

**Step 10:** The left-hand side is the derivative of $\left(\sigma_v \cdot e^{4\mu K h / D}\right)$:

$$\frac{d}{dh}\left(\sigma_v \cdot e^{4\mu K h / D}\right) = \rho g \cdot e^{4\mu K h / D}$$

**Step 11:** Integrate both sides with respect to $h$:

$$\sigma_v \cdot e^{4\mu K h / D} = \frac{\rho g \cdot D}{4 \mu K} \cdot e^{4\mu K h / D} + C$$

**Step 12:** Apply the boundary condition: At the top surface ($h = 0$), the vertical pressure equals the surcharge pressure $q$ (typically $q = 0$ for free-flowing grain).

For $q = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{\rho g \cdot D}{4 \mu K} \cdot 1 + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = -\frac{\rho g \cdot D}{4 \mu K}$$

**Step 13:** Substitute back to get the final Janssen equation:

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-4\mu K h / D}\right)$$

**Step 14:** As $h \to \infty$, the exponential term vanishes, yielding the **asymptotic maximum vertical pressure**:

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rankine's Active Earth Pressure Coefficient for Shallow Silos

### Setup
For a shallow silo where the bulk solid behaves hydrostatically, the lateral pressure is determined by the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion at the limit of active failure.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion states that at active failure:

$$\sin \phi = \frac{\sigma_v - \sigma_h}{\sigma_v + \sigma_h}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the ratio $\sigma_h / \sigma_v$:

$$\sigma_v - \sigma_h = \sin \phi \cdot (\sigma_v + \sigma_h)$$

$$\sigma_v - \sigma_h = \sigma_v \sin \phi + \sigma_h \sin \phi$$

**Step 3:** Collect $\sigma_h$ terms on one side:

$$\sigma_v - \sigma_v \sin \phi = \sigma_h + \sigma_h \sin \phi$$

$$\sigma_v (1 - \sin \phi) = \sigma_h (1 + \sin \phi)$$

**Step 4:** Define the lateral pressure ratio:

$$K_a = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the trigonometric identity. Note that $\dfrac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$.

Therefore:

$$K_a = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** For a typical food grain with $\phi = 30°$:

$$K_a = \tan^2(45° - 15°) = \tan^2(30°) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A cylindrical silo stores wheat (bulk density $\rho = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.4$, $K = 0.5$, $D = 6 \text{ m}$) up to a height of $H = 12 \text{ m}$.

**(a)** Determine whether the silo is **deep or shallow**.

**(b)** Calculate the **vertical pressure** at the base.

**(c)** Calculate the **lateral pressure** at the base.

**(d)** Calculate the **asymptotic maximum vertical pressure** if the silo were infinitely tall.

### Solution

**Part (a):** Classification

$$\frac{H}{D} = \frac{12}{6} = 2.0$$

Since $\dfrac{H}{D} = 2.0 > 1.5$, this is a **Deep Silo**. Janssen's equation applies.

**Part (b):** Vertical pressure at base

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-4\mu K H / D}\right)$$

Compute the exponential argument:

$$\frac{4 \mu K H}{D} = \frac{4 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 12}{6} = \frac{9.6}{6} = 1.6$$

$$\sigma_v = \frac{780 \times 9.81 \times 6}{4 \times 0.4 \times 0.5} \times \left(1 - e^{-1.6}\right)$$

$$\sigma_v = \frac{45,910.8}{0.8} \times (1 - 0.2019)$$

$$\sigma_v = 57,388.5 \times 0.7981 \approx 45,797 \text{ Pa} \approx 45.8 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (c):** Lateral pressure at base

$$\sigma_h = K \cdot \sigma_v = 0.5 \times 45,797 \approx 22,898 \text{ Pa} \approx 22.9 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (d):** Asymptotic maximum vertical pressure

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K} = \frac{780 \times 9.81 \times 6}{0.8} = 57,388.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 57.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Verification:** At $H = 12$ m, the silo has reached $\dfrac{45,797}{57,388.5} \approx 79.8\%$ of its maximum theoretical pressure. Adding more height would only marginally increase $\sigma_v$, confirming the **self-limiting nature** of deep silo pressure — a hallmark feature of Janssen's theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Hydrostatic Assumption (Shallow Formula):** If we had incorrectly used $\sigma_v = \rho g H = 780 \times 9.81 \times 12 = 91,821$ Pa, we would have **overestimated** the pressure by ~100%. This is the classic error KTU examiners target in numerical problems.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q:** Define a deep silo. What is the criterion used to classify a silo as deep or shallow?

**Model Answer:**

A deep silo is a vertical cylindrical storage structure in which the **height-to-diameter ratio (H/D) is greater than 1.5**. The criterion for classification is the geometric H/D ratio:

- If **H/D > 1.5** → **Deep Silo** (Janssen's equation governs pressure)
- If **H/D < 1.5** → **Shallow Silo / Bin** (Rankine's theory governs pressure)

This threshold is universally adopted in Indian Standard **IS 4995**, Eurocode 1 Part 4, and German DIN 1055. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q:** State Janssen's equation for vertical pressure in a deep silo and explain the physical significance of the asymptotic term.

**Model Answer:**

Janssen's equation for vertical pressure at depth $h$ in a deep silo is:

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-4\mu K h / D}\right)$$

**Physical significance of the asymptotic term:**

As $h \to \infty$, the exponential term $e^{-4\mu K h / D} \to 0$, and the vertical pressure approaches a **finite limiting value**:

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K}$$

This represents the **maximum possible vertical stress** that can ever be transmitted to the silo floor, regardless of how tall the silo is. The wall friction **absorbs the additional weight** of any material added above the asymptotic depth. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **structural and functional differences** between deep and shallow silos. Discuss their typical applications in the **food storage industry**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A deep cylindrical silo has an internal diameter of $D = 8$ m and stores maize ($\rho = 720 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.45$, $K = 0.5$) to a height of $H = 14$ m. Calculate:
- (i) The vertical pressure at the base. *(3 marks)*
- (ii) The lateral pressure at the base. *(2 marks)*
- (iii) The wall frictional shear stress at the base. *(2 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Differences and Applications:**

| Parameter | Deep Silo (H/D > 1.5) | Shallow Silo / Bin (H/D < 1.5) |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| H/D Ratio | > 1.5 | < 1.5 |
| Governing Theory | Janssen's Equation | Rankine's Earth Pressure Theory |
| Vertical Stress Profile | Asymptotic / Self-limiting | Hydrostatic (Linear) |
| Wall Friction Effect | Significant — relieves floor load | Negligible |
| Floor Load | Reduced (asymptotic) | Full hydrostatic |
| Storage Duration | Long-term (3–12 months) | Short-term (hours to days) |
| Typical Applications | FCI godowns, rice warehouses, port grain silos, copra storage | Flour mill buffer bins, sugar refinery hoppers, day-storage bins |
| Cost | Higher — needs reinforced walls | Lower — lighter construction |
| Flow Pattern Risk | Funnel flow, ratholing | Generally mass flow |

**Applications in Food Industry:**

- **Deep silos** are used by FCI (Food Corporation of India), Kerala State Civil Supplies Corporation, and private rice millers for **paddy, rice, wheat, and maize storage** for periods of 3–12 months.
- **Shallow bins** are used inside processing plants — e.g., in roller flour mills as **buffer bins between cleaning and milling sections**, in sugar refineries for **raw sugar holding**, and in spice processing units for **short-term holding**. *(7 marks)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Verify deep silo classification:

$$\frac{H}{D} = \frac{14}{8} = 1.75 > 1.5 \quad \therefore \text{Deep Silo (Janssen applies)} \checkmark$$

**Step 2 (i):** Calculate the exponential argument:

$$\frac{4 \mu K H}{D} = \frac{4 \times 0.45 \times 0.5 \times 14}{8} = \frac{12.6}{8} = 1.575$$

**Step 3 (i):** Calculate the coefficient:

$$\frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K} = \frac{720 \times 9.81 \times 8}{4 \times 0.45 \times 0.5} = \frac{56,505.6}{0.9} = 62,784 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 (i):** Apply Janssen's equation:

$$\sigma_v = 62,784 \times \left(1 - e^{-1.575}\right) = 62,784 \times (1 - 0.2071) = 62,784 \times 0.7929$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_v \approx 49,780 \text{ Pa} \approx 49.78 \text{ kPa}} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 5 (ii):** Lateral pressure at base:

$$\sigma_h = K \cdot \sigma_v = 0.5 \times 49,780 = \boxed{24,890 \text{ Pa} \approx 24.89 \text{ kPa}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 6 (iii):** Wall frictional shear stress at base:

$$\tau_w = \mu \cdot \sigma_h = 0.45 \times 24,890 = \boxed{11,200.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 11.2 \text{ kPa}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Verification:** If hydrostatic assumption (shallow formula) were wrongly used: $\sigma_v = 720 \times 9.81 \times 14 = 98,884.8$ Pa — nearly **double** the Janssen value, confirming the importance of correct classification. ✅

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive **Janssen's equation** for vertical pressure in a deep silo, starting from a vertical force balance on a horizontal differential slice. State clearly all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shallow bin has a height of $H = 2.5$ m and diameter $D = 3$ m. It stores sugar (bulk density $\rho = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$, angle of internal friction $\phi = 35°$). Calculate:
- (i) The Rankine active lateral pressure coefficient. *(2 marks)*
- (ii) The vertical pressure at the base. *(2 marks)*
- (iii) The lateral pressure at the base. *(3 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 for full step-by-step):**

**Assumptions of Janssen's Theory:**

1. The bulk solid is in an **active state of failure** (i.e., it has yielded and is mobilizing internal friction).
2. The lateral-to-vertical pressure ratio $K$ is **constant** throughout the silo height.
3. The wall friction coefficient $\mu$ is **constant** at all depths.
4. The bulk solid is **horizontally isotropic** (same properties in all horizontal directions).
5. The vertical walls are **rigid** and do not deform outward.
6. The **arching effect** stabilizes within the bulk solid so that vertical stress does not exceed the asymptotic limit.

**Governing ODE:**

$$\frac{d\sigma_v}{dh} + \frac{4\mu K}{D}\sigma_v = \rho g$$

**Solution (with $\sigma_v = 0$ at $h = 0$):**

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\rho g D}{4 \mu K}\left(1 - e^{-4\mu K h / D}\right)$$

*Full derivation is provided in Section 3.1 of these notes.* *(7 marks)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Verify shallow bin classification:

$$\frac{H}{D} = \frac{2.5}{3} = 0.833 < 1.5 \quad \therefore \text{Shallow Bin (Rankine applies)} \checkmark$$

**Step 2 (i):** Rankine active lateral pressure coefficient:

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \frac{1 - \sin 35°}{1 + \sin 35°} = \frac{1 - 0.5736}{1 + 0.5736} = \frac{0.4264}{1.5736}$$

$$\boxed{K_a \approx 0.271} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 3 (ii):** Vertical pressure at base (hydrostatic):

$$\sigma_v = \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 800 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 = \boxed{19,620 \text{ Pa} \approx 19.62 \text{ kPa}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 4 (iii):** Lateral pressure at base:

$$\sigma_h = K_a \cdot \sigma_v = 0.271 \times 19,620 = \boxed{5,317 \text{ Pa} \approx 5.32 \text{ kPa}} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$

**Verification:** For shallow bins, the lateral pressure is roughly 27% of the vertical — a much lower ratio than the deep silo case (typically 40–60%). This validates the design principle of using shallow bins for materials stored in small lots.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Misclassification trap (2–3 marks lost):** Students often apply Janssen's equation to shallow bins or Rankine to deep silos. **Always compute H/D ratio first.**
> 2. **Unit inconsistency (1 mark lost):** Mixing bulk density in kg/m³ with $g$ in m/s² yields Pa. Do NOT use weight density $\gamma = \rho g$ in kN/m³ unless you convert carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting the boundary condition:** Janssen's ODE requires $\sigma_v(0) = 0$ (or $\sigma_v(0) = q$ for surcharge). Setting this wrong gives an incorrect integration constant.
> 4. **Assuming linear pressure in deep silos:** This is the most common conceptual error. Pressure in a deep silo is **asymptotic, not linear**.
> 5. **Neglecting hopper pressure:** KTU numerical problems sometimes include a conical hopper. Always check whether the problem specifies a hopper and apply the hopper transition formula accordingly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Silos: Deep vs. Shallow**

- ✅ **Classification rule:** H/D > 1.5 → **Deep Silo** | H/D < 1.5 → **Shallow Bin** *(IS 4995 standard)*.
- ✅ **Deep silo pressure** → governed by **Janssen's equation** (1895), giving asymptotic vertical stress.
- ✅ **Shallow bin pressure** → governed by **Rankine's active earth pressure theory**, giving hydrostatic-linear stress.
- ✅ **Janssen equation:** $\sigma_v = \dfrac{\rho g D}{4\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-4\mu K h / D}\right)$.
- ✅ **Asymptotic pressure (deep silo max):** $\sigma_{v,\infty} = \dfrac{\rho g D}{4\mu K}$ — pressure **does not increase indefinitely** with height.
- ✅ **Rankine coefficient:** $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2) = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$.
- ✅ **Wall friction formula:** $\tau_w = \mu \cdot \sigma_h = \mu K \sigma_v$.
- ✅ **Standard design K for grain:** ~ **0.4** (IS 4995); typical food grain angle of internal friction: 25°–35°.
- ✅ **Wall carries vertical load** in deep silos via friction; **floor carries vertical load** in shallow bins.
- ✅ **Applications:** Deep silos → long-term grain storage (FCI, port silos). Shallow bins → process buffer bins in flour mills, sugar refineries.
- ✅ **Common KTU exam trap:** Applying the wrong theory due to misclassification — always start by computing the H/D ratio.
- ✅ **Numerical must-know:** For $H/D = 1.75$, Janssen pressure ≈ 79% of asymptotic value; for $H/D = 2.0$, ≈ 85%.
- ✅ **International codes:** Eurocode 1 Part 4, IS 4995, DIN 1055 — all use the same 1.5 H/D threshold.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Factors Influencing Flow Characteristics, Mechanics of Bulk Solids, Flow Through Hoppers, Openings, and Ducts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical Factors Influencing Flow Characteristics, Mechanics of Bulk Solids, Flow Through Hoppers, Openings, and Ducts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bulk Solids and Their Flow Behaviour — Formal Definition

In the context of food storage engineering, **bulk solids** are defined as a large assembly of discrete particulate solid particles (grains, powders, flours, pellets, granules, etc.) handled and stored in bulk quantities rather than as individual units. The science of how these particles flow, deform, and resist movement when stored in silos, bins, hoppers, and ducts is governed by the **mechanics of bulk solids**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEFTT633 — Module 3):**
> Bulk solids behave neither like a true solid nor a true fluid. They exhibit *time-dependent* and *stress-dependent* flow behaviour controlled by inter-particle forces (friction, cohesion, van der Waals, electrostatics, moisture bridges) and the geometry of the containing vessel.

**Key Engineering Metrics of Bulk Solids (KTU Mandatory Terminology):**

- **Bulk Density (ρ_b):** Mass of particles per unit volume of bulk (including voids). Units: **kg/m³**.
- **Particle Density (ρ_p):** Mass per unit volume of a single solid particle (excluding voids).
- **Porosity (ε):** Fraction of bulk volume occupied by voids/air.
- **Angle of Internal Friction (φᵢ):** Angle characterizing frictional resistance between particles.
- **Angle of Wall Friction (φ_w):** Angle characterizing friction between bulk solid and bin wall.
- **Cohesion (C):** Tensile strength of bulk solid at zero normal stress. Units: **Pa** or **N/m²**.
- **Compressibility Index (CI):** Indicator of flowability based on tapped vs. loose bulk density.
- **Hausner Ratio (HR):** Ratio of tapped density to loose bulk density.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salted Coffee Jar" Intuition

Imagine a large transparent jar full of dry coffee granules with a small hole at the bottom.

- If the granules are **dry, round, and uniform**, they pour out in a smooth, continuous column — this is **mass flow** (like shaking a saltshaker).
- If the granules are **sticky, moist, or cohesive** (think raw sugar clumps or wet flour), they form a stable "arch" or "dome" above the opening, blocking flow — this is **arching/ratholing**, a common silo failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Food Storage Examples:**
> - Wheat in a silo (free-flowing) → *Mass flow desired*
> - Wet corn flour in a hopper (cohesive) → *Funnel flow / arching risks*
> - Sugar, milk powder, rice flour → *Cohesive, need special hopper design*

The same jar behaviour depends on **particle size, shape, moisture, surface roughness, and container geometry** — these are the *physical factors* governing flow.

## 1.3 Physical Factors Influencing Flow Characteristics

The **flow characteristics** of a bulk solid depend on a combination of intrinsic (particle-level) and extrinsic (system-level) factors. KTU 2024 emphasises the following:

| **S.No** | **Factor** | **Influence on Flow** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Particle Size & Size Distribution** | Larger, uniform particles → free-flowing. Fine particles (< 100 µm) → cohesive due to high surface area-to-mass ratio |
| 2 | **Particle Shape** | Spherical → best flow. Flaky, fibrous, needle-like → poor flow (high interlock) |
| 3 | **Moisture Content** | Increases liquid bridges → higher cohesion → poor flow. Critical threshold is **critical moisture content** |
| 4 | **Surface Roughness & Texture** | Rough surfaces → higher inter-particle friction → slower flow but stable arches |
| 5 | **Bulk Density & Porosity** | Higher porosity → more compressible → more cohesive behaviour |
| 6 | **Temperature** | Affects moisture migration, fat melting, caking (chocolate, sugar) |
| 7 | **Fat/Oil Content** | Can form liquid bridges → caking in oilseeds, milk powder |
| 8 | **Storage Time (Time Consolidation)** | Longer storage → more caking under sustained load |
| 9 | **Electrostatic Charges** | Fine powders (cocoa, flour) may agglomerate due to static |
| 10 | **Container Geometry & Material** | Wall friction, hopper angle, opening size |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *“Shape, Size, Surface, Smoothness, Squeeze, Stickiness, Storage, Static, Slopes & Silos”* — the 10 S's of bulk solid flow factors.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance in Food Industry

Understanding these factors is critical for:
- Designing **silos for grain storage** (wheat, rice, corn, soybean)
- Designing **hoppers for discharge** of flour, sugar, milk powder
- Preventing **arching, ratholing, and flooding** in bins
- Selecting **pneumatic conveying duct diameters** to avoid saltation
- Ensuring **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** discharge to prevent spoilage
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mohr-Coulomb Yield Criterion for Bulk Solids

The foundational theory describing the strength of bulk solids is the **Mohr-Coulomb Yield Criterion**. It states that a bulk solid yields (begins to flow plastically) when the **shear stress (τ)** on a failure plane reaches a critical value dependent on the **normal stress (σ)** and the material's frictional and cohesive properties.

**Operational Concept — Step by Step:**

- **Step 1:** When a bulk solid is loaded in a silo, it experiences normal stress (vertical load) and develops shear stress internally.
- **Step 2:** As loading increases, the bulk solid eventually yields along a plane of weakness.
- **Step 3:** The Mohr circle representing the stress state touches the **yield locus** (a straight line in σ–τ space).
- **Step 4:** The yield locus is characterised by two material properties: **cohesion (C)** and **angle of internal friction (φᵢ)**.

**Physical Meaning of the Yield Locus:**
- The **intercept** on the τ-axis = **Cohesion (C)**
- The **slope** of the line = **tan(φᵢ)** where φᵢ is the **angle of internal friction**
- It separates "no flow" (below the line) from "flow" (above the line) regions.

## 2.2 Effective Angle of Internal Friction

In practice, the Mohr-Coulomb yield locus is curved, so a secant line is drawn from the origin to a specific Mohr circle's failure point, defining the **effective angle of internal friction (δ)**:

$$\delta = \arctan\left(\frac{\tau}{\sigma}\right)$$

This single parameter is widely used in hopper design (e.g., Jenike's hopper design charts).

## 2.3 The Jenike Shear Cell Concept

The standard laboratory test to measure flow properties of bulk solids is the **Jenike Shear Cell Test**, which determines:
- **Cohesion (C)**
- **Angle of internal friction (φᵢ)**
- **Effective angle of internal friction (δ)**
- **Angle of wall friction (φ_w)**
- **Unconfined Yield Strength (σ_y)** — the compressive strength of bulk solid under no lateral confinement.

## 2.4 Flow Function (FF) and Flowability Classification

The **Flow Function (FF)** is a plot of **Unconfined Yield Strength (σ_y)** vs. **Major Consolidating Stress (σ₁)**. It classifies materials as:

| **Class** | **FF Range** | **Flowability** | **Typical Food Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | FF < 1 | Not flowing (cohesive) | Wet flour, caked sugar |
| II | 1 < FF < 2 | Very cohesive | Cocoa powder, milk powder |
| III | 2 < FF < 4 | Cohesive | Icing sugar, fine salt |
| IV | 4 < FF < 10 | Easy-flowing | Granulated sugar, salt |
| V | 10 < FF | Free-flowing | Dry wheat, rice, corn |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The steeper the flow function (higher FF), the better the flow. Cohesive materials (Class I/II) require specialised hopper design (e.g., vibrators, aeration, steep conical hoppers).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Equation** | **Description / Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Bulk Density** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_{bulk}}$ | Mass per unit total bulk volume (incl. voids) |
| **Porosity** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$ | Fraction of void space in bulk |
| **Compressibility Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_{tapped} - \rho_{loose}}{\rho_{tapped}} \times 100$ | Carr's index; flowability indicator |
| **Hausner Ratio (HR)** | $HR = \dfrac{\rho_{tapped}}{\rho_{loose}}$ | HR < 1.25 → free-flowing; HR > 1.5 → cohesive |
| **Mohr-Coulomb Yield** | $\tau = C + \sigma \tan(\phi_i)$ | Shear stress at failure on a plane |
| **Effective Friction Angle** | $\delta = \arctan(\tau / \sigma)$ | Used in Jenike hopper design |
| **Janssen Vertical Stress (in silo)** | $\sigma_v = \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)\right]$ | Stress at depth H in cylindrical silo |
| **Janssen Switch Stress (σ_crit)** | $\sigma_{crit} = \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot g \cdot D}{4 \mu K}$ | Asymptotic maximum vertical stress |
| **Beverloo's Equation (discharge from orifice)** | $W = C \cdot \rho_b \cdot \sqrt{g} \cdot (D_o - k d_p)^{2.5}$ | Mass flow rate through circular opening |
| **Hopper Half-Angle (mass flow, conical)** | $\theta_c \leq \phi_{crit}$ | Critical cone angle for mass flow |
| **Critical Arching Diameter** | $D_{arch} = \dfrac{2 \cdot C \cdot g_c}{\rho_b \cdot g}$ | Min. opening to prevent stable arch |

> Where: $D$ = silo diameter, $H$ = depth of fill, $\mu$ = wall friction coefficient, $K$ = lateral pressure ratio, $D_o$ = orifice diameter, $d_p$ = particle diameter, $k$ ≈ 1.5 (empirical constant), $C$ = Beverloo constant (≈ 0.58 for circular openings).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Janssen's Equation for Vertical Stress in a Silo

**Background (KTU Board Favourite):**
When granular material is filled into a deep cylindrical silo, the **vertical stress at the bottom does NOT increase linearly with height** (unlike a liquid). It asymptotically approaches a constant value. This is described by **Janssen's Equation (1895)**.

**Assumptions:**
1. Vertical slices of bulk solid move downward as a rigid plug.
2. Wall friction fully mobilised (Janssen's fully developed flow assumption).
3. Horizontal stress is a constant fraction $K$ of vertical stress (Rankine-type assumption).
4. Silo is cylindrical with diameter $D$, filled to height $H$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Consider a thin horizontal slice of thickness $dH$ at depth $H$ in a cylindrical silo of diameter $D$ and cross-sectional area $A = \pi D^2 / 4$.

The forces acting on this slice (per unit cross-section) are:

- **Weight of slice:** $\rho_b \, g \, A \, dH$ (downward)
- **Vertical stress on top:** $\sigma_v \, A$ (downward, acting on slice from above)
- **Vertical stress on bottom:** $(\sigma_v + d\sigma_v) \, A$ (upward)
- **Friction on side wall:** $\tau_w \, (\pi D \, dH)$ (upward, opposing motion)

> The wall shear stress is related to wall friction coefficient $\mu$ and horizontal stress $\sigma_h$ by:
> $\tau_w = \mu \, \sigma_h = \mu \, K \, \sigma_v$

**Equilibrium of the slice (force balance, downward positive):**

The weight and top stress push down; bottom stress and wall friction push up.

$$
(\rho_b \, g \, A \, dH) + \sigma_v \, A = (\sigma_v + d\sigma_v) \, A + \mu \, K \, \sigma_v \, (\pi D \, dH)
$$

**Step 1:** Simplify the balance equation.

$$
A \, d\sigma_v = \rho_b \, g \, A \, dH - \mu \, K \, \sigma_v \, (\pi D) \, dH
$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $A = \pi D^2 / 4$.

$$
d\sigma_v = \rho_b \, g \, dH - \dfrac{4 \mu K \sigma_v}{D} \, dH
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to form a first-order linear ODE.

$$
\dfrac{d\sigma_v}{dH} + \dfrac{4 \mu K}{D} \, \sigma_v = \rho_b \, g
$$

**Step 4:** Solve using integrating factor $I = \exp\left(\dfrac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)$.

Multiply both sides by $I$:

$$
\dfrac{d}{dH}\left[\sigma_v \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)\right] = \rho_b \, g \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Integrate both sides with respect to $H$ (assuming $\sigma_v = 0$ at $H = 0$, i.e., top surface).

$$
\sigma_v(H) = \dfrac{\rho_b \, g \, D}{4 \mu K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Janssen's Equation (KTU Board Final Form):**
> $$\sigma_v = \dfrac{\rho_b \, g \, D}{4 \mu K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)\right]$$

**Step 6:** Identify the asymptotic (maximum) "switch" stress.

As $H \to \infty$, the exponential term vanishes, and the vertical stress saturates at:

$$
\sigma_{crit} = \dfrac{\rho_b \, g \, D}{4 \mu K}
$$

This is the famous **Janssen switch stress** — beyond this, adding more material does **not** increase the floor load. This is critical for silo structural design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beverloo's Equation for Gravity Discharge Through an Orifice

**Background (KTU Board Favourite):**
Beverloo et al. (1961) empirically developed the most widely used equation for **mass discharge rate of granular material through a circular opening** at the bottom of a hopper.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, gravity-driven discharge.
2. Opening diameter $D_o$ is much larger than the mean particle diameter $d_p$ (typically $D_o > 6 d_p$).
3. The "empty annulus" effect: particles near the wall of the orifice don't move, so the effective opening is reduced by a factor of $k d_p$ (typically $k \approx 1.5$).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For a fluid, Torricelli's law gives the velocity at depth $h$ as $v = \sqrt{2gh}$, and the volumetric flow rate through an area $A_o$ is:

$$
Q_{fluid} = A_o \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

**Step 2:** For granular materials, Beverloo modified this to account for two effects:
- A **discharge coefficient** $C$ (replaces $\sqrt{2}$ with an empirical value, typically $C \approx 0.58$ for circular openings).
- An **effective diameter** $(D_o - k d_p)$ to account for the stagnant "empty annulus" of width $k d_p/2$ at the orifice wall.

**Step 3:** The volumetric flow rate becomes:

$$
Q = C \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot D_o}
$$

Where the effective area for a circular opening is:

$$
A_{eff} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o - k d_p)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Convert volumetric to mass flow rate by multiplying by bulk density $\rho_b$:

$$
W = \rho_b \cdot Q = C \cdot \rho_b \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D_o - k d_p)^2 \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot D_o}
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to the standard form (combining constants):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Beverloo's Equation (KTU Board Form):**
> $$W = C \, \rho_b \, \sqrt{g} \, (D_o - k d_p)^{2.5}$$

Where:
- $W$ = mass discharge rate (kg/s)
- $C$ ≈ 0.58 for circular openings (≈ 0.64 for slot openings)
- $D_o$ = orifice diameter (m)
- $d_p$ = mean particle diameter (m)
- $k$ ≈ 1.5 (empty annulus factor)
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Wheat (ρ_b = 750 kg/m³, d_p = 3.5 mm) is discharged from a hopper with a circular orifice of diameter D_o = 150 mm. Calculate the mass flow rate using Beverloo's equation. (Take C = 0.58, k = 1.5.)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the effective diameter.

$$
D_{eff} = D_o - k d_p = 0.150 - 1.5 \times 0.0035 = 0.150 - 0.00525 = 0.14475 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $(D_{eff})^{2.5}$.

$$
D_{eff}^{2.5} = (0.14475)^{2.5}
$$

Intermediate: $0.14475^2 = 0.020952$, then $0.020952 \times \sqrt{0.14475} = 0.020952 \times 0.38046 = 0.007972$ m^(2.5).

**Step 3:** Calculate $\sqrt{g}$.

$$
\sqrt{g} = \sqrt{9.81} = 3.132 \text{ m}^{0.5}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Beverloo's equation.

$$
W = 0.58 \times 750 \times 3.132 \times 0.007972
$$

**Step 5:** Sequential multiplication.

$$
0.58 \times 750 = 435
$$

$$
435 \times 3.132 = 1362.42
$$

$$
1362.42 \times 0.007972 = 10.86 \text{ kg/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** W ≈ **10.86 kg/s** (or 39.1 tonnes/hour). This is a typical KTU Part B numerical.

## 3.4 Critical Arching Diameter — Derivation

A **stable arch** forms over a circular opening if the unconfined yield strength of the bulk solid exceeds the gravitational stress that tends to collapse the arch.

Force balance on a hemispherical arch of diameter $D$ and bulk density $\rho_b$:

- **Compressive (holding) force on arch base:** $\sigma_y \cdot A = C \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- **Gravitational (collapsing) force on arch:** $\rho_b \cdot g \cdot V = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^3}{12}$

> Setting these equal at the **critical arching condition**:
> $$C \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_{crit}^2}{4} = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_{crit}^3}{12}$$

Simplify:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Arching Diameter:**
> $$D_{crit} = \dfrac{3 C}{\rho_b \, g}$$

> The minimum opening diameter $D_o$ must be **larger** than $D_{crit}$ to prevent arching.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Some textbooks use $D_{crit} = 2C/(\rho_b g)$ (for 2D slot analysis). KTU 2024 expects the **3C/(ρ_b·g)** form for circular openings unless otherwise stated. Always verify the geometry.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mass Flow vs. Funnel Flow

| **Parameter** | **Mass Flow (Ideal)** | **Funnel Flow (Common Fault)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Flow Pattern** | All material moves downward uniformly | Central core moves; stagnant zones at walls |
| **Discharge Sequence** | FIFO (First-In-First-Out) | LIFO in stagnant zone (Last-In-First-Out) |
| **Residence Time** | Uniform | Long in stagnant zones → spoilage risk |
| **Arching Risk** | Low (if designed properly) | High |
| **Segregation** | Minimal | High (fines concentrate in stagnant zones) |
| **Hopper Angle** | Steep (≤ φ_crit) | Shallow |
| **Wall Friction** | Low (smooth walls) | High |
| **KTU Term to Remember** | **"Mass flow = uniform velocity field"** | **"Funnel flow = pipe flow inside stagnant annulus"** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Bulk Solid Flow Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bulk Solid in Storage] --> B{Is material cohesive?}
    B -- No --> C[Free-Flowing Solid]
    B -- Yes --> D[Cohesive Solid]
    C --> E[Hopper Design: Shallow cone OK]
    D --> F[Hopper Design: Steep cone required]
    E --> G[Check Arching Risk]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Do particles arch over opening?}
    H -- No --> I[Successful Mass Flow]
    H -- Yes --> J[Apply Solutions]
    J --> K1[Vibrator on hopper wall]
    J --> K2[Aeration / Fluidisation]
    J --> K3[Steepen cone angle]
    J --> K4[Use hopper insert / liner]
    K1 --> L[Verify with Jenike test]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> M[Final Design Validated]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanics of Bulk Solids in a Silo

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Particle Properties] --> B[Size, Shape, Density, Moisture]
    B --> C[Bulk Properties]
    C --> D[Phi-i: Internal Friction]
    C --> E[Cohesion C]
    C --> F[Wall Friction Phi-w]
    C --> G[Flow Function FF]
    D --> H[Jenike Analysis]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Silhouette Check}
    I -- Pass --> J[Stable Mass Flow Design]
    I -- Fail --> K[Redesign Hopper or Add Aids]
    A1[Silo Geometry] --> H
    A2[Hopper Angle] --> H
    A3[Opening Size] --> H
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Janssen Stress Build-up in Silo

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Surface H=0] -->|Sigma-v approx 0| B[Shallow Depth H small]
    B -->|Exponential rise| C[Mid-Height H moderate]
    C -->|Approaching asymptote| D[Deep Depth H large]
    D -->|Sigma-v approx Sigma-crit| E[Switch Stress Saturation]
    E --> F[Further addition does NOT increase floor load]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Flow Through Orifice (Beverloo Mechanism)

```mermaid
    A[Granular Material in Hopper] --> B[Gravity-Driven Discharge]
    B --> C[Funnel Zone Formation]
    C --> D[Empty Annulus at Orifice Wall]
    D --> E[Effective Diameter Reduced by k x d-p]
    E --> F[Volumetric Flow Q via Beverloo]
    F --> G[Mass Flow W = Rho-b x Q]
    G --> H[Steady Discharge Output]
    style D fill:#ffe1e1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.5 Mermaid — Classification of Flow Regimes in Hoppers

```mermaid

    A[Bulk Solid in Conical Hopper] --> B{Hopper half-angle}
    B -- Less than Phi-crit --> C[Mass Flow Regime]
    B -- Greater than Phi-crit --> D[Funnel Flow Regime]
    C --> E[All material moves at once]
    D --> F[Stagnant zones at walls]
    F --> G[Arching risk high]
    E --> H[Stable, predictable discharge]
    G --> I[Spoilage, segregation risk]
    style C fill:#d4f4dd
    style D fill:#ffd4d4
```

```mermaid
    B --> C[Orifice / Discharge Valve]
    C --> D[Gravity Flow Duct]

    E --> F[Process or Packaging Unit]
    B --> G[Vibrator Aerator]
    G --> B
    D --> H[Duct Diameter Check]
    H --> I{Avoid Saltation?}

```
