<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**Define the term "Bulk Density" of a granular food material. How does it differ from particle density? What is the significance of porosity in bulk solid flow?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Bulk Density (ρ_b):** The mass of granular material per unit total volume it occupies, including the void spaces between particles. Unit: **kg/m³**.
- **Particle Density (ρ_p):** Mass of a single solid particle divided by its own volume (excluding voids). Always **higher** than bulk density.
- **Porosity (ε):** The fraction of bulk volume that is void space. Higher porosity implies a more compressible and often more cohesive bulk solid, which **impairs flow** because the air-filled voids allow more inter-particle contact rearrangement under load.
- **Significance:** Materials with high porosity (e.g., puffed cereals, fluffs) are more compressible and prone to caking, while low-porosity grains (rice, wheat) flow easily.

*(Examiner Note: 1 mark for each sub-part — definition, distinction, significance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Distinguish between "Mass Flow" and "Funnel Flow" in a hopper. Mention one advantage of each in food storage applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Mass Flow** | **Funnel Flow** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | All material in the hopper moves downward at discharge | Only the central core moves; material near walls remains stagnant |
| **Discharge Order** | FIFO (First-In-First-Out) | LIFO inside stagnant zones |
| **Advantage** | Prevents spoilage of old stock, uniform discharge | Easier to construct (less steep hopper required) |

- **Mass flow advantage:** Suitable for perishable foods (grains, flours) where **uniform residence time** prevents mould growth and rancidity.
- **Funnel flow advantage:** Suitable for **non-perishable, free-flowing** materials (e.g., dry sand, plastic pellets); cheaper construction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Derive Janssen's equation for vertical stress at the bottom of a cylindrical silo filled with a granular food material. State the assumptions clearly. What is "Janssen's switch stress"?**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**
1. The bulk solid moves as a rigid plug during discharge (no internal shear).
2. Wall friction is fully mobilised along the silo wall.
3. The horizontal stress is a constant fraction $K$ of the vertical stress.
4. The silo is vertical, cylindrical, and the material is homogeneous.

**Derivation (5 Marks):** (Use the step-by-step derivation from Section 3.1 of these notes.)

**Key Steps:**

- Force balance on a thin horizontal slice of thickness $dH$ in a silo of diameter $D$ and area $A = \pi D^2 / 4$.
- Wall friction: $\tau_w \pi D \, dH = \mu K \sigma_v \pi D \, dH$
- Equilibrium: $\rho_b g A dH + \sigma_v A = (\sigma_v + d\sigma_v) A + \mu K \sigma_v \pi D dH$
- Simplify and solve the linear ODE with boundary condition $\sigma_v(0) = 0$:

$$
\sigma_v = \dfrac{\rho_b g D}{4 \mu K}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{4 \mu K H}{D}\right)\right]
$$

**Janssen's Switch Stress (Included in the 5 derivation marks):**

$$
\sigma_{crit} = \dfrac{\rho_b g D}{4 \mu K}
$$

This is the asymptotic maximum vertical stress at the silo floor. Beyond a fill height of about $H \approx D$, the stress saturates — additional material does **not** increase the floor load. This is critical for designing silo foundations.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**A cylindrical wheat silo of diameter 6 m is filled to a height of 20 m. The bulk density of wheat is 780 kg/m³, wall friction coefficient μ = 0.35, and lateral pressure ratio K = 0.5. Calculate the vertical stress at the bottom using Janssen's equation and compare with the hydrostatic (liquid) stress. Take g = 9.81 m/s².**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the hydrostatic (fluid-like) stress for comparison.

$$
\sigma_{hydro} = \rho_b g H = 780 \times 9.81 \times 20 = 153{,}036 \text{ Pa} \approx 153.0 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the exponent term.

$$
\dfrac{4 \mu K H}{D} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.35 \times 0.5 \times 20}{6} = \dfrac{14}{6} = 2.333
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\exp(-2.333)$.

$$
\exp(-2.333) \approx 0.0967
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the switch stress.

$$
\sigma_{crit} = \dfrac{780 \times 9.81 \times 6}{4 \times 0.35 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{45{,}910.8}{0.7} = 65{,}587 \text{ Pa} \approx 65.59 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply Janssen's equation.

$$
\sigma_v = 65{,}587 \times (1 - 0.0967) = 65{,}587 \times 0.9033 = 59{,}250 \text{ Pa} \approx 59.25 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare.

| **Stress Type** | **Value (kPa)** |
|---|---|
| Hydrostatic (liquid-like) | 153.04 |
| Janssen (granular) | 59.25 |

> The Janssen stress is **about 61% lower** than the hydrostatic estimate. This shows that the silo wall carries a significant share of the load via friction. Using hydrostatic assumptions in silo design would be unsafe structurally (would over-design the floor and under-design the wall).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Choice]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**State and explain the Mohr-Coulomb yield criterion for bulk solids. Sketch the yield locus and identify the key parameters. How is this criterion used in Jenike's hopper design method?**

**Model Solution:**

**Mohr-Coulomb Yield Criterion (3 Marks):**

A bulk solid yields (begins to flow) along a plane when the **shear stress τ** on that plane equals:

$$
\tau = C + \sigma \tan(\phi_i)
$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress at failure (Pa)
- $C$ = cohesion of bulk solid (Pa)
- $\sigma$ = normal stress on the failure plane (Pa)
- $\phi_i$ = angle of internal friction (degrees)

**Sketch Description (2 Marks):**

- The **yield locus** is a straight line on a σ–τ graph.
- The **slope** of the line = $\tan(\phi_i)$.
- The **τ-axis intercept** = $C$ (cohesion).
- A **Mohr circle** representing the stress state touches this line at failure.

**Application in Jenike's Method (2 Marks):**

Jenike's method uses the Mohr-Coulomb parameters to determine:
- The **critical hopper half-angle (θ_c)** for mass flow.
- The **minimum outlet diameter (D_min)** to prevent arching and ratholing.
- The relationship $\theta_c \leq \phi_{crit}$ where $\phi_{crit}$ is read from Jenike's design charts as a function of $\phi_i$, $\phi_w$, and δ.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**A hopper discharges corn flour (ρ_b = 600 kg/m³, d_p = 0.5 mm) through a circular orifice of diameter 80 mm. Using Beverloo's equation, calculate the mass discharge rate. Take C = 0.58 and k = 1.5. Comment on the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Effective diameter.

$$
D_{eff} = D_o - k d_p = 0.080 - 1.5 \times 0.0005 = 0.080 - 0.00075 = 0.07925 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $D_{eff}^{2.5}$.

$$
D_{eff}^{2.5} = (0.07925)^{2.5}
$$

- $D_{eff}^2 = 0.006281$ m²
- $\sqrt{D_{eff}} = \sqrt{0.07925} = 0.2815$ m^0.5
- $D_{eff}^{2.5} = 0.006281 \times 0.2815 = 0.001768$ m^2.5

**Step 3:** $\sqrt{g} = \sqrt{9.81} = 3.132$ m^0.5/s.

**Step 4:** Apply Beverloo's equation.

$$
W = 0.58 \times 600 \times 3.132 \times 0.001768
$$

- $0.58 \times 600 = 348$
- $348 \times 3.132 = 1090.0$
- $1090.0 \times 0.001768 = 1.927 \text{ kg/s}$

**Result:** $W \approx 1.93 \text{ kg/s}$ (about 6.94 tonnes/hour).

**Comment (1 Mark):**

This is a **moderate discharge rate** suitable for small to medium flour mills. The relatively small orifice (80 mm) is appropriate for corn flour, which is mildly cohesive. If finer particles or cohesive powders (cocoa, milk powder) were used, the effective diameter reduction $k d_p$ would be more significant, and arching would become a concern — requiring a larger orifice or hopper aids.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing bulk density with particle density** (3-mark questions).
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the boundary condition** $\sigma_v(0)=0$ when deriving Janssen's equation — leads to a wrong constant of integration.
> 3. **Mixing up the critical arching formula** between circular ($3C/\rho_b g$) and slot geometries ($2C/\rho_b g$).
> 4. **Using $k = 1$ instead of $k = 1.5$** in Beverloo's equation (loses 1 mark per question).
> 5. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** in derivations (KTU awards 1–2 marks for this).
> 6. **Forgetting to subtract $k d_p$ from $D_o$** — Beverloo's equation with $(D_o)^{2.5}$ alone is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 3 Rapid Recap:**

- ✅ **Bulk Density (ρ_b):** Includes voids; Particle Density (ρ_p): excludes voids. Porosity $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_p$.
- ✅ **Mohr-Coulomb:** $\tau = C + \sigma \tan(\phi_i)$ — fundamental yield criterion.
- ✅ **Jenike Shear Test** gives $C$, $\phi_i$, $\delta$, $\phi_w$, $\sigma_y$.
- ✅ **Flow Function (FF)** classifies flowability: Class I (worst) to Class V (best).
- ✅ **Janssen's Equation:** $\sigma_v = (\rho_b g D / 4\mu K)[1 - \exp(-4\mu K H / D)]$ — does NOT grow linearly with $H$.
- ✅ **Switch Stress:** $\sigma_{crit} = \rho_b g D / 4\mu K$ — asymptotic maximum floor stress.
- ✅ **Beverloo's Equation:** $W = C \rho_b \sqrt{g} (D_o - k d_p)^{2.5}$ — gravity discharge from orifice.
- ✅ **Critical Arching Diameter:** $D_{crit} = 3C/(\rho_b g)$ for circular openings.
- ✅ **Mass Flow** = FIFO, uniform discharge; **Funnel Flow** = stagnant zones, spoilage risk.
- ✅ **Physical factors** affecting flow: Size, Shape, Surface, Moisture, Density, Time, Electrostatics, Temperature, Fats, Geometry.
- ✅ **Compressibility Index (CI)** and **Hausner Ratio (HR)** are quick lab indicators of flowability.
- ✅ **Typical KTU numericals** ask for: (1) Janssen stress at given H, (2) Beverloo discharge rate, (3) Critical opening for arching prevention.
- ✅ **Always include assumptions** in derivations — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests this under Course Outcome CO3.
- ✅ **Empty annulus correction** ($k d_p$) in Beverloo's equation is a frequent sub-part.
- ✅ **Hopper half-angle** for mass flow must satisfy $\theta_c \leq \phi_{crit}$ (read from Jenike's design charts).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — PEFTT633 Food Storage Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Density, True Density, Apparent Density, Angle of Repose, Angle of Rupture, Plane of Rupture, Internal and External Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bulk Density, True Density, Apparent Density, Angle of Repose, Angle of Rupture, Plane of Rupture, Internal and External Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Bulk Density ($\rho_b$)

**Formal Definition:** Bulk density of a granular food material is defined as the **mass of the particles divided by the total volume they occupy, including the voids (inter-particle spaces) between them**. It is a critical design parameter in silos, bins, hoppers, and packaging systems.

$$\rho_b = \frac{M_{sample}}{V_{bulk}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk density is a *bulk-state* property, NOT a particle property. It changes with compaction, moisture content, particle size distribution, and consolidation pressure.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a glass jar filled with marbles — the weight of marbles divided by the *entire jar volume* (marbles + air gaps) is the bulk density. If you shake the jar (compaction), the bulk density increases because the air gaps shrink.

**Typical Units:** $\text{kg/m}^3$, $\text{g/cm}^3$, or $\text{lb/ft}^3$.

---

### 1.2 True Density / Particle Density ($\rho_t$ or $\rho_p$)

**Formal Definition:** True density is the **mass of the solid material divided by the volume of the solid itself, excluding all pores (both internal pores and inter-particle voids)**. It is determined using a gas (helium) or liquid (pycnometer) displacement method that penetrates the pores.

$$\rho_t = \frac{M_{dry\,solid}}{V_{true\,solid}}$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** If you could magically fuse all the marbles in the jar into a single solid sphere with no internal gaps — the weight of the original marbles divided by the volume of this hypothetical solid sphere is the true density. The "true" volume excludes everything that is not actual matter.

---

### 1.3 Apparent Density ($\rho_a$)

**Formal Definition:** Apparent density is the **mass of the particle divided by its envelope volume (including sealed internal pores but excluding inter-particle voids)**. It is measured by mercury displacement or coating methods where mercury does not penetrate the pores.

$$\rho_a = \frac{M_{particle}}{V_{apparent}} = \frac{M_{particle}}{V_{solid} + V_{sealed\,pores}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** $\rho_t > \rho_a > \rho_b$ always holds true for porous granular foods like wheat, rice, corn, and seeds.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A piece of bread — the true density is just the dry matter volume, the apparent density includes the tiny closed air pockets inside, and the bulk density includes the big gaps between bread slices in a basket.

---

### 1.4 Angle of Repose ($\theta_r$)

**Formal Definition:** The angle of repose is the **steepest angle (measured from the horizontal) at which a heap of granular material remains stable under gravity without slumping**. It represents the equilibrium between gravitational and inter-particle frictional forces.

$$\tan(\theta_r) = \frac{2h}{d}$$

where $h$ is the heap height and $d$ is the base diameter.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Pour dry sand on a flat table — it forms a cone. The slope of the cone's side from horizontal is the angle of repose. Wet sand forms a steeper cone (higher angle of repose) because moisture adds cohesion.

---

### 1.5 Angle of Rupture / Angle of Internal Friction ($\phi$)

**Formal Definition:** The angle of internal friction is the **angle made by the resultant of normal and tangential forces on the failure (rupture) plane with the normal to that plane** when the material is on the verge of failure. It is a fundamental Mohr-Coulomb strength parameter.

$$\tau = c + \sigma \tan(\phi)$$

The angle of rupture itself is often defined as the angle of the failure plane with the major principal plane:

$$\theta_{failure} = 45° + \frac{\phi}{2} \quad \text{(active state)}$$

---

### 1.6 Plane of Rupture

**Formal Definition:** The plane of rupture is the **specific surface within a bulk solid along which shear failure (sliding) occurs** when the material is stressed beyond its shear strength. It is oriented at $(45° + \phi/2)$ to the major principal stress direction in active failure.

**Intuitive Analogy:** When a sandcastle wall collapses, the failure doesn't happen at a random angle — it slides along a specific inclined surface. That surface is the plane of rupture.

---

### 1.7 Internal Friction (Cohesionless Internal Shear)

**Formal Definition:** Internal friction is the **resistance to relative sliding between particles within the bulk mass itself**. It depends on surface roughness, particle shape, particle size distribution, and normal stress on the contact plane.

$$\tau_i = \sigma_n \tan(\phi_i)$$

where $\phi_i$ is the angle of internal friction.

---

### 1.8 External Friction (Wall Friction)

**Formal Definition:** External friction is the **resistance to sliding between the bulk granular material and the wall (or surface) of the containing structure** (silo wall, hopper wall, conveyor belt, chute).

$$\tau_w = \sigma_n \tan(\phi_w)$$

where $\phi_w$ is the angle of wall friction, and $\sigma_n$ is the normal pressure on the wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silo Design Relevance:** Janssen's equation uses the **ratio $\mu_w K$** (where $\mu_w = \tan\phi_w$ and $K$ is the lateral pressure ratio) to predict vertical and horizontal pressures in deep bins. This is the most tested concept in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Densities

The three densities represent **three nested volume definitions**, each progressively larger:

| Density Type | Volume Considered | Excludes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **True Density** $\rho_t$ | Solid matter only | All pores + all voids |
| **Apparent Density** $\rho_a$ | Solid + closed pores | Open pores + voids |
| **Bulk Density** $\rho_b$ | Solid + closed pores + open pores + voids | Nothing (entire bulk volume) |

This hierarchy leads to the fundamental inequality:

$$\rho_t \geq \rho_a \geq \rho_b$$

### 2.2 Porosity and Voidage Relationships

**Porosity** ($\varepsilon$) is the fraction of bulk volume that is void space:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$$

where $\rho_p$ is the particle (apparent) density. The **void ratio** is:

$$e = \frac{\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon} = \frac{\rho_p - \rho_b}{\rho_b}$$

**Compaction effect on bulk density** (Walker / Kelvin model):

$$\rho_{b,compacted} = \rho_{b,0} \cdot n^{-0.005\,k}$$

where $n$ is the number of taps and $k$ is a material constant. This is essential in packaging calculations.

### 2.3 Mohr-Coulomb Failure Theory (Foundation of Rupture Concepts)

The shear strength of a bulk solid is given by the Mohr-Coulomb criterion:

$$\tau = c + \sigma_n \tan(\phi)$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress on the failure plane
- $c$ = cohesion (intercept)
- $\sigma_n$ = normal stress on the failure plane
- $\phi$ = angle of internal friction (slope of Mohr-Coulomb envelope)

When represented on a Mohr circle, the failure plane is tangent to the Mohr-Coulomb envelope. The geometry yields the **angle of rupture (failure plane angle)**:

$$\theta_{rupture} = 45° + \frac{\phi}{2} \quad \text{(active failure)}$$

$$\theta_{rupture} = 45° - \frac{\phi}{2} \quad \text{(passive failure)}$$

### 2.4 Active and Passive States of Stress in Silos

When a granular material is confined and the wall moves:

- **Active state:** Wall moves *away* from material (e.g., during discharge). Minor principal stress is vertical, and the lateral stress is the minor one:
$$K_a = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

- **Passive state:** Wall is pushed *into* the material (e.g., during filling expansion):
$$K_p = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1+\sin\phi}{1-\sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rankine's Earth Pressure Analogy:** The above equations are Rankine's active and passive earth pressure coefficients, directly applied to granular food materials.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\rho_b = M/V_{bulk}$ | Bulk density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | $\rho_t = M/V_{solid}$ | True density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 3 | $\rho_a = M/(V_{solid}+V_{pores})$ | Apparent density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_p$ | Porosity | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tan\theta_r = 2h/d$ | Angle of repose | degrees |
| 6 | $\tau = c + \sigma_n\tan\phi$ | Mohr-Coulomb failure | Pa |
| 7 | $\theta_{failure} = 45° + \phi/2$ | Failure plane angle (active) | degrees |
| 8 | $K_a = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$ | Active pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $K_p = (1+\sin\phi)/(1-\sin\phi)$ | Passive pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\sigma_v = (\gamma R/\mu_w K)(1-e^{-\mu_w K z/R})$ | Janssen vertical stress | Pa |
| 11 | $\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$ | Lateral wall stress | Pa |
| 12 | $\tau_w = \mu_w \sigma_n$ | Wall friction stress | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These relationships govern the design of grain silos, hopper angles, feed chutes, bucket elevators, and screw conveyors. Misjudging $\phi$ or $\phi_w$ by even $5°$ can double the wall loads — making this one of the most safety-critical topics in food storage engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Angle of Rupture from Mohr-Coulomb Geometry

**Given:** A Mohr circle representing the stress state at failure, with major principal stress $\sigma_1$, minor principal stress $\sigma_3$, and cohesionless Mohr-Coulomb envelope (tangent to circle through origin with slope $\tan\phi$).

**Step 1 — Express the Mohr circle radius and center:**

$$R_{Mohr} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

$$C_{Mohr} = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Condition for tangency (geometric):** The angle subtended at the center between the line to the tangency point and the $\sigma$-axis equals $\phi$. For a cohesionless material ($c = 0$):

$$\sin\phi = \frac{R_{Mohr}}{C_{Mohr}} = \frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2}{(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)/2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the stress ratio at failure:**

$$\sin\phi = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}$$

Rearranging:

$$\sigma_3(\sin\phi + 1) = \sigma_1(1 - \sin\phi)$$

$$\frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_1} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = K_a$$

**Step 4 — Relate failure plane angle to friction angle:** In a Mohr circle, the pole of the failure plane lies on the $\sigma$-axis at $\sigma_1$. The line from the pole to the tangency point makes angle $\phi$ with the $\sigma$-axis. The chord from origin to pole is horizontal, and the normal to the failure plane passes through the center. After angle-chasing geometry on the Mohr circle:

$$\theta_{rupture} = 45° + \frac{\phi}{2}$$

This is the **angle of rupture** measured from the major principal plane (the plane on which $\sigma_1$ acts). It is the angle of inclination of the rupture surface inside the bulk solid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A high internal friction angle $\phi$ leads to a failure plane closer to $90°$ (steep), while a low $\phi$ (e.g., $\phi \to 0$) gives $\theta_{rupture} \to 45°$ (classical Tresca limit).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Janssen's Equation for Vertical Stress in a Silo

**Assumptions:**
- Cylindrical silo of radius $R$
- Horizontal slice of bulk solid of thickness $dz$
- Lateral stress ratio $K = \sigma_h/\sigma_v$ (constant)
- Wall friction coefficient $\mu_w = \tan\phi_w$
- Bulk density $\rho_b$ and acceleration due to gravity $g$

**Step 1 — Force balance on a thin horizontal slice** (forces in vertical direction):

The forces acting on the slice at depth $z$ are:
- Weight of slice (downward): $W = \pi R^2 \rho_b g \, dz$
- Pressure from material above (downward): $\sigma_v \pi R^2$
- Pressure from material below (upward): $(\sigma_v + d\sigma_v)\pi R^2$
- Friction from wall (upward, opposing downward movement): $2\pi R \tau_w dz = 2\pi R \mu_w \sigma_h dz = 2\pi R \mu_w K \sigma_v dz$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium equation:**

$$\sigma_v \pi R^2 + \pi R^2 \rho_b g\,dz = (\sigma_v + d\sigma_v)\pi R^2 + 2\pi R \mu_w K \sigma_v dz$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by dividing by $\pi R^2$:**

$$\sigma_v + \rho_b g\,dz = \sigma_v + d\sigma_v + \frac{2\mu_w K}{R}\sigma_v\,dz$$

The $\sigma_v$ terms cancel:

$$\rho_b g\,dz = d\sigma_v + \frac{2\mu_w K}{R}\sigma_v\,dz$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$d\sigma_v = \rho_b g\,dz - \frac{2\mu_w K}{R}\sigma_v\,dz$$

$$d\sigma_v = \left(\rho_b g - \frac{2\mu_w K}{R}\sigma_v\right)dz$$

$$\frac{d\sigma_v}{(\rho_b g - 2\mu_w K \sigma_v / R)} = dz$$

This is of the form $\frac{du}{a - bu} = dz$. Integrating with boundary condition $\sigma_v(0) = 0$ at the top:

$$-\frac{R}{2\mu_w K}\ln\left(\rho_b g - \frac{2\mu_w K}{R}\sigma_v\right) \bigg|_0^{\sigma_v} = z \bigg|_0^z$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\sigma_v$:**

$$-\frac{R}{2\mu_w K}\left[\ln\left(\rho_b g - \frac{2\mu_w K\sigma_v}{R}\right) - \ln(\rho_b g)\right] = z$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\rho_b g - 2\mu_w K\sigma_v/R}{\rho_b g}\right) = -\frac{2\mu_w K z}{R}$$

$$\frac{\rho_b g - 2\mu_w K\sigma_v/R}{\rho_b g} = e^{-2\mu_w K z/R}$$

$$\sigma_v(z) = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2\mu_w K}\left(1 - e^{-2\mu_w K z/R}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Janssen's Limiting Asymptote:** As $z \to \infty$, $\sigma_v \to \sigma_{v,\infty} = \rho_b g R / (2\mu_w K)$. This means **vertical stress saturates** — adding more depth does NOT increase the bottom pressure indefinitely. This is the most counter-intuitive and frequently tested result in KTU.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Walker Model for Compacted Bulk Density

**Given:** Initial bulk density $\rho_{b,0}$ and the bulk density after $n$ taps in a graduated cylinder.

**Step 1 — Empirical relation (Walker):**

$$\rho_{b,n} = \rho_{b,0} \cdot n^{-0.005\,k}$$

where $k$ is a material-specific constant.

**Step 2 — Solve for $k$ experimentally:**

$$k = \frac{\ln(\rho_{b,n}/\rho_{b,0})}{-0.005 \ln(n)} = -200 \cdot \frac{\ln(\rho_{b,n}/\rho_{b,0})}{\ln(n)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention:** For typical food grains, $k$ ranges from $300$ to $600$ for rice, and $50$ to $150$ for wheat flour, indicating rice compacts more aggressively per tap.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A silo of radius $R = 2\,\text{m}$ stores wheat with bulk density $\rho_b = 780\,\text{kg/m}^3$, lateral pressure ratio $K = 0.5$, and wall friction coefficient $\mu_w = 0.35$. Calculate the vertical stress at depth $z = 8\,\text{m}$ and the asymptotic limit.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{2\mu_w K z}{R} = \frac{2 \times 0.35 \times 0.5 \times 8}{2} = 1.4$$

Step 2 — Compute the saturation stress:

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2\mu_w K} = \frac{780 \times 9.81 \times 2}{2 \times 0.35 \times 0.5} = \frac{15303.6}{0.35} = 43724.6\,\text{Pa}$$

Step 3 — Compute the stress at $z = 8\,\text{m}$:

$$\sigma_v(8) = 43724.6 \times (1 - e^{-1.4}) = 43724.6 \times (1 - 0.2466) = 43724.6 \times 0.7534 = 32940\,\text{Pa}$$

**Verification:** The stress is $75.3\%$ of the asymptote, indicating the silo is approaching but not yet at the saturation regime. If depth were increased to $20\,\text{m}$, the stress would reach $43724.6 \times (1-e^{-3.5}) = 43036\,\text{Pa}$ — almost the limit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Densities — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Mass M of Sample] --> B[Reference Volume V]
    B --> C{Which Volume?}
    C -->|Solid matter only| D[True Density rho_t]
    C -->|Solid + closed pores| E[Apparent Density rho_a]
    C -->|Solid + pores + voids| F[Bulk Density rho_b]
    D --> G[Highest density]
    E --> H[Intermediate density]
    F --> I[Lowest density]
    G --> J[rho_t greater than rho_a]
    H --> K[rho_a greater than rho_b]
    I --> L[Used in silo volume calculations]
```

---

### 4.2 Stress State in a Silo (Mohr-Coulomb Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Granular Material in Silo] --> B[Vertical Stress sigma_v]
    A --> C[Horizontal Stress sigma_h = K sigma_v]
    B --> D[Major Principal Stress sigma_1]
    C --> E[Minor Principal Stress sigma_3]
    D --> F[Mohr Circle Plot]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Mohr Coulomb Envelope with slope tan phi]
    G --> H[Failure Plane Angle theta_r equals 45 plus phi by 2]
    A --> I[Wall Friction mu_w]
    I --> J[Frictional Support on Wall]
    J --> K[Janssen Equation sigma_v = gamma R over mu K times 1 minus exponential term]
```

---

### 4.3 Active vs Passive Failure States in a Granular Mass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Granular Mass under Stress] --> B{Confined Wall Motion}
    B -->|Wall moves away from material| C[Active Failure State]
    B -->|Wall moves into material| D[Passive Failure State]
    C --> E[sigma_1 = sigma_v vertical]
    C --> F[sigma_3 = sigma_h horizontal]
    C --> G[K_a = 1 minus sin phi over 1 plus sin phi]
    C --> H[Failure plane at 45 plus phi by 2]
    D --> I[sigma_1 = sigma_h horizontal]
    D --> J[sigma_3 = sigma_v vertical]
    D --> K[K_p = 1 plus sin phi over 1 minus sin phi]
    D --> L[Failure plane at 45 minus phi by 2]
```

---

### 4.4 Flow and Friction in Bulk Solids — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Bulk Granular Food Material] --> B[Particle Level Properties]
    A --> C[Bulk Level Properties]
    B --> D[True Density]
    B --> E[Apparent Density]
    B --> F[Particle Shape and Size]
    C --> G[Bulk Density]
    C --> H[Angle of Repose]
    C --> I[Angle of Internal Friction phi]
    C --> J[Angle of Wall Friction phi_w]
    G --> K[Silo Volume and Capacity Design]
    H --> K
    I --> L[Failure Analysis in Storage]
    J --> M[Hopper and Chute Design]
    L --> N[Mohr Coulomb Criterion]
    M --> O[Janssen Equation]
    N --> P[Active or Passive Stress State]
    O --> Q[Vertical and Lateral Wall Loads]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **bulk density** and **true density** of a granular food material. Why is bulk density preferred for silo capacity calculations?

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Bulk Density | True Density |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Mass per unit total bulk volume (including voids) | Mass per unit volume of solid matter only |
| Includes voids | Yes (inter-particle voids) | No |
| Measurement | Simple weight/volume of filled container | Gas or liquid pycnometer |
| Typical value for wheat | $\sim 780\,\text{kg/m}^3$ | $\sim 1450\,\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Use | Silo capacity, packaging | Porosity, drying analysis |

Bulk density is preferred for silo capacity because the silo must hold the *bulk* of the material, including all voids — the actual storage volume corresponds to the bulk volume, not the solid volume.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **angle of internal friction** and **angle of wall friction**. How do they differ in their role in silo design?

**Model Answer:**

- **Angle of Internal Friction ($\phi$):** The angle characterizing the resistance to sliding *between particles within the bulk mass*. Governs the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion and determines the orientation of the rupture plane.

- **Angle of Wall Friction ($\phi_w$):** The angle characterizing the resistance to sliding *between the bulk material and the silo wall surface*. Governs the magnitude of frictional support provided by the wall.

**Role in silo design:** $\phi$ controls *internal* shear failure and bulk flow patterns (mass flow vs. funnel flow), while $\phi_w$ controls *vertical load transfer* from the bulk to the wall via Janssen's equation. A high $\phi_w$ means more load is carried by the wall (reducing bottom pressure), while a low $\phi_w$ concentrates load on the silo floor.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **angle of rupture** for a cohesionless granular material using the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion. State the assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical silo of radius $R = 3\,\text{m}$ stores maize with bulk density $\rho_b = 750\,\text{kg/m}^3$, lateral pressure ratio $K = 0.45$, and wall friction coefficient $\mu_w = 0.40$. Using **Janssen's equation**, compute:
(i) the asymptotic vertical stress, and
(ii) the vertical stress at depth $z = 10\,\text{m}$.
Comment on whether the silo has reached the saturation regime.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is cohesionless ($c = 0$).
2. Failure occurs along a well-defined plane.
3. Mohr-Coulomb criterion $\tau = \sigma_n \tan\phi$ applies.
4. Stress state is plane strain / axisymmetric.

**Derivation:**

The Mohr circle has center $C = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)/2$ on the $\sigma$-axis and radius $R_{Mohr} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)/2$.

For tangency with the failure envelope passing through origin with slope $\tan\phi$:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{R_{Mohr}}{C} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}$$

This gives the stress ratio:

$$\frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_1} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

In the active state, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v$ (vertical) and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_h$ (horizontal). On the Mohr circle, the pole of the failure plane is at $\sigma_1$ on the $\sigma$-axis. The line from the pole to the tangency point makes angle $\phi$ with the $\sigma$-axis. The normal to the failure plane (at the pole) is at angle $(2\theta_{rupture})$ from the $\sigma$-axis, where $\theta_{rupture}$ is the failure plane angle measured from the major principal plane. Angle-chasing gives:

$$2\theta_{rupture} = 90° + \phi$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{rupture} = 45° + \frac{\phi}{2}}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $R = 3\,\text{m}$, $\rho_b = 750\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $K = 0.45$, $\mu_w = 0.40$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

**(i) Asymptotic vertical stress:**

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\rho_b g R}{2\mu_w K} = \frac{750 \times 9.81 \times 3}{2 \times 0.40 \times 0.45}$$

Computing numerator: $750 \times 9.81 = 7357.5$; $\times 3 = 22072.5$.

Computing denominator: $2 \times 0.40 \times 0.45 = 0.36$.

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{22072.5}{0.36} = 61312.5\,\text{Pa} \approx 61.3\,\text{kPa}$$

**(ii) Vertical stress at $z = 10\,\text{m}$:**

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{2\mu_w K z}{R} = \frac{2 \times 0.40 \times 0.45 \times 10}{3} = \frac{3.6}{3} = 1.2$$

Compute stress:

$$\sigma_v(10) = 61312.5 \times (1 - e^{-1.2}) = 61312.5 \times (1 - 0.3012)$$

$$= 61312.5 \times 0.6988 = 42842\,\text{Pa} \approx 42.8\,\text{kPa}$$

**Comment:** At $z = 10\,\text{m}$, the vertical stress is $42.8/61.3 = 69.9\%$ of the asymptote. The silo is approaching but has not yet reached the saturation regime. Full saturation would require $z \to \infty$ (practically, $z \gtrsim 4R = 12\,\text{m}$).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with suitable diagrams the difference between **active state** and **passive state** of stress in a granular food material stored in a silo. Derive the lateral pressure ratios $K_a$ and $K_p$ in terms of the angle of internal friction $\phi$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A grain heap on a flat surface forms a cone of base diameter $1.2\,\text{m}$ and height $0.35\,\text{m}$. Calculate the **angle of repose**. If the same grain is poured into a 5-liter container weighing $1.2\,\text{kg}$ and the filled container weighs $5.0\,\text{kg}$, compute the **bulk density**, **apparent density** (assume particle density = $1300\,\text{kg/m}^3$), and **porosity**.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Active State:** The wall moves *away* from the confined material (typical during discharge). The granular mass expands laterally, horizontal stress decreases, and vertical stress is the major principal stress ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_v$). Failure plane is inclined at $(45° + \phi/2)$ to the horizontal.

**Passive State:** The wall is pushed *into* the material (typical during over-filling or constrained lateral expansion). The mass compresses laterally, horizontal stress increases, and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h$. Failure plane is inclined at $(45° - \phi/2)$ to the horizontal.

**Derivation of $K_a$:** From the Mohr-Coulomb criterion at failure (cohesionless case, derived in Section 3.1):

$$\frac{\sigma_3}{\sigma_1} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$

In active state, $\sigma_3 = \sigma_h$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v$:

$$K_a = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Derivation of $K_p$:** By symmetry (swap $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$):

$$K_p = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Numerical example:** If $\phi = 30°$, then $K_a = \tan^2(30°) = 0.333$ and $K_p = \tan^2(60°) = 3.0$. The passive state produces 9 times more lateral stress than the active state.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Angle of Repose:**

$$\tan\theta_r = \frac{2h}{d} = \frac{2 \times 0.35}{1.2} = \frac{0.70}{1.2} = 0.5833$$

$$\theta_r = \arctan(0.5833) = 30.26° \approx 30.3°$$

**(ii) Bulk Density:**

Mass of grain: $5.0 - 1.2 = 3.8\,\text{kg}$.
Container volume: $5\,\text{L} = 0.005\,\text{m}^3$.

$$\rho_b = \frac{3.8}{0.005} = 760\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

**(iii) Porosity (using apparent/particle density = $1300\,\text{kg/m}^3$):**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_p} = 1 - \frac{760}{1300} = 1 - 0.5846 = 0.4154 \approx 41.5\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:** Students often compute the angle of repose using $\tan\theta_r = h/r$ (single radius) instead of $2h/d$. Both forms are correct, but mixing them up leads to wrong numerical answers. **Always confirm whether the given dimension is radius or diameter.**
>
> **Common Mistake #2:** For Janssen's equation, students frequently forget to compute the saturation stress first and directly use the formula at a given $z$, which is fine, but they then **report the asymptotic value as the answer**, losing 2 marks.
>
> **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **angle of rupture** (failure plane angle = $45° + \phi/2$) with **angle of internal friction** $\phi$ itself. These are different quantities and the relationship must be stated explicitly.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Density Hierarchy:** $\rho_t > \rho_a > \rho_b$ — true density is the highest, bulk density is the lowest.
- **Bulk density** is a *system property* that changes with compaction, moisture, and particle size; it is NOT a constant for a given food.
- **Apparent density** uses the envelope volume (closed pores included); **true density** uses only the solid matter volume.
- **Porosity** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_p$ is the fraction of bulk volume occupied by voids.
- **Angle of repose** is empirically measured; **angle of internal friction** is a Mohr-Coulomb strength parameter.
- **Angle of rupture** (failure plane angle) = $45° + \phi/2$ for active failure, $45° - \phi/2$ for passive failure.
- **Active pressure ratio:** $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$; **Passive pressure ratio:** $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$.
- **Internal friction** acts between particles; **External (wall) friction** acts between bulk and wall.
- **Janssen's equation** predicts that vertical stress *saturates* at $\sigma_{v,\infty} = \rho_b g R / (2\mu_w K)$ for deep silos.
- **Mohr-Coulomb criterion:** $\tau = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$ — the foundation of all rupture analysis in food storage.
- **Walker's model:** $\rho_{b,n} = \rho_{b,0} \cdot n^{-0.005k}$ for compaction by tapping — used in packaging design.
- Always use **correct units** (Pa for stress, kg/m³ for density, degrees for angles) — examiners deduct marks for unit mismatches.
- Always state **assumptions** explicitly in derivations (cohesionless vs. cohesive, plane strain, etc.) — KTU awards marks for clarity of assumptions.
- **Mohr circle construction** is the most powerful visual tool for rupture analysis — practice drawing it.
- **Silhouette of $\mu_w K$**: When $\mu_w K$ is high (rough wall + high lateral pressure), the asymptote is *low* (more load carried by wall); when low, asymptote is *high* (more load on floor).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Distribution in Silos, Rankine's Earth Pressure Coefficient, Airys and Janssens Equations (Numerical)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pressure Distribution in Silos, Rankine's Earth Pressure Coefficient, Airys and Janssens Equations (Numerical) # 📘 MODULE 3 — PRESSURE DISTRIBUTION IN SILOS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Pressure Distribution in a Silo? When granular food materials (wheat, rice, maize, flour, sugar, pulses) are stored in tall cylindrical or rectangular bins (called **silos** or **deep bins**), the material behaves neither like a true solid nor like a true liquid. The **vertical pressure** at the bottom of the silo does **not** increase indefinitely with depth (as it would in a liquid, governed by hydrostatics $p = \rho g H$). Instead, a major portion of the load is transferred to the silo walls through **friction**, and the vertical pressure approaches a **finite asymptotic value**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Mouat, Mohsenin, Kroll):** > *The lateral (horizontal) and vertical pressures exerted by a bulk granular solid on the floor and walls of a storage silo are a function of the bulk density, the internal friction angle, the wall friction coefficient, the silo geometry (hydraulic radius), and the depth of fill.* ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stacked Coins in a Tube" Imagine filling a transparent plastic tube with stackable coins. If you shake the tube lightly, the coins settle, and most of the downward force is carried by the **side walls** (because coins "lean" against each other and rub against the tube). A much smaller force reaches the bottom plate. **Janssen's equation** mathematically captures this exact behaviour. If the walls were perfectly smooth (μ = 0), every gram of weight would reach the bottom — like water in a glass. This limiting case reduces to **hydrostatic pressure**. ### 1.3 Why This Matters in Food Storage Engineering - **Structural safety**: Silo walls must be designed for the **lateral pressure** $p_h$, not the full hydrostatic head. - **Silo failure prevention**: Under-design leads to **hoop-stress bursting** of cylindrical walls. - **Flow problems**: Excessive vertical pressure causes **arching**, **rat-holing**, and **caking** of food grains. - **Fumigation & aeration design**: Lateral pressure distribution affects air-flow patterns. > [!NOTE] > **Key constants/metrics** used throughout this module: > - Bulk density of wheat: **750–850 kg/m³** > - Internal friction angle φ for grains: **25°–35°** > - Wall friction coefficient μ (grain on steel): **0.3–0.4** > - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Foundational Theories (Step-by-Step Logic) #### (A) Rankine's Earth Pressure Coefficient (1857) Rankine analysed a semi-infinite mass of granular material bounded by a **plane surface**, assuming: - The material is **cohesionless** (no true cohesion; valid approximation for dry grains). - The material is in a state of **plastic equilibrium** (on the verge of failure). - Two limiting states exist: - **Active state (Ka)** → mass tries to expand laterally → wall pushes back. - **Passive state (Kp)** → mass is compressed laterally → wall pushes outward. ##### Step 1: Stress on a vertical plane At depth $z$, the vertical principal stress is $\sigma_v = \gamma z$. ##### Step 2: Mohr's circle geometry For the active state, the minor principal stress is the lateral pressure $\sigma_h = p_h$. ##### Step 3: Geometry of the failure plane The angle of the failure plane with the horizontal is $45° + \phi/2$. ##### Step 4: Sine-rule on the elemental wedge Combining the equilibrium of a wedge with the failure-plane geometry: $$K_a = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v}\bigg\vert_{\text{active}} = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ $$K_p = \frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_v}\bigg\vert_{\text{passive}} = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{K_a}$$ #### (B) Janssen's Equation (1895) — The Bin Theory ##### Step 1: Identify the engineering problem For a **deep bin** (silo with $\text{height} \gtrsim 1.5 \times \text{diameter}$), hydrostatics over-predicts bottom pressure. ##### Step 2: Free-body of an elemental slice Take a horizontal slice of thickness $dz$ at depth $z$ in a vertical-walled silo. ##### Step 3: Equilibrium of the slice Forces acting on the slice: weight (down), top pressure (down), bottom pressure (up), wall friction (up, opposing the downward settlement of grain). ##### Step 4: Introduce the lateral pressure ratio For filled granular material at rest, define $K$ as the ratio of horizontal to vertical pressure. This is **not** $K_a$ — it is the **at-rest** lateral pressure coefficient $K = K_0$ (often empirically taken as $K_a$ for design). ##### Step 5: Derive the ODE $$\frac{dp_v}{dz} = \gamma - \frac{\mu K p_v U}{A} = \gamma - \frac{\mu K p_v}{R}$$ where $R = A/U$ is the **hydraulic radius** (for a circular silo, $R = D/4$). ##### Step 6: Solve with boundary condition $p_v(0) = 0$ $$p_v(z) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)$$ ##### Step 7: Lateral pressure $$p_h(z) = K \cdot p_v(z) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)$$ #### (C) Airy's Equation (Wedge Theory, 1897) Airy analysed a silo wall by considering a **rupture wedge** sliding down behind the wall, with the wall friction angle $\delta$ acting on the wall. Airy treats the problem like a **Coulomb-type retaining wall** analysis. For a vertical wall with the wedge making angle $\alpha$ with the vertical (or $\beta = 90° - \alpha$ with the horizontal), the lateral pressure per unit length of wall is: $$p_a = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{\cos^2(\alpha - \phi)}{\cos^2\alpha \cdot \cos(\alpha + \delta)\left[1 + \sqrt{\frac{\sin(\phi + \delta)\sin\phi}{\cos(\alpha + \delta)\cos\alpha}}\right]^2}$$ For a **vertical wall** ($\alpha = 0$ from the vertical, i.e. $\alpha$ measured from wall normal), this simplifies considerably, and for a **smooth vertical wall** ($\delta = 0$), Airy reduces to Rankine. Janssen's equation and Airy's equation give similar numerical results for **smooth walls**, but diverge for rough walls because Airy accounts for wall friction explicitly in the wedge. ### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility | Theory | Primary Use in Food Engineering | |---|---| | **Rankine (Ka, Kp)** | Estimating lateral pressure ratio for low silos, designing shallow hopper walls, computing flow-promoter loads | | **Janssen** | Designing deep cylindrical bins for grain, sugar, flour; computing vertical load on silo floor | | **Airy** | Designing rectangular bins with rough walls, hoppers, evaluating pressure on inclined walls | > [!NOTE] > **Crucial distinction for KTU:** Janssen assumes the lateral pressure ratio $K$ is constant with depth (a good engineering assumption because measured $K$ varies only weakly with depth for dry grains). Airy does **not** assume this — the lateral pressure $p_a$ varies with $H^2$ (parabolic with depth), while Janssen's is exponential. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Valid For | |:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:----------| | 1 | Active pressure coefficient | $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | dimensionless | Granular material, active state | | 2 | Passive pressure coefficient | $K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | dimensionless | Passive state | | 3 | Hydraulic radius (circular silo) | $R = \dfrac{D}{4}$ | m | Circular cross-section | | 4 | Hydraulic radius (general) | $R = \dfrac{A}{U}$ | m | Any cross-section | | 5 | Janssen vertical pressure | $p_v(z) = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)$ | Pa | Deep bins, $\text{height} \gtrsim 1.5D$ | | 6 | Janssen lateral pressure | $p_h(z) = K \cdot p_v(z) = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)$ | Pa | Same as above | | 7 | Asymptotic (limiting) pressure | $p_\infty = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu K}$ | Pa | As $z \to \infty$ | | 8 | Wall friction force per unit perimeter | $F_f = \mu \cdot p_h \cdot z$ per unit length | N/m | Vertical wall friction | | 9 | Airy lateral pressure (vertical wall) | $p_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot K_{Airy}(\phi, \delta, \alpha)$ | Pa | Inclined/vertical wall with wall friction $\delta$ | | 10 | At-rest lateral coefficient (empirical) | $K_0 \approx 1 - \sin\phi$ | — | Empirical Jaky's formula | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on units for $\gamma$:** When using $p_v$ in **Pa (N/m²)**, use $\gamma = \rho g$ in **N/m³** (i.e. $\rho \text{[kg/m³]} \times 9.81$). When the result is in **kgf/m²**, use $\rho$ directly in **kgf/m³** (numerically equal to kg/m³). Do not mix. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Janssen's Equation **Setup:** Consider a vertical cylindrical silo of diameter $D$, filled with a granular food (say wheat) up to a depth $H$. Take an elemental horizontal slice at depth $z$ from the free surface, of thickness $dz$. **Define variables:** - $A$ = cross-sectional area of the silo - $U$ = perimeter of the cross-section - $R = A/U$ = hydraulic radius - $\gamma$ = bulk specific weight of the grain (= $\rho g$) - $p_v(z)$ = average vertical pressure on the slice - $p_h(z)$ = average lateral (horizontal) pressure on the wall at depth $z$ - $\mu$ = coefficient of wall friction (grain-on-steel) - $K$ = lateral-to-vertical pressure ratio **Step 1 — Draw a free-body diagram of the slice.** Four forces act vertically: 1. Weight of the slice acting downward: $dW = \gamma \cdot A \cdot dz$ 2. Vertical pressure from the grain above pushing down on the top of the slice: $F_{\text{top}} = p_v(z) \cdot A$ 3. Vertical pressure from the grain below pushing up on the bottom of the slice: $F_{\text{bot}} = p_v(z + dz) \cdot A$ 4. Friction force from the silo wall acting upward (opposing the downward settlement tendency): $F_f = \mu \cdot p_h(z) \cdot U \cdot dz$ **Step 2 — Apply static equilibrium (vertical direction, $\sum F_y = 0$):** The grain is in a stationary state, so: $$F_{\text{top}} + dW = F_{\text{bot}} + F_f$$ Substitute the forces: $$p_v(z)\cdot A + \gamma A\,dz = p_v(z+dz)\cdot A + \mu\, p_h(z)\, U\, dz$$ **Step 3 — Use the definition of a derivative:** $$p_v(z+dz) = p_v(z) + \frac{dp_v}{dz}dz$$ Substitute: $$p_v A + \gamma A\,dz = \left(p_v + \frac{dp_v}{dz}dz\right) A + \mu\, p_h\, U\, dz$$ **Step 4 — Simplify:** $$p_v A + \gamma A\,dz = p_v A + A\frac{dp_v}{dz}dz + \mu\, p_h\, U\, dz$$ Cancel $p_v A$ from both sides, then divide through by $dz$: $$\gamma A = A\frac{dp_v}{dz} + \mu\, p_h\, U$$ **Step 5 — Use the lateral-pressure relationship $p_h = K\, p_v$:** $$\gamma A = A\frac{dp_v}{dz} + \mu K p_v U$$ Divide throughout by $A$: $$\gamma = \frac{dp_v}{dz} + \frac{\mu K U}{A}\, p_v = \frac{dp_v}{dz} + \frac{\mu K}{R}\, p_v$$ where $R = A/U$. **Step 6 — Rearrange into a first-order linear ODE:** $$\frac{dp_v}{dz} + \frac{\mu K}{R}\, p_v = \gamma$$ **Step 7 — Solve using an integrating factor $e^{\mu K z / R}$:** The integrating factor is $IF = e^{\int (\mu K / R) dz} = e^{\mu K z / R}$. Multiply the ODE by $IF$: $$e^{\mu K z / R}\frac{dp_v}{dz} + \frac{\mu K}{R}e^{\mu K z / R}p_v = \gamma e^{\mu K z / R}$$ Recognise the left side as $\dfrac{d}{dz}\!\left(p_v \cdot e^{\mu K z / R}\right)$: $$\frac{d}{dz}\!\left(p_v \cdot e^{\mu K z / R}\right) = \gamma e^{\mu K z / R}$$ Integrate both sides from $0$ to $z$: $$p_v(z) e^{\mu K z / R} - p_v(0) = \gamma \int_0^z e^{\mu K t / R}\,dt$$ **Step 8 — Apply boundary condition at the top surface:** At the free surface ($z = 0$), the overburden pressure is zero (assuming no surcharge): $$p_v(0) = 0$$ The integral evaluates to: $$\int_0^z e^{\mu K t / R}\,dt = \frac{R}{\mu K}\left(e^{\mu K z / R} - 1\right)$$ **Step 9 — Combine and isolate $p_v$:** $$p_v(z) e^{\mu K z / R} = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(e^{\mu K z / R} - 1\right)$$ Divide both sides by $e^{\mu K z / R}$: $$\boxed{\,p_v(z) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)\,}$$ **Step 10 — Lateral pressure:** $$\boxed{\,p_h(z) = K \cdot p_v(z) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)\,}$$ **Step 11 — Asymptotic (limiting) pressure as $z \to \infty$:** $$p_\infty = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}$$ This is the **maximum possible vertical pressure** at the bottom of a very deep silo — the depth beyond which more grain **does not** increase the load. This is the famous "Janssen saturation" that distinguishes silos from liquid tanks. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Problems #### **NUMERICAL 1: Rankine Coefficients for Stored Wheat** > **Problem:** A wheat silo has a grain bed with internal friction angle $\phi = 28°$. Calculate $K_a$, $K_p$, and the lateral pressure at a depth of $4$ m if the bulk density is $800$ kg/m³. Assume active state. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $\sin\phi$ and $\cos\phi$:** $$\sin 28° = 0.4695, \quad \cos 28° = 0.8829$$ **Step 2 — Active coefficient:** $$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 28°}{1 + \sin 28°} = \frac{1 - 0.4695}{1 + 0.4695} = \frac{0.5305}{1.4695} = 0.3610$$ **Step 3 — Passive coefficient:** $$K_p = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{1}{0.3610} = 2.770$$ (Equivalently: $K_p = \frac{1 + 0.4695}{1 - 0.4695} = \frac{1.4695}{0.5305} = 2.770$ ✓) **Step 4 — Vertical stress at $z = 4$ m:** $$\sigma_v = \gamma z = 800 \times 9.81 \times 4 = 31\,392\ \text{Pa} \approx 31.39\ \text{kPa}$$ **Step 5 — Lateral pressure (active):** $$p_h = K_a \cdot \sigma_v = 0.3610 \times 31\,392 = 11\,332\ \text{Pa} \approx 11.33\ \text{kPa}$$ **Result Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | $K_a$ | **0.361** | | $K_p$ | **2.770** | | $\sigma_v$ at 4 m | **31.39 kPa** | | $p_h$ (active) at 4 m | **11.33 kPa** | --- #### **NUMERICAL 2: Janssen's Equation in a Deep Wheat Silo** > **Problem:** A cylindrical wheat silo has diameter $D = 6$ m and is filled to a height $H = 20$ m. Bulk density of wheat $\rho = 800$ kg/m³, internal friction angle $\phi = 28°$, wall friction coefficient $\mu = 0.30$, lateral pressure ratio $K = 0.50$ (taken equal to $K_a$ as design approximation). > Calculate, using **Janssen's equation**: > 1. The vertical and lateral pressures at $z = 0, 5, 10, 15, 20$ m. > 2. The asymptotic (limiting) pressure. > 3. Compare with hydrostatic (liquid) pressure. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Geometric data:** $$R = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5\ \text{m}$$ **Step 2 — Specific weight of wheat:** $$\gamma = \rho g = 800 \times 9.81 = 7\,848\ \text{N/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Useful constants:** $$\mu K = 0.30 \times 0.50 = 0.15$$ $$\frac{\gamma R}{\mu K} = \frac{7\,848 \times 1.5}{0.15} = \frac{11\,772}{0.15} = 78\,480\ \text{Pa} = 78.48\ \text{kPa}$$ $$\frac{\mu K}{R} = \frac{0.15}{1.5} = 0.10\ \text{m}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Tabulate vertical pressure $p_v(z) = 78\,480\,(1 - e^{-0.10 z})$ Pa:** | $z$ (m) | $0.10z$ | $e^{-0.10z}$ | $1 - e^{-0.10z}$ | $p_v$ (kPa) | $p_h = 0.5\,p_v$ (kPa) | Hydrostatic $\gamma z$ (kPa) | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 0 | 0.00 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | | 5 | 0.50 | 0.6065 | 0.3935 | 30.88 | 15.44 | 39.24 | | 10 | 1.00 | 0.3679 | 0.6321 | 49.61 | 24.80 | 78.48 | | 15 | 1.50 | 0.2231 | 0.7769 | 60.97 | 30.49 | 117.72 | | 20 | 2.00 | 0.1353 | 0.8647 | 67.87 | 33.93 | 156.96 | | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 0 | 1 | **78.48** | 39.24 | $\infty$ | **Step 5 — Asymptotic (limiting) pressure:** $$p_\infty = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K} = 78.48\ \text{kPa}\ (\text{vertical})$$ $$p_{h,\infty} = K \cdot p_\infty = 0.5 \times 78.48 = 39.24\ \text{kPa}\ (\text{lateral})$$ **Step 6 — Key observations (KTU High-Yield):** - At $z = 20$ m, Janssen gives $p_v = 67.87$ kPa vs. hydrostatic $156.96$ kPa → **Janssen is 56.8% lower**. - The vertical pressure **saturates** at 78.48 kPa; beyond ~$z = 30$ m, adding more grain does **not** increase floor load. - The lateral pressure (and hence wall thickness/hoop-stress design) is governed by $p_h \approx 39.24$ kPa maximum. - This is why silos are **not** designed for hydrostatic pressure — a major design error if Rankine-active hydrostatic analogy is used for deep bins. --- #### **NUMERICAL 3: Comparison — Janssen vs. Airy vs. Hydrostatic** > **Problem:** For the same silo above, compare Janssen's lateral pressure with Airy's lateral pressure assuming a wall friction angle $\delta = \phi/2 = 14°$. Use a vertical wall ($\alpha = 0$ measured from wall normal, so $\alpha$ in Airy = 90° from horizontal). **Solution (compact form):** **Airy's lateral pressure per unit length of wall:** $$p_a = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{\cos^2(90° - \phi)}{\cos^2 90° \cdot \cos(90° + \delta)\left[1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{\sin(\phi + \delta)\sin\phi}{\cos(90° + \delta)\cos 90°}}\right]^2}$$ For a **vertical wall**, Airy's formula reduces to (using the conventional form with $\alpha$ measured from the horizontal): $$p_a = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{\cos^2\alpha}{\cos(\alpha + \delta)\left[\cos\alpha + \sqrt{\sin(\phi + \delta)\sin\phi / \cos(\alpha + \delta)}\right]^2}$$ For a vertical wall ($\alpha = 90°$ from horizontal, i.e. wall is perpendicular to ground), and **smooth** ($\delta = 0$): $$p_a = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{1}{[1 + \sin\phi]} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{2\cos^2(45° - \phi/2)} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{K_a}{1 - K_a} \cdot (\text{adjustment})$$ The exact Airy value (smooth wall, vertical) is: $$p_a\big\vert_{\delta=0} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{\cos^2\phi}{(1 - \sin\phi)^2}$$ For $\phi = 28°$, $\cos 28° = 0.883$, $\sin 28° = 0.4695$: $$p_a = \frac{1}{2}(7\,848)(20)^2 \cdot \frac{(0.883)^2}{(1 - 0.4695)^2}$$ $$= 1\,569\,600 \cdot \frac{0.7797}{0.2817} = 1\,569\,600 \cdot 2.768 = 4\,344\,563\ \text{N/m} \approx 4.34\ \text{MN/m}$$ Wait — this is force per unit length; the corresponding equivalent pressure at the bottom is: $$p_{h,\text{Airy}}\big\vert_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2 p_a}{H} = \frac{2 \times 4.34 \times 10^6}{20} = 434\ \text{kPa (average)}$$ Airy's bottom pressure is **much higher** than Janssen's ($p_h = 33.93$ kPa at $z=20$ m) because Airy assumes a **Coulomb wedge** with full hydrostatic-like build-up, while Janssen accounts for the distributed wall friction over the entire height. **Airy over-estimates** for deep bins — hence the modern preference for Janssen in silo design. **Summary Table for the Three Theories (at $z = 20$ m, this silo):** | Theory | Lateral pressure | Remarks | |---|:-:|---| | Hydrostatic ($\gamma H$) | 156.96 kPa | Wildly over-conservative; **never use for silos** | | **Janssen** | **33.93 kPa** | **Recommended for deep bins** | | Airy (smooth wall) | ~434 kPa (avg) | Over-conservative; suitable for shallow bins/hoppers | | Rankine active ($K_a \gamma H$) | 56.69 kPa | Reasonable only for **shallow** bins | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Silo Pressure Modelling ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Silo Geometry
Diameter D, Height H] --> B[Cross-Sectional
Area A and
Perimeter U] B --> C[Hydraulic Radius
R equals A over U] A --> D[Granular Material
Properties] D --> D1[Bulk Density rho] D --> D2[Internal Friction
Angle phi] D --> D3[Wall Friction
Coefficient mu] D --> D4[Lateral Pressure
Ratio K] C --> E[Theory Selector] D2 --> E D3 --> E E -->|Shallow bin| F[Rankine Theory
K sub a and K sub p] E -->|Deep bin smooth wall| G[Janssen Equation
Exponential profile] E -->|Deep bin rough wall
or hopper| H[Airy Wedge Theory
Parabolic profile] F --> I[Design Pressure
Output p sub h] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Wall Thickness
Hoop Stress Check] I --> K[Floor Load
Foundation Design] I --> L[Flow Pattern
Mass and Funnel Flow] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic: Pressure Distribution in a Janssen Silo ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Top Surface
Pressure equals 0] --> S2[Depth z equals 5 m
p sub v = 30.88 kPa
p sub h = 15.44 kPa] S2 --> S3[Depth z equals 10 m
p sub v = 49.61 kPa
p sub h = 24.80 kPa] S3 --> S4[Depth z equals 15 m
p sub v = 60.97 kPa
p sub h = 30.49 kPa] S4 --> S5[Depth z equals 20 m
p sub v = 67.87 kPa
p sub h = 33.93 kPa] S5 --> S6[Asymptotic Limit
p sub v = 78.48 kPa
p sub h = 39.24 kPa] H1[Hydrostatic Reference
Linearly increases with z] -.-> S5 S1 -.-> S6 S1 ---|Vertical Pressure Curve| S6 S1 ---|Hydrostatic Straight Line| S6 ``` ### 4.3 Schematic: Rankine Active vs Passive Wedge ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Granular Mass with
Internal Friction phi] --> B[State of Stress] B -->|Wall moves away from fill| C[ACTIVE STATE
K sub a = 1 minus sin phi divided by 1 plus sin phi
Failure plane at 45 degrees plus phi over 2] B -->|Wall pushes into fill| D[PASSIVE STATE
K sub p = 1 plus sin phi divided by 1 minus sin phi
Failure plane at 45 degrees minus phi over 2] C --> E[Engineering Use
Silo discharge, emptied bin] D --> F[Engineering Use
Hopper push walls, flow promotion] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]** *Define Rankine's active and passive earth pressure coefficients for a granular food material. Derive the relationship between them and explain why the lateral pressure ratio $K$ in Janssen's equation is **not equal** to $K_a$ in design practice.* **Model Answer:** Rankine's active coefficient, $K_a$, is the ratio of horizontal to vertical principal stress in a granular mass that is **free to expand laterally** (wall moves away from the fill). It is given by: $$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ The passive coefficient, $K_p$, applies when the wall is **pushed into the fill** (mass compressed): $$K_p = \frac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{K_a}$$ The reciprocal relationship arises because the failure-plane angle flips from $45° + \phi/2$ (active) to $45° - \phi/2$ (passive). **Why $K \neq K_a$ in Janssen's equation:** Janssen uses the **at-rest** lateral pressure ratio $K_0$, because in a **filled, stationary silo** the wall is neither moving away (no active state) nor pushing inward (no passive state). Empirically, $K_0 \approx 1 - \sin\phi$ (Jaky's formula). Designers often conservatively use $K = K_a$ for **discharge** conditions when the grain is flowing and the wall sees active-state pressure. (3 marks: 1 for definitions, 1 for relationship, 1 for the $K$ vs $K_a$ distinction.) --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]** *State Janssen's equation for vertical and lateral pressures in a deep silo. Explain the meaning of the "hydraulic radius" $R$ and why the vertical pressure approaches a limiting value (asymptote) as the depth increases.* **Model Answer:** Janssen's equations (1895) for a granular material in a vertical-walled silo of hydraulic radius $R = A/U$ are: $$p_v(z) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)$$ $$p_h(z) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)$$ where $\gamma$ = bulk specific weight, $\mu$ = wall friction coefficient, $K$ = lateral-to-vertical pressure ratio, $z$ = depth from top. **Hydraulic radius** $R = A/U$ is the ratio of cross-sectional area to the wetted perimeter (perimeter in contact with the grain). For a circular silo of diameter $D$, $R = D/4$; for a square silo of side $a$, $R = a/4$; for a rectangular silo of sides $a$ and $b$, $R = ab/[2(a+b)]$. **Asymptotic behaviour:** As $z \to \infty$, the exponential term $e^{-\mu K z / R} \to 0$, so $p_v \to p_\infty = \gamma R / (\mu K)$. The vertical pressure **saturates** because the additional weight of overlying grain is entirely carried by **wall friction** (through the term $\mu K p_v U$), and the slice below does not "feel" any further increase in overburden. This is fundamentally different from hydrostatic behaviour ($p = \gamma z$, unbounded). (3 marks: 1 for equations, 1 for $R$ definition and examples, 1 for asymptotic explanation.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]** A cylindrical silo of diameter **6 m** and height **20 m** stores wheat with the following properties: bulk density $\rho = 800$ kg/m³, internal friction angle $\phi = 28°$, wall friction coefficient $\mu = 0.30$, lateral pressure ratio $K = 0.50$ (assumed constant with depth). ### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Derive **Janssen's equation** for vertical pressure in a deep silo. State all assumptions clearly. ### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Using the Janssen equation, compute the **vertical** and **lateral** pressures at depths of **5 m, 10 m, 15 m, and 20 m**. Determine the **asymptotic limiting pressure**. Compare the Janssen result at the floor with the **hydrostatic pressure** and discuss the engineering significance. --- **Model Solution:** ### Part (a) — Derivation of Janssen's Equation **Assumptions:** 1. The silo is vertical-walled and rigid. 2. The bulk material is cohesionless and homogeneous. 3. The lateral-to-vertical pressure ratio $K$ is **constant with depth**. 4. The wall friction coefficient $\mu$ is constant. 5. The vertical pressure is uniform over any horizontal cross-section. 6. Static equilibrium (no flow). **Derivation:** Consider an elemental slice of grain at depth $z$ with thickness $dz$. Apply vertical equilibrium (full derivation as in Section 3.1, step-by-step): $$\frac{dp_v}{dz} + \frac{\mu K}{R}\, p_v = \gamma$$ Integrating with $p_v(0) = 0$: $$p_v(z) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z / R}\right)$$ The lateral pressure: $p_h = K \cdot p_v$. **(7 marks: 1 for assumptions, 4 for derivation steps, 1 for final boxed result, 1 for $p_h$ expression.)** ### Part (b) — Numerical Computation **Step 1 — Compute constants (as in Numerical 2):** $$R = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5\ \text{m},\quad \gamma = 800 \times 9.81 = 7\,848\ \text{N/m}^3$$ $$\mu K = 0.30 \times 0.50 = 0.15, \quad \frac{\mu K}{R} = \frac{0.15}{1.5} = 0.10\ \text{m}^{-1}$$ $$p_\infty = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K} = \frac{7\,848 \times 1.5}{0.15} = 78\,480\ \text{Pa} = 78.48\ \text{kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Tabulate $p_v$ and $p_h$:** | $z$ (m) | $e^{-0.10z}$ | $1 - e^{-0.10z}$ | $p_v$ (kPa) | $p_h = 0.5 p_v$ (kPa) | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 5 | 0.6065 | 0.3935 | 30.88 | 15.44 | | 10 | 0.3679 | 0.6321 | 49.61 | 24.80 | | 15 | 0.2231 | 0.7769 | 60.97 | 30.49 | | 20 | 0.1353 | 0.8647 | **67.87** | **33.93** | **Step 3 — Hydrostatic comparison at the floor ($z = 20$ m):** $$p_{\text{hydro}} = \gamma H = 7\,848 \times 20 = 156\,960\ \text{Pa} = 156.96\ \text{kPa}$$ **Step 4 — Percentage difference:** $$\frac{p_{\text{hydro}} - p_{v,\text{Janssen}}}{p_{\text{hydro}}} \times 100\% = \frac{156.96 - 67.87}{156.96} \times 100\% = 56.8\%$$ **Step 5 — Engineering significance:** - Designing for hydrostatic pressure would result in **56.8% over-design** at the floor — a major cost penalty. - Designing for hydrostatic pressure for the **walls** would also over-design, since the lateral Janssen pressure (33.93 kPa) is also far below $\gamma H$. - The asymptotic limit (78.48 kPa) shows that **adding grain beyond ~30 m does not increase the floor load** — a silo is fundamentally different from a liquid tank. - Wall thickness is governed by the **hoop stress** $\sigma_h = p_h D / (2 t)$, where $t$ is the wall thickness. Using Janssen's $p_h$ gives the correct, safe, and economical design. **(7 marks: 2 for numerical table, 2 for asymptotic/hydrostatic comparison, 3 for engineering discussion.)** --- #### **📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]** A food-grain storage silo contains **rice** with the following properties: bulk density $\rho = 750$ kg/m³, internal friction angle $\phi = 30°$, wall friction angle $\delta = 18°$, coefficient of wall friction $\mu = \tan\delta = 0.325$. The silo is **square** in plan, of side $a = 4$ m, and the grain depth is $H = 16$ m. ### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Calculate the **Rankine active and passive earth pressure coefficients** $K_a$ and $K_p$. Discuss, with a sketch in description, the significance of the two states for silo operation (filling, storage, discharge). ### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Using **Janssen's equation** with $K = 0.45$ (assumed), determine the **vertical** and **lateral** pressures at $z = 0, 4, 8, 12,$ and $16$ m. For the **square silo**, the hydraulic radius is $R = a/4 = 1$ m. Compare the Janssen result with the **Airy (smooth-wall) lateral pressure at the floor** and comment on the difference. --- **Model Solution:** ### Part (a) — Rankine Coefficients **Step 1 — Compute $K_a$:** $$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 30°}{1 + \sin 30°} = \frac{1 - 0.5}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.333$$ **Step 2 — Compute $K_p$:** $$K_p = \frac{1 + \sin 30°}{1 - \sin 30°} = \frac{1.5}{0.5} = 3.000$$ Note: $K_p = 1/K_a = 3.000$ ✓ **Step 3 — Significance for silo operation:** - **Active state (filling/storage):** The grain is settling and expanding slightly outward; the wall "restrains" it. $K_a$ is the **lower bound** of lateral pressure; used in conservative design. - **Passive state (discharge, flow promoters):** The flowing grain pushes the wall outward; $K_p$ is the **upper bound** of lateral pressure. Some bins experience "switch pressures" up to $K_p$ on flow initiation. - During **filling**, the wall is pushed by the falling grain (impact + lateral spreading) — pressures momentarily exceed even $K_a \sigma_v$. - During **storage (quiescent)**, the wall sees the **at-rest** $K_0 \approx 1 - \sin\phi = 0.5$ pressure. - During **discharge (mass flow)**, the wall friction is **mobilized**, and the wall can see pressures up to $K_p \sigma_v$ (switch pressure phenomenon). **(7 marks: 1 each for $K_a, K_p$, 1 for relationship, 4 for the operation-state discussion.)** ### Part (b) — Janssen and Airy Comparison **Step 1 — Constants:** $$R = a/4 = 4/4 = 1\ \text{m},\quad \gamma = 750 \times 9.81 = 7\,357.5\ \text{N/m}^3$$ $$\mu K = 0.325 \times 0.45 = 0.1463, \quad \frac{\mu K}{R} = 0.1463\ \text{m}^{-1}$$ $$p_\infty = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu K} = \frac{7\,357.5 \times 1}{0.1463} = 50\,291\ \text{Pa} \approx 50.29\ \text{kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Tabulate $p_v(z) = 50.29(1 - e^{-0.1463 z})$ kPa and $p_h = 0.45 p_v$ kPa:** | $z$ (m) | $0.1463 z$ | $e^{-0.1463z}$ | $1 - e^{-0.1463z}$ | $p_v$ (kPa) | $p_h$ (kPa) | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 0 | 0 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.00 | 0.00 | | 4 | 0.5852 | 0.5570 | 0.4430 | 22.28 | 10.03 | | 8 | 1.1704 | 0.3103 | 0.6897 | 34.69 | 15.61 | | 12 | 1.7556 | 0.1728 | 0.8272 | 41.60 | 18.72 | | 16 | 2.3408 | 0.0963 | 0.9037 | **45.45** | **20.45** | **Step 3 — Airy (smooth wall, vertical) lateral pressure at the floor:** Using Airy's expression for a smooth vertical wall ($\delta = 0$, $\alpha = 90°$ from horizontal): $$p_{a,\text{Airy}} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma H^2 \cdot \frac{\cos^2\phi}{(1 - \sin\phi)^2}$$ For $\phi = 30°$: $$\frac{\cos^2 30°}{(1 - \sin 30°)^2} = \frac{(0.866)^2}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{0.750}{0.250} = 3.000$$ $$p_{a,\text{Airy}} = \frac{1}{2}(7\,357.5)(16)^2(3.000) = 0.5 \times 7\,357.5 \times 256 \times 3 = 2\,825\,280\ \text{N/m}$$ This is **force per unit length of wall**. To get an equivalent average pressure on the wall, divide by $H$: $$p_{h,\text{Airy,avg}} = \frac{2 \times 2\,825\,280}{16} = 353\,160\ \text{Pa} \approx 353.16\ \text{kPa}$$ **Step 4 — Compare:** $$\frac{p_{h,\text{Airy,avg}}}{p_{h,\text{Janssen}}(z=16)} = \frac{353.16}{20.45} \approx 17.3$$ **Comment:** Airy **over-predicts** the lateral wall pressure by a factor of ~17 for this deep square silo. This is because Airy treats the entire wall as a Coulomb retaining wall with a single rupture wedge, while Janssen recognises that **friction is mobilised continuously along the full wall height**, transferring load progressively. For deep bins ($H/D \gtrsim 1.5$), Janssen is the **appropriate** theory; Airy is better suited to **shallow bins and hoppers**. **(7 marks: 3 for tabulation, 2 for Airy calculation, 2 for comparison and discussion.)** --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:** > > 1. **Units of $\gamma$**: Using $\rho$ (kg/m³) directly in Janssen's equation and reporting pressures in Pa — a 2-mark deduction if the question specifies SI units. Always convert to N/m³ by multiplying by 9.81. > 2. **Forgetting the exponential** $e^{-\mu K z / R}$: Many students write $p_v = \gamma z$ for a silo — that is **hydrostatic**, **not** Janssen. Examiner deducts 3 marks minimum. > 3. **Hydraulic radius for square silo**: $R = a/4$ (not $a/2$ and not $a$). Wrong $R$ ⇒ wrong $p_\infty$ and the entire table is incorrect. > 4. **Confusing $K$ with $K_a$**: $K$ in Janssen is the **at-rest** or **design lateral ratio**, not necessarily $K_a$. $K_a$ is the Rankine **active** coefficient. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks. > 5. **Airy's formula** has many trigonometric terms. Students often drop the wall-friction term $\delta$. For **smooth wall** ($\delta = 0$), the formula simplifies; otherwise state $\delta$ explicitly. > 6. **Asymptotic pressure** must be reported as a **finite number** in kPa. Writing "$\infty$" for a deep silo is wrong. > 7. **No comparison with hydrostatic** in 14-mark answers — the engineering discussion carries 2–3 marks and is frequently skipped. --- ### 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Rankine's $K_a$** uses $\tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$; **$K_p$** uses $\tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$; $K_p = 1/K_a$. - ✅ **Janssen's vertical pressure** $p_v(z) = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-\mu K z/R}\right)$ — exponential rise to a finite asymptote. - ✅ **Janssen's lateral pressure** $p_h(z) = K \cdot p_v(z)$ — same shape, scaled by $K$. - ✅ **Asymptotic limit** $p_\infty = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\mu K}$ — the maximum possible pressure at the silo floor. - ✅ **Hydraulic radius** $R = A/U$; for a circle, $R = D/4$; for a square of side $a$, $R = a/4$. - ✅ **At-rest** lateral ratio $K_0 \approx 1 - \sin\phi$ (Jaky); design often uses $K = K_a$ for discharge case. - ✅ **Janssen vs. Airy**: Janssen for **deep bins** (continuous wall friction); Airy for **shallow bins and hoppers** (wedge analysis). - ✅ **Hydrostatic is WRONG** for silos — it over-predicts by 50–90% and is a common student error. - ✅ **Switch pressure**: During discharge, lateral pressure can momentarily exceed $K_a \sigma_v$ and approach $K_p \sigma_v$ (Jenike's design rule: design for $K_p \sigma_v$ during flow). - ✅ **Wall thickness** for cylindrical silo from hoop stress: $t = \dfrac{p_h D}{2 \sigma_{\text{allow}}}$, using Janssen's $p_h$ at the floor. - ✅ **Numerical workflow**: (i) compute $R$, (ii) compute $\mu K$, (iii) compute $p_\infty$, (iv) tabulate $p_v, p_h$ at given depths, (v) compare with hydrostatic, (vi) state engineering significance. - ✅ **Granular food material typical values**: wheat $\rho = 750$–$850$ kg/m³, $\phi = 25°$–$32°$, $\mu = 0.3$–$0.4$ (steel), $K \approx 0.4$–$0.5$. - ✅ **Three classical assumptions of Janssen** — constant $K$, constant $\mu$, uniform $p_v$ over cross-section — must be stated for full marks in derivations. - ✅ **KTU favourite 14-mark question format**: a numerical on Janssen + a derivation + a comparison with Airy or hydrostatic. Practice the three worked examples above as the gold standard. ---

topics.title: Warehouses: Selection of Site for Storage Types, Specific Volume of Bagged Grains, Percentage Utilization of Warehouse (Numerical)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Warehouses: Selection of Site for Storage Types, Specific Volume of Bagged Grains, Percentage Utilization of Warehouse (Numerical)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Warehouse — The Engineering Definition

A **warehouse** in food-grain engineering is a planned, scientifically designed structure used for the safe preservation, handling, and storage of agricultural produce (especially food grains) from the time of harvest until final consumption or dispatch. According to the **Food Corporation of India (FCI)** and **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 607)**, a warehouse is *a commercial building equipped with facilities for receipt, storage, preservation, and issue of food-grains in bags, bulk, or containers.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The focus is on three pillars —
> 1. **Site selection** for different storage types
> 2. **Specific volume** of bagged grains (a design parameter)
> 3. **Percentage utilization** of the warehouse (an efficiency metric)

---

## 1.2 Classification of Warehouses (Storage Types)

Warehouses are broadly classified by **structure**, **commodity**, and **function**.

| Sl. No. | Type | Structural Form | Typical Commodity | KTU Board Frequency |
|:------:|:-----|:----------------|:------------------|:-------------------:|
| 1 | **Conventional / Flat Warehouse (Godown)** | Single or multi-storey RCC/steel building with stackable floor | Bagged grains (FCI norm) | **★★★★★** |
| 2 | **Silo** | Tall cylindrical steel/concrete structure | Bulk grains, oilseeds | ★★★★ |
| 3 | **CAP (Covered and Plinth) Storage** | Raised masonry plinth + removable cover | Buffer/seasonal stocks | ★★★ |
| 4 | **Open Storage** | Open yard with tarpaulin/HDPE cover | Coarse grains (maize, sorghum) | ★★ |
| 5 | **Bonded Warehouse** | Customs-controlled godown | Imported food grains | ★★ |
| 6 | **Cold Storage** | Insulated, refrigerated structure | Perishables (fruits, dairy) | ★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a warehouse like a *hotel for grains* — the **type of room (flat, silo, CAP)** depends on how long the grain is staying, how much space it needs, and how fussy the grain is about climate. Just as a backpacker hostel and a 5-star hotel serve different traveller needs, godowns and silos serve different grain-handling needs.

---

## 1.3 Specific Volume of Bagged Grains

**Definition:** The *specific volume of bagged grain* is the volume (in m³) occupied by **one tonne (1000 kg)** of grain when it is packed in standard bags and stacked in a warehouse.

$$V_s \;=\; \frac{\text{Volume of one bag (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Mass of grain in one bag (tonnes)}}$$

**Units:** **m³/tonne** (SI) or **ft³/lb** (FPS).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Specific volume is the *real estate* a grain demands. Wheat is polite and compact (~1.2 m³/tonne), paddy is fluffier (~1.6 m³/tonne) because of husk, and maize sits in between. This number decides **how big a godown you must build** to store 10,000 tonnes of grain.

---

## 1.4 Percentage Utilization of Warehouse

**Definition:** The percentage utilization expresses **how efficiently the volumetric envelope of a warehouse is converted into useful storage space** for grain bags.

$$\%U \;=\; \frac{\text{Volume actually occupied by grain bags}}{\text{Total geometric volume of warehouse}} \times 100$$

A perfectly packed warehouse approaches ~80–85 %; practically, with aisles, walls, ventilation ducts and the "stack-pyramid effect," good designs settle at **60–70 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Numericals:** Always state the *effective* volume (volume of bags) and the *gross* warehouse volume. Examiners look for both the numerator and the denominator explicitly written.

---

## 1.5 Standard Reference Values (Must Memorize)

| Grain | Specific Volume (m³/tonne) | Bulk Density (kg/m³) |
|:------|:--------------------------:|:--------------------:|
| **Wheat** | **1.20 – 1.40** | 720 – 800 |
| **Paddy (rough rice)** | **1.50 – 1.75** | 560 – 620 |
| **Milled Rice** | **1.35 – 1.50** | 720 – 780 |
| **Maize** | **1.45 – 1.70** | 700 – 750 |
| **Sorghum** | **1.45 – 1.60** | 720 – 780 |
| **Pulses** | **1.30 – 1.50** | 750 – 800 |
