**Q. (b)** A cold storage room has dimensions 10 m × 8 m × 4 m (L × W × H). Wall and ceiling U-values are 0.30 W/(m²·K). Floor U-value is 0.40 W/(m²·K). Outside temperature = 38 °C, inside = 0 °C. Calculate the total **transmission heat load** in kW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Surface Areas:**
- Two walls (long sides): $2 \times (10 \times 4) = 80\,\text{m}^2$
- Two walls (short sides): $2 \times (8 \times 4) = 64\,\text{m}^2$
- Total wall area: $A_w = 80 + 64 = 144\,\text{m}^2$
- Ceiling area: $A_c = 10 \times 8 = 80\,\text{m}^2$
- Floor area: $A_f = 10 \times 8 = 80\,\text{m}^2$

**Temperature Difference:**
$$\Delta T = 38 - 0 = 38\,\text{K}$$

**Heat Loads:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{wall} &= U_w \cdot A_w \cdot \Delta T = 0.30 \times 144 \times 38 = 1641.6\,\text{W} \\
Q_{ceil} &= U_c \cdot A_c \cdot \Delta T = 0.30 \times 80 \times 38 = 912.0\,\text{W} \\
Q_{floor} &= U_f \cdot A_f \cdot \Delta T = 0.40 \times 80 \times 38 = 1216.0\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= 1641.6 + 912.0 + 1216.0 \\
&= 3769.6\,\text{W} \\
&\approx 3.77\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Total transmission heat load ≈ **3.77 kW**.

*(7 marks: 1 for area calculation + 1 for ΔT + 3 for individual loads + 2 for summation and result)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the floor load** — Students often calculate only walls and ceiling. Floor contributes 25–30% of total transmission load and is **always** required.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing kJ/hr with kW. Remember: **1 kW = 3600 kJ/hr**. When using $c_p$ in kJ/(kg·K) and mass in kg/hr, the result is in kJ/hr; convert to kW by dividing by 3600.
> 3. **Wrong TR conversion** — Using 1 TR = 3.86 kW (this is kcal/hr, not kW). Correct: **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/hr**.
> 4. **Including respiration heat for non-horticultural products** — Meat, fish, and dairy do NOT respire. Lose 1 mark if added incorrectly.
> 5. **Vapor barrier on cold side** — This is the *most common conceptual error*. Examiners specifically test whether students understand the *direction* of vapor drive.
> 6. **No safety factor** — Designs without a 10% margin are marked down for "unprofessional engineering practice."

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ Cold storage is a **temperature + humidity controlled warehouse**, not just a refrigerator.
- ☐ Temperature range: **−40 °C to +15 °C** depending on commodity.
- ☐ Total heat load = $Q_1$ (transmission) + $Q_2$ (product) + $Q_3$ (respiration, horticulture only) + $Q_4$ (air infiltration) + $Q_5$ (internal) + $Q_6$ (door) + $Q_7$ (misc).
- ☐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/hr** — memorize this conversion.
- ☐ $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ — calculate separately for wall, roof, floor.
- ☐ $Q_2 = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ (sensible) + $m \cdot h_{fg}$ (latent, only if freezing).
- ☐ Respiration rates roughly **double per 10 °C rise** (Van't Hoff rule).
- ☐ Air changes for sealed cold rooms: **0.5–2.0 per hour**.
- ☐ **PUF** (k = 0.022 W/m·K) is the industry-standard insulation; **closed-cell** structure is critical.
- ☐ **Vapor barrier goes on the WARM side** — otherwise moisture condenses inside insulation.
- ☐ For a wall with only insulation: $t = k / U$.
- ☐ Always apply **10% design safety margin** to final refrigeration capacity.
- ☐ COP = $Q_{evap} / W_{comp}$ — efficiency measure of refrigeration cycle.
- ☐ Floor on ground requires **equivalent temperature difference (ETD)** corrections.
- ☐ Typical insulation thickness: **80–150 mm PUF** for walls, **100–150 mm** for roof, **75–100 mm** for floor.
- ☐ Cold chain integrity requires **pre-cooling** of produce *before* entering main storage.
- ☐ **CA (Controlled Atmosphere) storage** additionally regulates O₂ and CO₂ for long-term apple/pear storage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controlled and Modified Atmospheric Storage, Membrane Storage, Hypobaric Storage, Evaporative Cooling Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Controlled and Modified Atmospheric Storage, Membrane Storage, Hypobaric Storage, Evaporative Cooling Storage # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Controlled Atmosphere Storage is a precision postharvest preservation technique in which the concentration of respiratory gases — primarily **O₂ (1–5%)**, **CO₂ (0–5%)**, and **N₂ (balance)** — is actively measured, regulated, and maintained at a predetermined optimum level throughout the entire storage duration using external gas generators, scrubbers, and computerized control systems. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Breathing Aquarium"** Think of CA storage like a **sealed fish aquarium for fruits**. The fish (fruit) consume oxygen and release CO₂ as they "breathe" (respire). Instead of refilling the tank with fresh air constantly, we precisely tune the O₂/CO₂ balance so the fish stay calm, slow their metabolism, and live far longer. The atmosphere is **externally controlled** by an engineer with instruments — hence the term *Controlled*. > [!NOTE] > **Key Feature:** External monitoring and correction (active regulation) — *not passive equilibrium*. --- ## 1.2 Modified Atmosphere (MA) Storage > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** Modified Atmosphere Storage is a preservation method in which the composition of the storage atmosphere is altered from the normal atmospheric ratio (**~21% O₂, ~0.04% CO₂, ~78% N₂**) and is allowed to equilibrate **passively** with the product's respiration, or is established initially and then drifts with time. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Ziplock Lunch Bag"** A mother packs a child's lunch in a sealed Ziplock bag with apples. The apples slowly consume the limited O₂ and emit CO₂. The atmosphere *changes* on its own. There is **no sensor, no pump, no external regulation** — the fruit's own respiration modifies the bag's atmosphere. This is **MA Storage**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Difference vs CA:** MA is **passive / one-time establishment**; CA is **active / continuous regulation**. --- ## 1.3 Membrane Storage (Selective Permeation MA) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** Membrane Storage employs polymeric films (e.g., **LDPE, PVC, EVA, silicone-based membranes**) with **selective gas permeability** so that O₂, CO₂, and H₂O vapor pass through at different rates, thereby creating a self-regulating modified atmosphere inside the package. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Window Pane"** Imagine a window made of a magical material that **lets CO₂ out fast, lets O₂ in slowly, and keeps moisture in**. Open the window, seal the room with fruit inside, and the gas mix will naturally drift toward an *equilibrium* that suits the fruit. The film **engineers the atmosphere passively** through its molecular pore size and thickness. --- ## 1.4 Hypobaric (Low-Pressure / Sub-Atmospheric) Storage > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** Hypobaric Storage is a technique in which fresh produce is held in a sealed vacuum chamber maintained at a **reduced total pressure (typically 0.1–0.3 atm ≈ 76–228 mmHg)**, causing a corresponding drop in O₂ partial pressure, accelerated diffusion of respiratory gases, and rapid removal of ethylene and water vapor. **Conceptual Analogy — The "High-Altitude Warehouse"** Take an apple to Mount Everest (~0.3 atm). The apple "feels" the O₂ scarcity, respires slower, and lasts longer. Hypobaric storage **simulates a high-altitude environment** inside a steel chamber with continuous vacuum pumps. --- ## 1.5 Evaporative Cooling Storage > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** Evaporative Cooling Storage is a low-cost, off-grid preservation method that exploits the **latent heat of vaporization** of water to lower the storage temperature. Warm, dry air is passed through a water-saturated pad, cooling it close to the **wet-bulb temperature**, which is then circulated through the produce chamber. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Clay Pot Cooler"** In rural India, water is stored in an unglazed clay pot. Water seeps through the porous walls and evaporates from the outer surface, drawing heat from inside and keeping the water cool. **Evaporative cooling storage uses the same physics** — water evaporates, absorbs latent heat (≈ **2260 kJ/kg**), and the surrounding air cools. > [!NOTE] > **Limitation:** Only effective when ambient **relative humidity is below ~70%**. In humid Kerala climate, achievable cooling is limited to 3–8 °C above wet-bulb temperature. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage — Working Principle 1. **Produce is loaded** into a gastight, insulated chamber (typically **0.2–0.3 cm leak rate per day at 10 mmHg pressure differential**). 2. **Initial atmosphere adjustment:** Air is flushed and replaced with target gas mix using **nitrogen generators (PSA / membrane separation)**. 3. **O₂ depletion:** Product respiration consumes O₂ until it reaches set-point (e.g., 2%). 4. **CO₂ scrubbing:** Excess CO₂ is removed using **activated carbon scrubbers, hydrated lime, water scrubbers, or molecular sieves**. 5. **Continuous feedback:** O₂ and CO₂ analyzers (typically **infrared + paramagnetic sensors**) feed data to a PLC for automated gas injection. 6. **Maintain optimum** at ±0.1% tolerance throughout storage life. > [!IMPORTANT] > **CA = Active + Continuous + Engineered Gas Mix.** > Commercial use: Apples, pears, kiwifruit, cabbage, sweet cherries. --- ## 2.2 Modified Atmosphere (MA) Storage — Working Principle 1. Package the commodity in a **permeable or semi-permeable container** (PVC stretch film, LDPE bag, rigid tray with film lid). 2. Either **passive MA** (let respiration modify the atmosphere naturally to equilibrium) or **active MA** (gas-flush initially with target mix, then passive drift). 3. Equilibrium is reached when **O₂ in = O₂ consumed by respiration** and **CO₂ out = CO₂ produced**. **Respiration Model (Michaelis–Menten Kinetics):** $$r_{O_2} = \frac{V_m^{O_2} \cdot C_{O_2}}{K_m^{O_2} + C_{O_2}}$$ where: - $r_{O_2}$ = O₂ consumption rate (mL kg⁻¹ h⁻¹) - $V_m^{O_2}$ = Maximum respiration rate - $K_m^{O_2}$ = Michaelis constant (≈ 2–5% O₂ for most produce) - $C_{O_2}$ = Instantaneous O₂ concentration (%) > [!NOTE] > Below **K_m**, respiration drops sharply — this is the **"critical O₂ threshold"** (typically **1–3%**) targeted in CA/MA storage. --- ## 2.3 Membrane Storage — Permeation Physics Gas transmission through a polymeric film follows **Fick's First Law + Henry's Law (solution–diffusion mechanism)**: $$J_i = \frac{P_i \cdot A \cdot (p_{i,out} - p_{i,in})}{\delta}$$ where: - $J_i$ = Molar flux of gas *i* (mol s⁻¹) - $P_i$ = Permeability coefficient of gas *i* in the polymer (mol m m⁻² s⁻¹ Pa⁻¹) - $A$ = Film area (m²) - $\delta$ = Film thickness (m) - $p_{i,out}, p_{i,in}$ = Partial pressures outside and inside the package (Pa) **Permselectivity ratio (the "tuning knob" of membrane storage):** $$\alpha_{CO_2/O_2} = \frac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{O_2}}$$ | Polymer Film | $P_{O_2}$ (barrer) | $P_{CO_2}$ (barrer) | $\alpha_{CO_2/O_2}$ | |---|---|---|---| | LDPE | 6.0 | 25 | 4.2 | | HDPE | 1.0 | 6.0 | 6.0 | | PVC (rigid) | 0.2 | 1.5 | 7.5 | | EVOH | 0.001 | 0.01 | 10 | | Silicone | 300 | 1600 | 5.3 | > [!NOTE] > 1 **barrer** = $3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ mol m m⁻² s⁻¹ Pa⁻¹ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** By selecting a film with the *right* $\alpha$, a food technologist can passively engineer an atmosphere that suppresses respiration without active gas control — the basis of **"equilibrium modified atmosphere packaging (EMAP)"**. --- ## 2.4 Hypobaric Storage — Pressure-Respiration Coupling At reduced total pressure, the **partial pressure of O₂** is reduced proportionally: $$p_{O_2} = (P_{total} - p_{H_2O}) \cdot 0.21$$ For a chamber at $P_{total}$ = 0.1 atm (76 mmHg) and water-vapor saturated at 10 °C ($p_{H_2O}$ ≈ 9.2 mmHg): $$p_{O_2} = (76 - 9.2) \cdot 0.21 \approx 14.0 \text{ mmHg}$$ vs **160 mmHg** at 1 atm — an **~11× reduction** in O₂ availability. **Hypobaric mass transfer enhancement (diffusion coefficient scaling):** $$D \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{P_{total}}$$ So at $P$ = 0.1 atm, $D$ increases by **~10×**, accelerating outward diffusion of ethylene and inward diffusion of O₂ to the tissue. **Heat and mass balance in hypobaric chamber:** $$q = h_c \cdot A \cdot (T_{fruit} - T_{chamber}) + \rho \cdot \lambda \cdot (W_{fruit} - W_{air})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Hypobaric storage extends shelf life of cut flowers, strawberries, mushrooms, and tropical fruits. Invented by **Stanley P. Burg (1976)**. --- ## 2.5 Evaporative Cooling Storage — Psychrometric Foundation The cooling capacity of an evaporative system: $$Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$$ where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are the **enthalpies of inlet and outlet air** (kJ kg⁻¹ dry air). **Wet-bulb temperature limit** (the theoretical lower limit of cooling): $$T_{out} \geq T_{wb}$$ **Saturation Efficiency (η), the figure of merit of an evaporative cooler:** $$\eta = \frac{T_{db,in} - T_{db,out}}{T_{db,in} - T_{wb,in}}$$ Typical η values: - **Pad-type cooler:** 70–90% - **Spray/mist type:** 50–75% - **Passive clay-pot cooler:** 30–55% **Required airflow for given cooling load:** $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_{total}}{c_p \cdot (T_{db,in} - T_{db,out})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Zero-energy cool chambers for **onion, garlic, potato, citrus, tomato** storage in rural India / Africa (PUSA Zero Energy Cool Chamber, NIF design). --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Consolidated Cheat Sheet | Equation | Description | Engineering Use | |---|---|---| | $r_{O_2} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot C_{O_2}}{K_m + C_{O_2}}$ | Michaelis–Menten respiration | MA/CA design | | $J_i = \dfrac{P_i \cdot A \cdot \Delta p_i}{\delta}$ | Fickian permeation through film | Membrane package design | | $\alpha = \dfrac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{O_2}}$ | Permselectivity ratio | Film selection | | $p_{O_2} = (P_{tot} - p_{H_2O}) \cdot 0.21$ | Partial pressure of O₂ | Hypobaric chamber design | | $D \propto T^{3/2} / P$ | Gas diffusivity scaling | Hypobaric performance | | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{db,in} - T_{db,out}}{T_{db,in} - T_{wb,in}}$ | Saturation efficiency | Evaporative cooler rating | | $Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat removal | Cool chamber sizing | | $Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | Latent heat removal (evap.) | Pad saturation analysis | | $h = 1.005 T + W(2501 + 1.88 T)$ | Moist air enthalpy (kJ/kg) | Psychrometric calcs | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Atmosphere in a Membrane Package **Statement:** Derive the steady-state O₂ and CO₂ concentrations inside a sealed package containing respiring produce covered by a permeable film. **Assumptions:** - Steady state: $\dfrac{dn_{O_2}}{dt} = \dfrac{dn_{CO_2}}{dt} = 0$ - Respiration quotient **RQ = 1** (i.e., 1 mol CO₂ produced per 1 mol O₂ consumed) - Film permeation obeys Fick's law with constant permeability - Package is at uniform temperature $T$ **Step 1 — Mass balance on O₂:** At steady state, O₂ entering by permeation = O₂ consumed by respiration: $$\frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A}{\delta} \cdot (p_{O_2,out} - p_{O_2,in}) = R \cdot W \cdot r_{O_2}$$ where $R$ = universal gas constant, $W$ = produce mass, $r_{O_2}$ = specific respiration rate. **Step 2 — Express partial pressures in terms of mole fractions:** Using ideal gas law inside package volume $V$: $$p_{O_2,in} = y_{O_2} \cdot P_{total}$$ So the driving force becomes: $$(p_{O_2,out} - p_{O_2,in}) = P_{total} \cdot (0.21 - y_{O_2})$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back:** $$\frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot P_{total}}{\delta} \cdot (0.21 - y_{O_2}) = R \cdot W \cdot r_{O_2}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the equilibrium O₂ mole fraction:** $$y_{O_2}^{eq} = 0.21 - \frac{R \cdot W \cdot r_{O_2} \cdot \delta}{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot P_{total}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Equilibrium O₂ **decreases** with increasing respiration, produce mass, and film thickness; **increases** with film area and permeability. **Step 5 — By RQ = 1 assumption, equilibrium CO₂:** $$y_{CO_2}^{eq} = 0.0004 + (0.21 - y_{O_2}^{eq})$$ For $y_{O_2}^{eq}$ = 0.03 (3%), we get $y_{CO_2}^{eq} \approx 0.18$ (18%) — which is **injurious** to most produce, showing why RQ ≈ 1 is rarely realistic; the Michaelis–Menten coupling is needed in real design. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Capacity of an Evaporative Cool Chamber **Statement:** A 1 m³ chamber receives outside air at $T_{db}$ = 35 °C, RH = 30%. Air is passed through a wet pad, exits at saturation efficiency η = 0.85, and is used to cool 100 kg of apples. Estimate the steady-state chamber temperature and cooling load. **Given data:** - $T_{db,in}$ = 35 °C - $T_{wb,in}$ ≈ 21 °C (from psychrometric chart at 30% RH) - $\eta$ = 0.85 **Step 1 — Outlet dry-bulb temperature:** $$T_{db,out} = T_{db,in} - \eta \cdot (T_{db,in} - T_{wb,in})$$ $$T_{db,out} = 35 - 0.85 \cdot (35 - 21) = 35 - 11.9 = 23.1 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 2 — Air enthalpy change (sensible + latent):** Using psychrometric relations: - $h_1$ (at 35 °C, 30% RH) ≈ 64.5 kJ/kg dry air - $h_2$ (at 23.1 °C, saturated) ≈ 68.7 kJ/kg dry air $$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 4.2 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$ Wait — this shows that evaporative cooling **adds moisture and slightly raises enthalpy** in some conditions. The actual *sensible* cooling of the air is: $$\Delta T_{sensible} = T_{db,out} - T_{db,in} = 23.1 - 35 = -11.9 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 3 — Required airflow to remove apple field heat (assume 4 kJ/kg·K cooling from 30 to 10 °C):** $$Q_{load} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 100 \cdot 3.6 \cdot 20 = 7200 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 4 — Air mass flow needed:** Assuming air cools from 23.1 → 10 °C (ΔT = 13.1 K): $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_{load}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{7200}{1.005 \cdot 13.1} \approx 547 \text{ kg/h}$$ At 1.2 kg/m³ density, this is ≈ **456 m³/h** airflow. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering conclusion:** A 0.85-efficiency pad cooler in 30% RH ambient can drop chamber temperature by **~12 °C**, sufficient to extend shelf life of apples by 2–3 weeks. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Hypobaric Chamber Pressure for Target O₂ **Statement:** Apples are stored at 2 °C. The target O₂ partial pressure is 12 mmHg (≈ 1.6%). Find the required total chamber pressure. **Step 1 — Saturated vapor pressure of water at 2 °C:** $$p_{H_2O}(2°C) \approx 5.3 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the partial pressure equation:** $$p_{O_2} = (P_{total} - p_{H_2O}) \cdot 0.21$$ $$12 = (P_{total} - 5.3) \cdot 0.21$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $P_{total}$:** $$P_{total} - 5.3 = \frac{12}{0.21} = 57.14$$ $$P_{total} = 62.4 \text{ mmHg} \approx 0.082 \text{ atm}$$ **Step 4 — Verify humidity effect:** At this pressure, RH inside the chamber = 100% (saturated), so $p_{H_2O}$ remains at 5.3 mmHg. Product weight loss via evaporation will be controlled by continuous water injection or humidification. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical note:** Real hypobaric chambers operate at **50–100 mmHg** with continuous humidification to prevent product desiccation. --- ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Parameter | CA Storage | MA Storage | Membrane Storage | Hypobaric Storage | Evaporative Cooling | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Atmosphere regulation | Active, continuous | Passive / one-time | Passive, self-tuning | Active (vacuum) | None (T control only) | | Typical O₂ (%) | 1–5 | 2–10 | 2–8 | 1–4 (effective) | 21 (ambient) | | Typical CO₂ (%) | 0–5 | 5–15 | 5–15 | < 1 | 0.04 | | Capital cost | Very high | Low | Low | High | Very low | | Energy intensity | Medium | None | None | Medium–High | None | | Shelf life extension | 2–5× | 1.5–2× | 1.5–2× | 2–4× | 1.2–1.5× | | Commodity fit | Apple, pear, cabbage | Cut fruit, salads | Cut fruit, vegetables | Strawberry, mushroom, flowers | Onion, potato, citrus | | Suitability for Kerala | Limited (humidity) | Moderate | Moderate | Limited (humidity) | Limited in monsoon | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Controlled Atmosphere Storage — System Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Produce
Loaded
Chamber] --> B1[O2 Sensor
Paramagnetic] A --> B2[CO2 Sensor
NDIR] A --> B3[T RH Sensor] B1 --> C[PLC Controller] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> D1[N2 Generator
PSA Membrane] C --> D2[CO2 Scrubber
Activated Carbon] C --> D3[Compressor
Air Supply] D1 --> A D2 --> A D3 --> A C --> E[Display
Data Logger] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Function:** A closed feedback loop where sensors measure gas composition, PLC commands gas generators / scrubbers to inject or remove gases, maintaining target O₂/CO₂ set points. --- ## 4.2 Modified Atmosphere Package — Permeation Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ext [Outside Atmosphere] O1[O2 21 percent] C1[CO2 0.04 percent] end subgraph film [Polymer Film] F1[Selective Permeation] end subgraph in [Package Interior] O2[O2 decreasing] C2[CO2 increasing] P[Respiring Produce] end O1 --> F1 C1 --> F1 F1 --> O2 F1 --> C2 P --> O2 P --> C2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Function:** Two opposing flows — outward (CO₂, H₂O) and inward (O₂) — cross the film. At equilibrium, these flows balance respiration exactly. --- ## 4.3 Membrane Storage — Engineering Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Commodity
Type] --> B{Respiration
Rate} B -->|Low| C1[LDPE Film] B -->|Medium| C2[PVC Stretch Film] B -->|High| C3[EVA Multilayer] C1 --> D[Equilibrium MA
O2 3 to 5 percent] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E[Shelf Life
Extension 1.5 to 2x] ``` --- ## 4.4 Hypobaric Chamber — Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fresh Produce
Tray Load] --> B[Sealed
Vacuum Chamber] B --> C[Vacuum Pump
76 to 228 mmHg] C --> B B --> D[Humidifier
Water Injection] D --> B B --> E[Ethylene
Removal Trap] E --> B B --> F[Pressure
Controller] F --> C B --> G[T RH
Monitor] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Function:** Vacuum pump reduces total pressure; humidifier prevents desiccation; ethylene trap (often KMnO₄) prevents autocatalytic ripening. --- ## 4.5 Evaporative Cool Chamber — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ambient Air
35 C Dry] --> B[Water Saturated
Pad or Mesh] B --> C[Humidified Air
22 to 24 C] C --> D[Produce Chamber
Brick Cavity] D --> E[Cooled Produce
10 to 15 C] D --> F[Recirculation
Fan Optional] F --> B B --> G[Water Tank
Reservoir] G --> B ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Note:** A **PUSA Zero Energy Cool Chamber (PUSA-ZECC)** uses double-brick wall with sand-filled cavity, wet top, and a perforated base. Achieves **10–12 °C below ambient** in dry-season conditions with **zero electricity**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Differentiate between Controlled Atmosphere (CA) and Modified Atmosphere (MA) storage with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | CA Storage | MA Storage | |---|---|---| | Regulation mode | Active, continuous | Passive / one-time | | Instrumentation | O₂/CO₂ analyzers, PLC | None | | Gas adjustment | Real-time correction | Initial flush / natural drift | | Atmosphere drift | Negligible (±0.1%) | Yes, with time | | Capital cost | Very high | Low | | Example commodity | Apple (2% O₂, 1% CO₂, 0 °C) | Fresh-cut lettuce in LDPE bag | CA storage is used for **long-term (6–12 months)** storage of high-value commodities like apple and pear. MA is used for **short-term (1–4 weeks)** packaging of fresh-cut produce. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is saturation efficiency of an evaporative cooler? Why is it less than 100%?** **Model Answer:** Saturation efficiency (η) is defined as the ratio of actual temperature drop to the theoretical maximum (wet-bulb) drop: $$\eta = \frac{T_{db,in} - T_{db,out}}{T_{db,in} - T_{wb,in}}$$ It is **less than 100%** because: (i) contact time between air and water is finite, (ii) air-water interface area is limited, (iii) incomplete saturation due to non-uniform pad wetting, and (iv) heat ingress from chamber walls and produce field heat. Typical η ranges from **70–90%** for well-designed pad coolers. --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle, construction, and operation of a **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) storage** system for apples. Discuss the role of gas generators, scrubbers, and control systems. **Model Answer:** **Principle:** CA storage exploits the fact that reducing O₂ to 1–3% and elevating CO₂ to 0.5–5% slows the respiration rate, delays ripening, suppresses ethylene sensitivity, and inhibits microbial spoilage. **Construction:** - A gastight, insulated room with **welded steel/aluminium panel walls** (leak rate ≤ 0.2 cm/day at 10 mmHg). - Refrigeration coils for temperature control. - Humidifier for 90–95% RH. - Gas-tight access door with inflatable rubber seal. - Internal O₂ (paramagnetic), CO₂ (NDIR), and C₂H₄ (electrochemical) analyzers. - PLC-based control panel with data logging. **Operation (Step-by-step):** 1. Load fruit within 24–48 h of harvest. 2. Close and seal the chamber; pull down temperature to 0 °C over 5–7 days. 3. Flush chamber with N₂ from PSA generator to drop O₂ to ~3%. 4. Maintain O₂ within ±0.1% using nitrogen injection; remove CO₂ using lime scrubber. 5. Periodically check fruit firmness, acidity, and disorders. 6. Open chamber only for final dispatch. **Gas Generator:** Pressure-Swing Adsorption (PSA) unit uses **carbon molecular sieves** to separate O₂ from compressed air, yielding **95–99% N₂** for O₂ scrubbing. **Scrubbers:** Hydrated lime [Ca(OH)₂] reacts with CO₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O; activated carbon adsorbs ethylene and volatiles. **Control System:** PLC receives analog 4–20 mA signals from analyzers, compares to set points, and actuates solenoid valves for N₂ injection or scrubber bypass. Modbus/Profibus communication allows remote SCADA monitoring. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Michaelis–Menten respiration model** and explain its use in designing modified atmosphere packages. **Model Answer:** **Derivation:** The respiration of fresh produce is an enzyme-mediated oxidative process. Following classical enzyme kinetics of **Michaelis & Menten (1913)**: 1. **Enzyme-substrate complex formation:** $E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES$ 2. **Product formation:** $ES \underset{}{\overset{k_2}{\rightarrow}} E + P$ 3. **At steady state**, $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$, and the rate of product formation is: $$v = \frac{k_2 \cdot [E]_{tot} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$ 4. **Substituting for produce respiration** with O₂ as substrate: $$r_{O_2} = \frac{V_m \cdot C_{O_2}}{K_m + C_{O_2}}$$ where $V_m = k_2 [E]_{tot}$ (max rate) and $K_m = (k_{-1} + k_2)/k_1$ (substrate affinity constant). **Use in MA Package Design:** - Identify the **critical O₂ level** where respiration transitions from aerobic to fermentative (typically $C_{O_2} < 1.5 K_m$). - Choose a film whose $P_{O_2}$ matches the target steady-state O₂. - Avoid the **anaerobic threshold** (typically < 1% O₂ for most produce) to prevent off-flavor and ethanol accumulation. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Pitfall — KTU Examiner's Warning:** Many students write only the equation without deriving the *steady-state assumption* or explaining how $K_m$ is determined experimentally. In 7-mark questions, **derivation must include the steady-state assumption and rate law origin**. Also, do not confuse $K_m$ (substrate concentration at half $V_m$) with the storage "critical O₂" — they are related but **not identical**. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction, working, and applications of **Hypobaric (Low-Pressure) Storage** with a suitable block diagram. **Model Answer:** **Construction:** A hypobaric chamber is a **horizontal cylindrical or rectangular stainless-steel pressure vessel** rated for full vacuum. It has: - A gastight access door with quick-release clamps. - A **liquid-ring or rotary-vane vacuum pump** with condenser. - Humidification system (ultrasonic nebulizer). - Ethylene scrubber (KMnO₄ on alumina). - Pressure transmitter, T-RH sensor, and digital controller. - Internal trolleys for produce trays. **Working Principle:** 1. Load produce (e.g., strawberries, cut flowers). 2. Seal chamber; evacuate to 50–100 mmHg. 3. Humidified air is continuously drawn through to remove water vapor, ethylene, and heat. 4. Pressure is regulated by inlet air bleed controlled via PID loop. 5. Storage duration: typically 2–6 weeks. **Key Equations:** $$p_{O_2} = (P_{tot} - p_{H_2O}) \cdot 0.21$$ $$D \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{P_{tot}}$$ **Applications:** - Strawberries (14 days vs 7 days in refrigeration). - Cut flowers (carnation, rose) — 4× longer vase life. - Mushrooms — reduced cap opening and browning. - Avocado, mango, papaya ripening control. - Pharmaceutical and biomedical specimen storage. **Advantages over CA:** Faster gas exchange, no gas cylinders needed, automatic ethylene removal. **Disadvantages:** High pump energy cost, desiccation risk, limited commodity compatibility. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **Evaporative Cool Chamber (PUSA-ZECC)** for on-farm storage of fruits and vegetables. Show the cooling load calculation for a 100 kg onion batch. **Model Answer:** **PUSA-ZECC Construction:** - A **double-brick wall** structure with a 7.5 cm cavity filled with wet river sand. - A top layer of wet sand/cloth that is watered twice daily. - A **perforated bottom** for drainage. - A thatched/insulated roof to reduce solar gain. - Chamber dimensions typically **1.65 m × 1.15 m × 0.65 m** holding ~100–150 kg produce. **Working Principle:** - Water from the wet sand evaporates, absorbing latent heat (h_fg ≈ 2260 kJ/kg). - The walls and top act as heat exchangers, dropping inside air temperature to 10–15 °C below ambient in dry conditions. - High RH (80–95%) inside chamber prevents produce shrinkage. **Cooling Load Calculation (100 kg onion, 30 °C → 15 °C):** Given: - $m$ = 100 kg - $c_p$ (onion) ≈ 3.9 kJ/kg·K - $T_1$ = 30 °C, $T_2$ = 15 °C **Sensible heat to be removed:** $$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 100 \cdot 3.9 \cdot 15 = 5850 \text{ kJ}$$ **Required water evaporation rate (assuming 30% of cooling from evaporation, 70% from sensible air-cooling):** $$Q_{latent} = 0.30 \cdot 5850 = 1755 \text{ kJ}$$ $$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{Q_{latent}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{1755}{2260} = 0.78 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Required water top-up:** 0.78 × 24 ≈ 19 kg/day (this is the field reality of PUSA-ZECC operation). > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Pitfall — KTU Examiner's Warning:** In 14-mark questions, students often **omit the numerical calculation** entirely. A numerical problem within a 7-mark sub-part is mandatory. Also, do not state PUSA-ZECC works "the same in Kerala" — admit the **humidity limitation** and suggest a desiccant pre-dehumidification stage or fan-assisted ventilation. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - ✅ **CA** = active, continuous, high-cost, long-term (apple, pear). - ✅ **MA** = passive, one-time, low-cost, short-term (cut produce, salads). - ✅ **Membrane Storage** = film-based self-regulating MA; key is **permselectivity** $\alpha_{CO_2/O_2}$. - ✅ **Hypobaric** = reduced total pressure → lower $p_{O_2}$, higher $D$, faster ethylene removal. - ✅ **Evaporative Cooling** = latent-heat-driven, **zero-energy**, limited by ambient RH (< 70%). - ✅ **Respiration kinetics** follow Michaelis–Menten; **critical O₂** ≈ 1–3% for most produce. - ✅ **Fick's Law** governs membrane permeation: $J = P A \Delta p / \delta$. - ✅ **Wet-bulb temperature** = theoretical floor of evaporative cooling. - ✅ **Kerala context:** High humidity (75–95%) limits evaporative and hypobaric effectiveness; CA/MA and membrane packaging are more relevant. - ✅ **1 barrer** = $3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ mol m m⁻² s⁻¹ Pa⁻¹. - ✅ **2260 kJ/kg** = latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C; ~2450 kJ/kg at 20 °C. - ✅ **PUSA-ZECC** achieves 10–12 °C below ambient in dry season; needs ~20 kg water/day for 100 kg load. - ✅ **Hypobaric** invented by **Stanley Burg (1976)**, originally for cut flowers. - ✅ Always state **assumptions** (RQ, isothermal, ideal gas) when deriving equilibrium equations.

topics.title: Control of Temperature and Relative Humidity Inside Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control of Temperature and Relative Humidity Inside Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Temperature and Relative Humidity (RH) Control in Food Storage** refers to the deliberate regulation of two fundamental psychrometric properties of the air surrounding stored food products — *air temperature* (measured in °C or K) and *relative humidity* (expressed as a percentage, 0–100%) — to maintain product quality, suppress microbial and enzymatic activity, retard respiration, and minimize moisture migration (both gain and loss) within the bulk of the stored commodity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The goal of RH and temperature control is **NOT** to create a "cold room" or "hot room" in isolation, but to maintain an **optimum equilibrium relative humidity (ERH)** for the specific commodity. The ERH is the RH at which the food neither gains nor loses moisture to the surrounding air.

The four canonical psychrometric variables governing storage environment design are:

| Variable | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Dry Bulb Temperature | $T_{db}$ or DBT | °C |
| Wet Bulb Temperature | $T_{wb}$ or WBT | °C |
| Dew Point Temperature | $T_{dp}$ or DPT | °C |
| Relative Humidity | $\phi$ or RH | % (0–100) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Greenhouse-In-Reverse" Principle

Imagine you are storing a **bag of dried chilli** in your kitchen cupboard. If the cupboard air is **hot and dry** (low RH), the chilli becomes brittle and loses weight (moisture migrates *out* of the food). If the cupboard air is **cool and humid** (high RH), the chilli absorbs moisture, becomes soft, and may develop mold.

Now imagine a *smart* cupboard with sensors, a small fan, a heater, and a mist sprayer. When temperature rises, the fan blows cool air in. When RH drops, the sprayer mists water. When RH rises too high, a small heater gently warms the air so it can "hold" more moisture (lower RH). **This is precisely what a commercial storage HVAC system does — at industrial scale.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** The cupboard = warehouse, the sensors = psychrometric probes, the fan = ventilation blower, the mist sprayer = humidifier, the heater = dehumidification coil, and the chilli = stored grain/fruit/vegetable.

## 1.3 Why Both Variables Must Be Controlled Together

Temperature and RH are **thermodynamically coupled** through the psychrometric equation of state. You cannot change one without affecting the other:

$$\phi = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws}(T_{db})} \times 100\%$$

where $p_w$ is the actual partial pressure of water vapor in the air, and $p_{ws}(T_{db})$ is the **saturation vapor pressure** at the current dry-bulb temperature. Notice that $p_{ws}$ depends *only* on $T_{db}$, meaning the *capacity* of air to hold moisture is set by temperature alone.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** Heating air at constant moisture content *lowers* its RH (think of a hot summer afternoon feeling "drier" than a cool morning even with the same amount of water vapor in the air). Cooling air at constant moisture content *raises* its RH until saturation (RH = 100%) is reached — this temperature is the **dew point**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Psychrometric Fundamentals — The Building Blocks

### A. Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT, $T_{db}$)
- The temperature of the air measured by a standard thermometer (mercury, RTD, or thermocouple) with the sensing bulb **dry** and shielded from radiation.
- This is what we colloquially call "the temperature."
- It defines the *thermal* state of the air.

### B. Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT, $T_{wb}$)
- The temperature reached by a thermometer bulb covered with a wick wetted with distilled water, exposed to a stream of air moving at > **3 m/s**.
- Water evaporates from the wick → consumes latent heat → cools the bulb until the evaporative cooling is balanced by convective heat transfer from the air.
- WBT is always ≤ DBT. **WBT = DBT** only when the air is saturated (RH = 100%).
- WBT is a *combined* indicator of both temperature and humidity — it is a "poor man's psychrometer."

### C. Dew Point Temperature (DPT, $T_{dp}$)
- The temperature to which air must be cooled (at constant pressure and constant moisture content) to become saturated.
- At DPT, condensation begins.
- DPT is a *direct* measure of the actual moisture content ($p_w$): it is the **only temperature that depends solely on $p_w$**, not on the DBT.

### D. Relative Humidity (RH, $\phi$)
- The ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor to the saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage.
- $\phi = 0\%$ → perfectly dry air
- $\phi = 100\%$ → saturated air (fog, dew, or rain formation)

### E. Humidity Ratio / Specific Humidity / Absolute Humidity ($W$)
- The mass of water vapor per unit mass of **dry air** (NOT per unit mass of moist air — this is a common KTU exam trap).
- Units: **kg water vapor / kg dry air** (dimensionless, often expressed in g/kg).
- $W$ is **conserved** during sensible heating or cooling (no moisture added/removed), but changes during humidification/dehumidification.

### F. Enthalpy of Moist Air ($h$)
- The total energy content per kg of dry air, including both sensible and latent contributions.
- Units: **kJ/kg dry air**.
- This is the central variable for HVAC load calculations.

### G. Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH)
- The RH at which a *specific food* neither gains nor loses moisture.
- Different foods have wildly different ERH values:
  - Dried milk: **~20%**
  - Wheat flour: **~65%**
  - Fresh apples: **~98%**
- The storage RH must be matched to the ERH of the product — this is the *biological* constraint that the HVAC system must satisfy.

## 2.2 Methods of Temperature Control in Storage

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Mechanical Refrigeration (Vapor Compression)** | Compressor–condenser–expansion–evaporator cycle using refrigerants (R-134a, R-404A, NH₃) | Cold storage of perishables (fruits, vegetables, dairy, meat) |
| **Evaporative Cooling** | Reducing air temperature by water evaporation; cools air close to WBT | Arid-region storage of fruits/vegetables |
| **Ventilation (Natural/Ducted)** | Replacing warm interior air with cooler ambient air using fans or wind | Grain storage in temperate climates |
| **Thermal Insulation** | Reducing heat ingress through walls/roof using materials like PU foam, EPS, VIPs | All controlled storage |
| **Underground / Earth-sheltered Storage** | Leveraging the constant ~10–18 °C temperature of earth at depth | Root crops, traditional grain storage |
| **Night Air Ventilation** | Pumping cool night air into mass thermal storage (grains, masonry) | Pre-cooling grains before sealing |

## 2.3 Methods of Relative Humidity Control

| Method | Mechanism | Direction of RH Change |
|--------|-----------|-------------------------|
| **Dehumidification by Cooling (Below DPT)** | Cooling air below dew point condenses out moisture; reheating to desired DBT gives very dry air | **Decrease RH** |
| **Chemical / Desiccant Dehumidification** | Silica gel, molecular sieves, LiCl rotors absorb water vapor | **Decrease RH** |
| **Adiabatic / Evaporative Humidification** | Spraying fine water mist or using wetted pads | **Increase RH** (and decrease DBT) |
| **Steam Humidification** | Injecting low-pressure steam directly into the airstream | **Increase RH** without changing DBT much |
| **Ventilation with Ambient Air** | When ambient RH is closer to target ERH than interior RH | Moves RH *toward* ambient |
| **Moisture Barriers (Packaging/Coatings)** | Polyethylene liners, vapor barriers, wax coatings on walls | Indirect control by reducing moisture flux |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\phi = \dfrac{p_w}{p_{ws}(T)} \times 100\%$ | Relative Humidity definition | % |
| 2 | $p_{ws} = 610.78 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right)$ (Magnus–Tetens, for $T$ in °C) | Saturation vapor pressure over water, 0–60 °C | Pa |
| 3 | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_w}{p - p_w}$ | Humidity ratio (specific humidity) | kg/kg dry air |
| 4 | $W_s = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_{ws}}{p - p_{ws}}$ | Saturated humidity ratio at $T_{db}$ | kg/kg dry air |
| 5 | $\phi = \dfrac{W\,(p - p_{ws})}{0.622 \cdot p_{ws}} \times 100\%$ | RH in terms of $W$ and $p_{ws}$ | % |
| 6 | $W = \dfrac{2501 - 2.381\,T_{wb}}{2501 + 1.805\,T_{db} - 4.186\,T_{wb}} \cdot (p_{ws,wb}) - p_{ws,db})$ ... [Carrier's equation] | W from DBT & WBT (approx.) | kg/kg |
| 7 | $h = 1.005\,T_{db} + W(2501 + 1.88\,T_{db})$ | Enthalpy of moist air per kg dry air | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $h = 1.005\,T_{db} + W(2501)$ (approx.) | Simplified enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $T_{dp} = \dfrac{237.3 \cdot \ln(p_w/610.78)}{17.27 - \ln(p_w/610.78)}$ | Dew point from $p_w$ | °C |
| 10 | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat load (heating/cooling) | kW |
| 11 | $Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | Latent heat load (humidification/dehumidification) | kW |
| 12 | $Q_t = Q_s + Q_l$ | Total HVAC load | kW |
| 13 | $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \dot{V}}{1 + W}$ | Mass flow of dry air from volumetric flow $\dot{V}$ | kg/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Note:** In all psychrometric equations, the mass flow $\dot{m}_a$ refers to **dry air only**, not moist air. This is a non-negotiable convention. The mass of water vapor is *additional* to this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Controlling temperature and RH is the foundation of:
1. **Cold chain logistics** for perishable food exports (Kerala → Gulf, fish/meat).
2. **Grain silo management** in FCI godowns to prevent aflatoxin growth in stored rice/wheat.
3. **Ripening chambers** for bananas, mangoes, tomatoes (ethylene + RH = 90–95%).
4. **Seed banks** where seed viability requires T ≈ 4 °C and RH ≈ 20–30% (low moisture content).
5. **Pharmaceutical and dairy storage** in cold rooms (2–8 °C, RH 60–70%).

The HVAC system designed for these applications is called a **Refrigerated and Atmospherically Controlled Storage (RACS)** system, or simply a **cold storage with humidity control**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Relative Humidity from Dalton's Law and Ideal Gas Behavior

**Step 1 — Start with Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures.**

For a mixture of dry air and water vapor (treated as ideal gases) at total pressure $p$:

$$p = p_a + p_w$$

where $p_a$ is the partial pressure of dry air and $p_w$ is the partial pressure of water vapor.

**Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law to water vapor.**

The mass of water vapor $m_w$ occupying volume $V$ at temperature $T$ satisfies:

$$p_w V = m_w R_w T \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_w = \frac{p_w V}{R_w T}$$

Similarly for dry air:

$$m_a = \frac{p_a V}{R_a T}$$

**Step 3 — Form the humidity ratio.**

$$W \equiv \frac{m_w}{m_a} = \frac{p_w / R_w}{p_a / R_a} = \frac{p_w \cdot R_a}{p_a \cdot R_w}$$

Using $R_a / R_w = M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97 = 0.622$:

$$\boxed{W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_w}{p - p_w}}$$

**Step 4 — Define saturation.**

At saturation, $p_w = p_{ws}(T_{db})$:

$$W_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_{ws}(T_{db})}{p - p_{ws}(T_{db})}$$

**Step 5 — Express RH in terms of $W$.**

Since the actual water vapor mass is the same, $W$ is fixed, and:

$$\phi = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws}(T)} = \frac{W \cdot p}{(0.622 + W) \cdot p_{ws}(T)}$$

This is the canonical **RH–W–T relation** plotted as the curved grid lines on every psychrometric chart.

## 3.2 Derivation of Saturation Vapor Pressure — Magnus–Tetens Form

The Antoine/Magnus equation is empirical but remarkably accurate. Derivation outline from **Clausius–Clapeyron**:

**Step 1 — Clausius–Clapeyron Equation for Phase Change:**

$$\frac{dp_{ws}}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot (v_g - v_l)} \approx \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_g}$$

where $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization and $v_g \gg v_l$ for water.

**Step 2 — Ideal Gas for Vapor:**

$$v_g = \frac{R_w T}{p_{ws}}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dp_{ws}}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg} \cdot p_{ws}}{R_w T^2}$$

**Step 3 — Assume $h_{fg}$ is approximately constant** (valid for narrow temperature ranges):

$$\frac{dp_{ws}}{p_{ws}} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R_w} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from a reference state** $(T_0, p_{ws,0})$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{ws}}{p_{ws,0}}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R_w}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to °C using $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ and choose empirical constants** (Tetens, 1930):

$$\boxed{p_{ws}(T) = 610.78 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right) \;\;\;[\text{Pa},\;T\; \text{in}\;°\text{C}]}$$

This is the most heavily used equation in psychrometric calculations.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Psychrometric State Point (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem (Modeled on KTU 2024 pattern):**
> In a potato cold storage, air is at DBT = 4 °C and WBT = 2 °C. The local atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa. Calculate: (a) RH, (b) Humidity ratio $W$, (c) Dew point temperature, (d) Enthalpy per kg dry air.

**Given:** $T_{db} = 4°C$, $T_{wb} = 2°C$, $p = 101.325$ kPa.

**Step (a-1) — Compute $p_{ws}$ at DBT = 4 °C:**

$$p_{ws,db} = 610.78 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 4}{4 + 237.3}\right) = 610.78 \cdot \exp(0.2863) = 610.78 \times 1.3316 = 813.4 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step (a-2) — Compute $p_{ws}$ at WBT = 2 °C:**

$$p_{ws,wb} = 610.78 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 2}{2 + 237.3}\right) = 610.78 \cdot \exp(0.1443) = 610.78 \times 1.1552 = 705.6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step (a-3) — Use the psychrometric wet-bulb relation:**

At steady state, energy balance on the wick gives:

$$W = \frac{ (p_{ws,wb} - p_{ws,db}) \cdot c_{pw} }{ p - p_{ws,wb} } \cdot \frac{1}{h_{fg}/T_{wb} \text{ terms}}$$

Using the simplified Carrier equation (valid for moderate T):

$$W = \frac{ (p_{ws,wb} - 0.000660 \cdot p \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) \cdot (1 + 1.146\,T_{wb}) ) \cdot 0.622 }{ p - p_{ws,wb} }$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$W = \frac{(705.6 - 0.000660 \times 101325 \times 2 \times (1 + 2.292)) \times 0.622}{101325 - 705.6}$$

$$= \frac{(705.6 - 0.000660 \times 101325 \times 2 \times 3.292) \times 0.622}{100619.4}$$

$$= \frac{(705.6 - 440.1) \times 0.622}{100619.4} = \frac{265.5 \times 0.622}{100619.4}$$

$$W = 0.001641 \text{ kg/kg dry air} = 1.641 \text{ g/kg}$$

**Step (a-4) — RH calculation:**

$$p_w = \frac{W \cdot p}{0.622 + W} = \frac{0.001641 \times 101325}{0.622 + 0.001641} = \frac{166.3}{0.6236} = 266.7 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\phi = \frac{266.7}{813.4} \times 100\% = 32.8\%$$

**Step (b) — Humidity ratio** (already computed): $\boxed{W = 0.001641 \text{ kg/kg}}$

**Step (c) — Dew point from $p_w = 266.7$ Pa:**

$$T_{dp} = \frac{237.3 \times \ln(p_w/610.78)}{17.27 - \ln(p_w/610.78)} = \frac{237.3 \times \ln(0.4367)}{17.27 - \ln(0.4367)}$$

$$= \frac{237.3 \times (-0.8285)}{17.27 - (-0.8285)} = \frac{-196.6}{18.10} = -10.86°C$$

**Step (d) — Enthalpy:**

$$h = 1.005 \times 4 + 0.001641 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 4) = 4.02 + 0.001641 \times 2508.52$$

$$= 4.02 + 4.116 = 8.14 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Final Results Summary Table:**

| Property | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Relative Humidity | $\phi$ | **32.8** | % |
| Humidity Ratio | $W$ | **1.641** | g/kg dry air |
| Dew Point | $T_{dp}$ | **−10.86** | °C |
| Enthalpy | $h$ | **8.14** | kJ/kg dry air |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** With $\phi \approx 33\%$, the storage air is *too dry* for potatoes (which prefer 90–95% RH to avoid shriveling). The HVAC system must **humidify** the air — typically by injecting low-pressure steam — to raise RH to ~90% without changing the 4 °C temperature.

## 3.4 Derivation of Mixed-Air State (Critical for Ventilation Design)

When two air streams mix (e.g., cold outdoor air + warm return air), the mixed state is found by **mass and energy balances on water vapor and enthalpy**:

**Dry air mass balance:**

$$\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_3$$

**Water vapor mass balance:**

$$\dot{m}_1 W_1 + \dot{m}_2 W_2 = \dot{m}_3 W_3$$

**Energy balance:**

$$\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3$$

Solving simultaneously (and dividing the water-vapor balance by the dry-air balance):

$$\boxed{W_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_1 W_1 + \dot{m}_2 W_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}, \quad h_3 = \frac{\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_2 h_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}}$$

Geometrically on the psychrometric chart, the mixed state $3$ lies on the **straight line** joining states 1 and 2, at a position governed by the **lever rule**:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_1}{\dot{m}_2} = \frac{\overline{2\text{-}3}}{\overline{1\text{-}3}}$$

where $\overline{ij}$ is the length of the line segment from state $i$ to state $j$ on the chart.
