# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Modeled)]
**Define relative humidity and dew point temperature. Why is dew point considered a more direct measure of absolute moisture content than RH?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Relative Humidity (RH) is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor in the air to the saturation vapor pressure at the same dry-bulb temperature, expressed as a percentage:

$$\phi = \frac{p_w}{p_{ws}(T_{db})} \times 100\%$$

Dew Point Temperature ($T_{dp}$) is the temperature at which moist air becomes saturated when cooled at constant pressure and constant moisture content, i.e., condensation just begins.

**Why $T_{dp}$ is a direct measure of $p_w$:** The saturation vapor pressure is a single-valued monotonic function of temperature. Therefore, the temperature at which the actual vapor pressure $p_w$ equals saturation ($p_{ws}(T_{dp}) = p_w$) uniquely identifies $p_w$. Since RH also depends on $T_{db}$ (via $p_{ws}(T_{db})$), two different air samples at different temperatures can have the *same RH* but very *different* $p_w$ (and thus different $T_{dp}$). Hence $T_{dp}$ is a *direct* and *invariant* indicator of absolute moisture, whereas RH is *relative* to the current temperature. **(1 mark for the explanation)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "RH tells us how much water is in the air." This is **wrong** — RH is a *ratio* of *capacity* (which depends on temperature) to *content*. The actual content is given by $W$ or $T_{dp}$, not RH. Marks lost for imprecise language.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Modeled)]
**Explain the term "Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH)" of a food. Give the ERH ranges for three different food categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH) of a food is the relative humidity of the surrounding air at which the food neither gains nor loses moisture — i.e., the water activity ($a_w$) of the food equals the RH of the air, and the system is at *thermodynamic equilibrium* with respect to moisture exchange.

**ERH ranges (any three accepted):**

| Food Category | Example | Typical ERH |
|---------------|---------|-------------|
| Very hygroscopic (high $a_w$) | Fresh fruits, vegetables, meat | **90 – 99%** |
| Intermediate | Bread, cheese, dry fruits | **60 – 80%** |
| Low moisture / hygroscopic (low $a_w$) | Dried milk, spices, biscuits, grains | **15 – 40%** |

**Engineering implication:** Storage RH must be matched to the ERH of the product. For potatoes (ERH ≈ 99%), the storage air must be near saturation. For dried spices (ERH ≈ 20–30%), the air must be very dry — using cold storage of high-RH ambient air would *wet* the spices. **(1 mark for implication)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU 2024 Pattern — Psychrometric Design]

**A grain warehouse stores wheat at 15 °C. The required storage RH is 65%. Ambient air enters at 30 °C DBT and 50% RH. The air is to be conditioned by the following sequence:**
- **(i) Sensible cooling to 20 °C**
- **(ii) Adiabatic saturation (evaporative cooling) to the desired humidity**
- **(iii) Reheating back to 15 °C**

**Take atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa. Plot the process on a schematic psychrometric chart and compute the following:**
**(a) The humidity ratio of the conditioned air supplied to the warehouse. (7 marks)**
**(b) The mass of water to be added or removed per 1000 m³ of air handled, and the heating/cooling loads (in kW) for a supply airflow of 1.5 m³/s. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**State 1 — Ambient Air: $T_{db,1} = 30°C$, $\phi_1 = 50\%$**

Compute $p_{ws,1}$ at 30 °C:

$$p_{ws,1} = 610.78 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 30}{30 + 237.3}\right) = 610.78 \cdot \exp(1.9367) = 610.78 \times 6.936 = 4236 \text{ Pa}$$

$$p_{w,1} = 0.50 \times 4236 = 2118 \text{ Pa}$$

$$W_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{2118}{101325 - 2118} = 0.622 \times \frac{2118}{99207} = 0.01328 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**State 2 — Sensible Cooling to 20 °C (constant $W$):** $W_2 = W_1 = 0.01328$ kg/kg, $T_{db,2} = 20°C$.

Compute $p_{ws,2}$ at 20 °C:

$$p_{ws,2} = 610.78 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 20}{20 + 237.3}\right) = 610.78 \cdot \exp(1.3434) = 610.78 \times 3.832 = 2341 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\phi_2 = \frac{2118}{2341} = 0.9047 = 90.47\%$$

**State 3 — Adiabatic (Evaporative) Saturation to target RH 65% at... wait, adiabatic saturation gives WBT-equilibrium state, not a fixed RH. Re-interpret: The student should humidify to reach 65% RH, then reheat.**

Let us re-interpret the problem: The target storage condition is **15 °C, 65% RH**. After reheat to 15 °C with the *same* $W$, we need $\phi = 65\%$.

**State 4 (Storage Condition) — $T_{db,4} = 15°C$, $\phi_4 = 65\%$**

$$p_{ws,4} = 610.78 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 15}{15 + 237.3}\right) = 610.78 \cdot \exp(1.0267) = 610.78 \times 2.792 = 1705 \text{ Pa}$$

$$p_{w,4} = 0.65 \times 1705 = 1108 \text{ Pa}$$

$$W_4 = 0.622 \times \frac{1108}{101325 - 1108} = 0.622 \times \frac{1108}{100217} = 0.006878 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Therefore, the supply-air humidity ratio = $W_4 = 0.006878$ kg/kg dry air. ✓ (7 marks — part a complete)**

**Part (b) — Mass of water removed and loads:**

**Step 1 — Mass of water to be removed per kg of dry air:**

$$\Delta W = W_1 - W_4 = 0.01328 - 0.006878 = 0.006402 \text{ kg water per kg dry air}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water removed per 1000 m³ of inlet air:**

At State 1, specific volume of moist air:

$$v_1 = \frac{R_a T_{a,1}(1 + 1.6078\,W_1)}{p} = \frac{287 \times 303.15 \times (1 + 1.6078 \times 0.01328)}{101325}$$

$$= \frac{87045 \times 1.02135}{101325} = 0.8774 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$

Dry air mass in 1000 m³:

$$m_a = \frac{1000}{0.8774} = 1139.7 \text{ kg dry air}$$

Water removed:

$$\Delta m_w = 1139.7 \times 0.006402 = 7.30 \text{ kg water per 1000 m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Enthalpies:**

$$h_1 = 1.005 \times 30 + 0.01328 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 30) = 30.15 + 0.01328 \times 2557.4 = 30.15 + 33.96 = 64.11 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005 \times 20 + 0.01328 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 20) = 20.10 + 0.01328 \times 2538.6 = 20.10 + 33.71 = 53.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$h_4 = 1.005 \times 15 + 0.006878 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 15) = 15.075 + 0.006878 \times 2529.2 = 15.075 + 17.40 = 32.47 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow rate of dry air for $\dot{V} = 1.5$ m³/s:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{1.5}{0.8774} = 1.710 \text{ kg dry air/s}$$

**Step 5 — Cooling load (State 1 → State 2, sensible cooling):**

$$Q_{cool} = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) = 1.710 \times (64.11 - 53.81) = 1.710 \times 10.30 = 17.61 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Dehumidification load (State 2 → State 3, i.e., the latent removal by cooling below dew point; here directly from ambient to storage $W$):**

$$Q_{lat} = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_1 - W_4) = 1.710 \times 2501 \times 0.006402 = 27.38 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Reheating load (State 3 → State 4, sensible reheat):**

$$Q_{reheat} = \dot{m}_a (h_4 - h_3) = 1.710 \times (h_4 - h_3)$$

Where $h_3$ corresponds to $W_3 = W_4 = 0.006878$ kg/kg at the temperature after dehumidification (approximately near saturation at $\sim 9°C$):

For simplicity, taking dehumidification by cooling close to dew point and then reheat:

$$Q_{reheat} \approx 1.710 \times (32.47 - 17.40) = 1.710 \times 15.07 = 25.77 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer Summary (7 marks — part b):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Water removed per 1000 m³ | **7.30 kg** |
| Cooling load | **17.61 kW** |
| Latent (dehumidification) load | **27.38 kW** |
| Reheating load | **25.77 kW** |
| Total HVAC load | **~70.76 kW** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Q-A):**
> - **(−1 mark)** Using $W$ based on total moist air mass instead of dry air — *check* the formula $W = 0.622 p_w / (p - p_w)$ is used.
> - **(−1 mark)** Forgetting that the supply-air RH must be 65% **at the storage temperature (15 °C)**, not at the post-cooling temperature.
> - **(−1 mark)** Treating 1.5 m³/s as dry-air flow — it is moist-air flow; divide by specific volume first.
> - **(−1 mark)** Not labeling units on intermediate values (always show $p_w$ in Pa, $W$ in kg/kg, $h$ in kJ/kg).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — ERH & Storage Design]

**(a) Discuss in detail the factors affecting the choice of storage temperature and relative humidity for different classes of food products. Support your answer with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**(b) A banana ripening chamber is to be designed for 25 °C and 95% RH. The chamber holds 10 tonnes of bananas that respire at 15 mW/kg. The walls have a heat-transfer coefficient of $U = 0.4$ W/m²·K and area 200 m². Ambient is 35 °C, 60% RH. The fresh-air ventilation rate is 0.05 m³/s. Compute: (i) the sensible heat load due to respiration and transmission, (ii) the moisture load on the chamber per hour, and (iii) the required refrigeration capacity. Take $h_{fg} = 2400$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Discussion of Factors (7 marks):**

The choice of storage temperature and RH is governed by **five principal factors**:

1. **Commodity Respiration Rate:** Fresh fruits and vegetables are *living* tissues that respire (consume O₂, produce CO₂, water vapor, and heat). Higher temperatures accelerate respiration exponentially ($Q_{10} \approx 2$ to 3). Example: Strawberries at 0 °C respire at ~20 mW/kg, but at 20 °C at ~150 mW/kg — drastically shortening shelf life. Thus, perishables are stored just above their chilling-injury temperature.

2. **Freezing Point / Chilling Injury:** Fruits like banana (chilling at <13 °C), mango (chilling at <10 °C), and cucumber (chilling at <7 °C) must not be stored too cold, while meats and fish are stored frozen at −18 to −25 °C.

3. **Microbial Growth Limits:** Bacteria need $a_w > 0.86$ (RH > 86%) to grow. Yeasts: $a_w > 0.65$. Molds: $a_w > 0.60$. Thus, dried products are stored at low RH to inhibit mold.

4. **Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH) of the Product:** As in Q2 above, RH must match the ERH to prevent weight loss/gain and texture changes. Example: Onion storage at 0 °C, 65–70% RH; dried milk at 15–20 °C, 20–30% RH.

5. **Enzymatic and Chemical Activity:** Lipid oxidation in nuts is faster at high RH; enzymatic browning in cut apples is faster at high T. Honey crystallizes faster at 14 °C (ideal storage ~25 °C).

6. **Insect and Pest Activity:** Grain weevils cannot survive below 13 °C or below 35% RH — the basis of "cool and dry" grain storage in India.

7. **Package Compatibility:** Some moisture-sensitive products (crackers, chips) require RH < 40% — packaging must include moisture barriers.

**Examples for KTU Exam:**

| Product | Storage T (°C) | Storage RH (%) | Reason |
|---------|---------------|----------------|--------|
| Apples (long term) | 0 to 4 | 90 – 95 | Avoid shriveling; suppress respiration |
| Onion (dried) | 0 | 65 – 70 | Avoid root growth & decay |
| Potato (table) | 4 to 10 | 90 – 95 | Avoid sprouting; minimize shrinkage |
| Wheat (grain) | 10 to 15 | < 60 | Prevent mold, weevil activity |
| Frozen fish | −18 to −25 | — | Halt all microbial & enzymatic action |
| Banana (ripening) | 13 to 20 | 90 – 95 | Allow ethylene-induced ripening |
| Dried milk | < 20 | 20 – 30 | Prevent caking & browning |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_{db,\text{chamber}} = 25°C$, $\phi_{\text{chamber}} = 95\%$
- Mass of bananas $M = 10{,}000$ kg
- Respiration heat $q_{\text{resp}} = 15$ mW/kg = $0.015$ W/kg
- Wall area $A = 200$ m², $U = 0.4$ W/m²·K
- $T_{\text{amb}} = 35°C$, $\phi_{\text{amb}} = 60\%$
- $\dot{V}_{\text{vent}} = 0.05$ m³/s
- $h_{fg} = 2400$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³

**(i) Sensible heat load — Respiration + Transmission + Ventilation:**

**Respiration load:**

$$Q_{\text{resp}} = M \cdot q_{\text{resp}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.015 = 150 \text{ W} = 0.150 \text{ kW}$$

**Transmission (conduction) load:**

$$Q_{\text{cond}} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ch}}) = 0.4 \times 200 \times (35 - 25) = 0.4 \times 200 \times 10 = 800 \text{ W} = 0.800 \text{ kW}$$

**Ventilation sensible load:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V} = 1.2 \times 0.05 = 0.06 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{vent,s}} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ch}}) = 0.06 \times 1.005 \times 10 = 0.603 \text{ kW}$$

**Total sensible load:**

$$Q_s = 0.150 + 0.800 + 0.603 = 1.553 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Moisture load per hour:**

First, compute $W$ at both states.

At $T_{db} = 35°C$: $p_{ws,35} = 610.78 \times \exp(17.27 \times 35 / 272.3) = 610.78 \times \exp(2.2193) = 610.78 \times 9.201 = 5620$ Pa.

$p_{w,\text{amb}} = 0.60 \times 5620 = 3372$ Pa.

$W_{\text{amb}} = 0.622 \times 3372 / (101325 - 3372) = 0.622 \times 3372 / 97953 = 0.02141$ kg/kg.

At $T_{db} = 25°C$: $p_{ws,25} = 610.78 \times \exp(17.27 \times 25 / 262.3) = 610.78 \times \exp(1.6457) = 610.78 \times 5.184 = 3167$ Pa.

$p_{w,\text{ch}} = 0.95 \times 3167 = 3009$ Pa.

$W_{\text{ch}} = 0.622 \times 3009 / (101325 - 3009) = 0.622 \times 3009 / 98316 = 0.01904$ kg/kg.

**Moisture removed by condensation / dehumidification (chamber → exhaust to maintain 95% RH):**

Actually, the chamber is gaining moisture from respiration and from ventilation. Let us clarify: the *ventilation air entering* has $W_{\text{amb}} = 0.02141$ kg/kg, while *chamber* is at $W_{\text{ch}} = 0.01904$ kg/kg. So incoming air is *more humid* → the system must remove moisture to maintain 95% RH.

**Moisture load (rate):**

$$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \cdot (W_{\text{amb}} - W_{\text{ch}}) = 0.06 \times (0.02141 - 0.01904) = 0.06 \times 0.00237 = 1.422 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

Plus respiration adds water: bananas produce ~0.5 g water per kg per hour at this rate (respiration stoichiometry: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O).

$$\dot{m}_{w,\text{resp}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} / 3600 = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Total moisture load:**

$$\dot{m}_{w,\text{total}} = 1.422 \times 10^{-4} + 1.389 \times 10^{-3} = 1.531 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Per hour:**

$$m_{w,\text{total}} = 1.531 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 5.51 \text{ kg/hour}$$

**(iii) Required refrigeration capacity:**

Total load = sensible + latent:

$$Q_{\text{lat}} = \dot{m}_{w,\text{total}} \cdot h_{fg} = 1.531 \times 10^{-3} \times 2400 = 3.674 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_s + Q_{\text{lat}} = 1.553 + 3.674 = 5.227 \text{ kW}$$

**Required refrigeration capacity ≈ 5.23 kW (≈ 1.5 TR).**

**Final Results Summary (7 marks — part b):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Total sensible load | **1.553 kW** |
| Total moisture load (latent) | **3.674 kW** |
| Moisture per hour | **5.51 kg/h** |
| **Required refrigeration capacity** | **5.23 kW (≈1.5 TR)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Q-B):**
> - **(−1 mark)** Forgetting the **ventilation sensible load** — many students omit the fresh-air load. A storage chamber with sealed walls and no ventilation is physically unrealistic over a 24-hour cycle; always include it.
> - **(−1 mark)** Mixing up $h_{fg}$ value — use 2501 kJ/kg at 0 °C, 2400 kJ/kg at 25 °C, 2257 kJ/kg at 100 °C. KTU expects you to *use* the value given in the problem.
> - **(−1 mark)** Reporting the moisture load in kg/s when the question asks per hour — read the units requested.
> - **(−1 mark)** Confusing the direction of moisture flow. If incoming air has higher $W$ than chamber, the system must *dehumidify* (chamber is gaining moisture, not losing it).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ☐ **RH = (actual vapor pressure) / (saturation vapor pressure at the same T) × 100%** — always a ratio at the *current* temperature.
- ☐ **Dew Point = the temperature at which saturation occurs** — depends only on $p_w$, not on DBT.
- ☐ **WBT ≤ DBT** always, with equality only at 100% RH.
- ☐ **W (humidity ratio) is conserved** during sensible heating/cooling — this is why we can reheat air without re-humidifying.
- ☐ **ERH of food = water activity (a_w) at equilibrium** — storage RH must equal ERH to prevent weight loss.
- ☐ **Magnus–Tetens equation** for $p_{ws}$: $p_{ws} = 610.78 \exp(17.27T / (T + 237.3))$ Pa, with $T$ in °C.
- ☐ **Enthalpy of moist air:** $h = 1.005 T_{db} + W(2501 + 1.88 T_{db})$ kJ/kg dry air.
- ☐ **Saturation humidity ratio:** $W_s = 0.622 p_{ws} / (p - p_{ws})$.
- ☐ **Humidity ratio formula:** $W = 0.622 p_w / (p - p_w)$ — note the **0.622 factor** comes from $M_w/M_a$ ratio.
- ☐ **Two coupled variables:** You cannot change T without affecting RH, and vice versa — control systems must account for this coupling via a **PID controller** with two loops.
- ☐ **Common storage conditions to memorize:**
  - Fresh fruits/vegetables: 0–4 °C, 90–95% RH
  - Banana ripening: 13–20 °C, 90–95% RH
  - Onion/dried garlic: 0 °C, 65–70% RH
  - Grains/seeds: 10–15 °C, < 60% RH
  - Frozen foods: −18 to −25 °C, no RH control needed
  - Dried milk/spices: 15–20 °C, 20–30% RH
- ☐ **HVAC loads have two parts:** Sensible (temperature) and Latent (moisture) — both must be sized separately.
- ☐ **Mixing of two air streams** follows the lever rule on the psychrometric chart.
- ☐ **Dew point is the lowest temperature any psychrometric process can cool air to without adding/removing moisture.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Conditions for Raw and Processed Fruits, Vegetables, Meat, Dairy, Factors Affecting Storage Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storage Conditions for Raw and Processed Fruits, Vegetables, Meat, Dairy, Factors Affecting Storage Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is "Storage Condition" in Food Engineering?

In **Food Storage Engineering**, a *storage condition* is defined as the precisely controlled combination of **physical, chemical, and microbiological environmental parameters** (temperature, relative humidity, atmospheric gas composition, light, and sanitation status) under which a raw or processed food commodity is held after harvest/slaughter/processing and before final consumption, in order to **minimize quality degradation** and **maximize shelf life** while preserving its nutritional, sensory, and safety attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Storage is **NOT just refrigeration**. It is an integrated engineering science combining thermodynamics, mass transfer, microbiology, and packaging technology. The correct storage condition is *commodity-specific* — a single set of parameters does NOT fit all foods.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pause Button" for Food

Imagine you have just taken a fresh, hot slice of bread out of the oven. The bread immediately begins to change — it loses moisture, the crust softens, and within a few days it becomes stale or develops mold. These changes are driven by **molecular motion, microbial activity, and chemical reactions** — all of which *speed up* at higher temperatures.

> A refrigerator, a controlled atmosphere room, or a frozen warehouse is essentially a **"pause button"** for food. By lowering the temperature, adjusting humidity, and controlling the gas atmosphere around the food, we are **slowing down the internal "clock"** of the food so that it stays fresh for days, weeks, or even months longer.

- **Raw foods** (fresh fruits, vegetables, meat, milk) are like *living systems* that continue to respire, ripen, and deteriorate — storage must respect their biological nature.
- **Processed foods** (canned, frozen, dried, fermented) are more stable but still degrade via *lipid oxidation, moisture migration, microbial recontamination, and packaging failure* — storage must defend against these.

## 1.3 Why Different Foods Need Different Conditions

The same "ideal" cold temperature can **damage** certain foods:

- Bananas stored at $0^\circ C$ develop **chilling injury** (skin blackening, failure to ripen).
- Tomatoes lose flavor and become mealy below $10^\circ C$.
- Onions **sprout and rot** at high humidity (above 70% RH).
- Milk fat separates and texture is lost if frozen improperly.

Hence, food storage is a **matrix of customized conditions**, not a one-size-fits-all approach.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Food Storage

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | $T$ | $-30^\circ C$ to $+15^\circ C$ | Controls reaction rates and microbial growth |
| Relative Humidity | $RH$ | 60% to 100% | Governs moisture loss/gain |
| Oxygen | $O_2$ | 0% to 21% | Drives respiration and oxidation |
| Carbon Dioxide | $CO_2$ | 0% to 20% | Inhibits microbial growth |
| Water Activity | $a_w$ | 0.0 to 1.0 | Determines microbial safety |
| pH | $pH$ | 2.0 to 7.0 | Influences microbial growth and enzyme activity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** The goal of storage is to keep the food *as close as possible* to its post-harvest/post-process quality state by **slowing the rate** of the three primary deterioration pathways: **(1) Microbial growth, (2) Chemical/enzymatic reactions, (3) Physical changes (moisture loss, texture softening).**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Categories of Foods and Their Storage Requirements

### 2.1.1 Raw Fruits (Whole, Fresh)

Fresh fruits are **living, respiring tissues** that continue metabolic activity post-harvest. Two biological classes exist:

- **Climacteric fruits** (banana, apple, mango, tomato, pear): exhibit a *respiratory burst* with peak ethylene production during ripening. These can be harvested mature-green and ripened in storage.
- **Non-climacteric fruits** (citrus, grapes, berries, pineapple): do *not* show a respiratory peak; must ripen on the plant.

**Standard Storage Conditions for Major Fruits:**

| Fruit | Temperature ($^\circ C$) | RH (%) | $O_2$ (%) | $CO_2$ (%) | Approx. Shelf Life |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apple | $0$ to $1$ | $90$ to $95$ | $2$ to $3$ | $1$ to $2$ | $6$ to $12$ months (CA) |
| Banana (green) | $13$ to $15$ | $85$ to $90$ | $2$ to $5$ | $2$ to $5$ | $1$ to $4$ weeks |
| Mango | $12$ to $13$ | $85$ to $90$ | $3$ to $5$ | $5$ to $8$ | $2$ to $3$ weeks |
| Citrus (orange) | $5$ to $10$ | $85$ to $90$ | $5$ to $10$ | $0$ to $5$ | $1$ to $3$ months |
| Strawberry | $0$ to $1$ | $90$ to $95$ | $5$ to $10$ | $15$ to $20$ | $5$ to $7$ days |
| Grape | $0$ to $-1$ | $90$ to $95$ | $2$ to $5$ | $1$ to $3$ | $2$ to $6$ months |
| Pineapple | $7$ to $13$ | $85$ to $90$ | $2$ to $5$ | $5$ to $10$ | $2$ to $4$ weeks |
| Papaya | $7$ to $13$ | $85$ to $90$ | $2$ to $5$ | $5$ to $8$ | $1$ to $3$ weeks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students often confuse *temperature* with *humidity*. Both are equally important. For most fresh fruits, **high RH (85–95%)** is needed to prevent shriveling, but for **onions and garlic, low RH (60–70%)** is mandatory to prevent sprouting and decay.

### 2.1.2 Raw Vegetables (Whole, Fresh)

Vegetables are botanically diverse — *leaves, roots, tubers, bulbs, stems* — each with its own physiology.

**Standard Storage Conditions for Major Vegetables:**

| Vegetable | Temperature ($^\circ C$) | RH (%) | Special Note | Shelf Life |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lettuce / Leafy greens | $0$ to $1$ | $95$ to $100$ | Highly perishable; use MAP | $1$ to $3$ weeks |
| Tomato (ripe) | $12$ to $15$ | $85$ to $90$ | Chilling injury below $10^\circ C$ | $1$ to $2$ weeks |
| Potato (table) | $4$ to $10$ | $90$ to $95$ | Curing at $15-20^\circ C$ first | $5$ to $10$ months |
| Onion (dry) | $0$ to $1$ | $60$ to $70$ | Low RH prevents sprouting | $6$ to $9$ months |
| Carrot | $0$ to $1$ | $95$ to $100$ | Top icing recommended | $4$ to $6$ months |
| Cabbage | $0$ to $1$ | $95$ to $100$ | Late types store longer | $3$ to $6$ months |
| Garlic | $0$ to $1$ | $60$ to $70$ | Low RH; well-ventilated | $6$ to $9$ months |
| Cauliflower | $0$ to $1$ | $95$ to $100$ | Ethylene-sensitive | $2$ to $4$ weeks |
| Mushroom | $0$ to $1$ | $90$ to $95$ | Very high respiration rate | $3$ to $7$ days |
| Cucumber | $10$ to $13$ | $90$ to $95$ | Chilling sensitive | $10$ to $14$ days |

### 2.1.3 Meat (Raw and Processed)

Meat is **highly perishable** due to its high $a_w$ ($\approx 0.99$), neutral pH ($\approx 5.5$ to $6.5$), and rich nutrient content supporting bacterial growth (especially *Pseudomonas*, *Salmonella*, *Clostridium*).

**Storage Conditions for Meat and Meat Products:**

| Product | Temperature ($^\circ C$) | RH (%) | Method | Shelf Life |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fresh red meat (beef, lamb) | $-1$ to $1$ | $85$ to $90$ | Chilled | $1$ to $3$ weeks |
| Fresh poultry | $-1$ to $1$ | $85$ to $90$ | Chilled; ice-packed | $5$ to $7$ days |
| Fresh pork | $-1$ to $1$ | $85$ to $90$ | Chilled | $1$ to $2$ weeks |
| Frozen meat (all) | $-18$ to $-25$ | — | Frozen storage | $6$ to $12$ months |
| Vacuum-packaged meat | $0$ to $1$ | — | Vacuum + chill | $4$ to $6$ weeks |
| Cured meat (ham, bacon) | $3$ to $7$ | $70$ to $75$ | Chilled | $2$ to $6$ weeks |
| Sausages (fresh) | $0$ to $2$ | — | Chilled | $2$ to $5$ days |
| Cooked sausages | $0$ to $4$ | — | Chilled | $2$ to $4$ weeks |
| Fermented sausages | $10$ to $15$ | $70$ to $75$ | Dry/semi-dry | $1$ to $6$ months |
| Canned meat | $10$ to $25$ | — | Ambient | $2$ to $5$ years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Microbial Concern:** *Clostridium botulinum* (a spore-forming, anaerobic pathogen) is the principal hazard in vacuum-packaged and cured meats. It is controlled by keeping storage below $3.3^\circ C$ and/or ensuring $a_w \lt 0.94$ and/or $pH \lt 4.6$.

### 2.1.4 Dairy Products

Milk and milk products vary from highly perishable (fresh milk) to long-life (UHT, powder).

**Storage Conditions for Dairy Products:**

| Product | Temperature ($^\circ C$) | RH (%) | Method | Shelf Life |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw milk | $\le 4$ | — | Chilled bulk tank | $1$ to $2$ days |
| Pasteurized milk | $\le 4$ | — | Chilled; cold chain | $7$ to $14$ days |
| UHT milk | Ambient ($20$ to $30$) | — | Aseptic packaging | $3$ to $6$ months |
| Butter (unsalted) | $4$ | — | Chilled; wrapped | $1$ to $2$ weeks |
| Butter (salted) | $4$ | — | Chilled; wrapped | $2$ to $3$ months |
| Ghee | Ambient | — | Sealed metal/glass jar | $6$ to $12$ months |
| Hard cheese (Cheddar) | $4$ to $8$ | $80$ to $85$ | Cold-ripened | $6$ to $12$ months |
| Soft cheese (Mozzarella) | $1$ to $4$ | — | Chilled | $1$ to $3$ weeks |
| Yogurt | $2$ to $6$ | — | Chilled | $2$ to $3$ weeks |
| Ice cream | $-25$ to $-18$ | — | Hard-frozen | $2$ to $12$ months |
| Milk powder | Ambient (cool/dry) | $< 60$ | Hermetically sealed | $6$ to $24$ months |
| Condensed milk | $4$ to $10$ | — | Chilled | $1$ to $2$ years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Important:** Lipid oxidation (rancidity) is the major deterioration pathway for butter, ghee, and cheese. Protection from **light, oxygen, and high temperatures** is critical — that is why dairy products are typically packaged in **opaque, vacuum-sealed, or nitrogen-flushed** containers.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Storage Life — Detailed Analysis

The storage life (shelf life) of any food product is governed by a **combination of intrinsic and extrinsic factors**. These are summarized in the high-yield conceptual table below:

### 2.2.1 Intrinsic Factors (Within the Food)

| Factor | Mechanism of Action | Effect on Shelf Life |
|---|---|---|
| **Water activity ($a_w$)** | Determines availability of water for microbial and chemical reactions | Higher $a_w$ → shorter shelf life; foods with $a_w \lt 0.6$ are microbiologically stable |
| **pH** | Influences microbial growth (most bacteria prefer $pH$ 6.5–7.5; yeasts 4.5–6; molds 1.5–9) | Acidic foods ($\lt 4.6$) inhibit *C. botulinum* |
| **Nutrient content** | Proteins, lipids, vitamins are substrates for microbes and enzymes | Richer nutrient profile → more perishable |
| **Redox potential ($E_h$)** | Aerobes need positive $E_h$; anaerobes need negative | Anaerobic packaged foods favor *Clostridium* |
| **Natural antimicrobials** | E.g., lysozyme in eggs, lactoferrin in milk | Extend shelf life naturally |
| **Native enzymes** | Pectinases, proteases, lipases in fresh produce | Cause softening, off-flavors |
| **Initial microbial load** | Surface contamination from harvest/slaughter | Higher load → faster spoilage |

### 2.2.2 Extrinsic Factors (Storage Environment)

| Factor | Mechanism of Action | Typical Control Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage temperature** | Governs reaction kinetics via Arrhenius; affects microbial growth rate | $-30^\circ C$ to $+15^\circ C$ depending on commodity |
| **Relative humidity (RH)** | Controls moisture loss (wilting) or gain (mold) | 60% to 100% |
| **Atmospheric composition** | $O_2$ drives oxidation/respiration; $CO_2$ inhibits microbes and delays ripening | CA: 1–5% $O_2$, 0–10% $CO_2$ |
| **Light exposure** | Triggers photo-oxidation of lipids and vitamins; greening in potatoes | Dark storage preferred for dairy, oils |
| **Ethylene exposure** | Plant hormone that accelerates ripening of climacteric fruits | $< 1$ ppm in mixed storage |
| **Sanitation / hygiene** | Reduces initial microbial load and cross-contamination | HACCP-based protocols |
| **Packaging** | Provides barrier to $O_2$, moisture, light, microbes | Vacuum, MAP, CAP, aseptic |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Q10 Rule** | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_1 - T_2}}$ | Acceleration factor of reaction rate per $10^\circ C$ rise | $R_1, R_2$ = rates at $T_1, T_2$ (in $^\circ C$) |
| **Arrhenius Equation** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Temperature dependence of rate constant | $k$ = rate constant, $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = $8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K) |
| **Shelf-life ratio (Arrhenius)** | $\dfrac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$ | Ratio of shelf lives at two temperatures | $\theta_1, \theta_2$ = shelf lives at $T_1, T_2$ (K) |
| **Respiration rate** | $r_{CO_2} = \dfrac{m \cdot (Y_{CO_2})_{net}}{1000}$ | $CO_2$ produced per kg per hour | $m$ = mass (kg); $Y$ = mL/kg·h |
| **Water activity** | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Ratio of partial vapor pressure to saturation pressure | $p$ = vapor pressure of food; $p_0$ = vapor pressure of pure water |
| **Modified Arrhenius (shelf life)** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Linear form for graphical estimation of $E_a$ | $T$ in Kelvin |
| **Decimal reduction time (D-value)** | $D = \dfrac{t}{\log N_0 - \log N}$ | Time (min) to destroy 90% of microbes at given $T$ | $N_0$ = initial count, $N$ = survivors |
| **Z-value** | $z = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\log D_1 - \log D_2}$ | Temperature change to change D by factor of 10 | $T$ in $^\circ C$ |
| **Clausius-Clapeyron (moisture)** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)$ | Used in sorption isotherms | $T$ in K |
| **Mass loss by evaporation** | $\dot{m} = h_m \cdot A \cdot (p_s - p_a)$ | Drying/shriveling of fresh produce | $h_m$ = mass transfer coefficient, $A$ = area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Tables:** Absolute value bars $\vert x \vert$ in equations are written using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in the rendered answer sheet.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above framework is the foundation for:

1. **Cold Chain Logistics** — Designing refrigerated transport for perishable commodities (India's $300$ billion cold chain industry).
2. **Warehouse Engineering** — Sizing refrigeration, humidity control, and CA room systems.
3. **Packaging Selection** — Choosing barrier films, MAP mixes, oxygen scavengers, and ethylene absorbers.
4. **Shelf Life Modeling** — Predicting product expiry using Arrhenius kinetics, enabling Just-In-Time inventory.
5. **Quality Assurance** — Defining HACCP critical limits (e.g., chilled meat must stay below $4^\circ C$).
6. **Food Safety Regulation** — FSSAI 2011, Codex Alimentarius, and USFDA rely on these formulas for setting storage standards.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Q10 Rule from Arrhenius Kinetics

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Governing Law

The Arrhenius equation describes how a reaction rate constant $k$ varies with absolute temperature $T$:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy of the deterioration reaction (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **$8.314$ J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin**

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Q10 Expression

For two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ (both in Kelvin, since Arrhenius uses absolute temperature), the ratio of the rate constants is:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_2}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_1}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)}$$

Now define $Q_{10}$ as the factor by which the rate *increases* for a $10^\circ C$ *rise* in temperature. If $T_2 - T_1 = 10$ K (note: a 10°C difference is identical to a 10 K difference on the absolute scale), then:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{T+10}}{k_T} = e^{\frac{10 \, E_a}{R \, T (T + 10)}}$$

### 3.1.3 Solving for Activation Energy

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln Q_{10} = \frac{10 \, E_a}{R \, T (T + 10)}$$

Solving for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \frac{R \, T (T + 10) \, \ln Q_{10}}{10}$$

This allows experimental determination of $E_a$ from a single $Q_{10}$ measurement at a given temperature.

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A fresh pasteurized milk sample is observed to spoil in **7 days** when stored at $4^\circ C$. Using $Q_{10} = 3.5$ (typical for dairy spoilage), estimate the shelf life when stored at $14^\circ C$ (poor refrigeration scenario).

**Step 1:** Identify temperatures.
$T_1 = 4^\circ C$, $T_2 = 14^\circ C$, so $T_2 - T_1 = 10^\circ C$ — exactly the Q10 condition.

**Step 2:** Apply the Q10 rule.

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = Q_{10}^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}} = 3.5^{\frac{10}{10}} = 3.5$$

**Step 3:** The spoilage rate triples. Since shelf life is **inversely proportional** to spoilage rate:

$$\theta_2 = \frac{\theta_1}{3.5} = \frac{7 \text{ days}}{3.5} = 2.0 \text{ days}$$

> The milk spoils in just **2 days** at $14^\circ C$ versus 7 days at $4^\circ C$ — a 71% reduction in shelf life from a mere $10^\circ C$ rise.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shelf-Life Ratio from Arrhenius (General Case)

When temperatures differ by an amount *other* than 10°C, the general form is required.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Two storage temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ (in Kelvin). Shelf life $\theta$ is **inversely proportional** to rate constant $k$ (faster reaction = shorter shelf life):

$$\theta \propto \frac{1}{k}$$

Therefore the **shelf life ratio** is:

$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{A \, e^{-E_a / R T_1}}{A \, e^{-E_a / R T_2}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A packaged salad is stored at $5^\circ C$ and has a shelf life of **12 days**. The package accidentally gets placed in a $20^\circ C$ display. The deterioration $E_a = 75$ kJ/mol. Calculate the new shelf life.

**Step 1:** Convert to Kelvin.
$T_1 = 5 + 273.15 = 278.15$ K
$T_2 = 20 + 273.15 = 293.15$ K

**Step 2:** Substitute into the shelf-life ratio equation.

$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = e^{\frac{75000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{293.15} - \frac{1}{278.15} \right)}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the temperature term.

$$\frac{1}{293.15} - \frac{1}{278.15} = \frac{278.15 - 293.15}{293.15 \times 278.15} = \frac{-15.0}{81549.5} = -1.8394 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the exponent.

$$\frac{75000}{8.314} \times (-1.8394 \times 10^{-4}) = 9021.4 \times (-1.8394 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.6594$$

**Step 5:** Take the exponential.

$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = e^{-1.6594} = 0.1901$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\theta_2$.

$$\theta_2 = 0.1901 \times 12 \text{ days} = 2.28 \text{ days} \approx 2.3 \text{ days}$$

> The shelf life collapses from 12 days to **about 2.3 days** — a 5.3× reduction. This is why cold chain integrity is non-negotiable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Water Activity and Microbial Stability

The water activity $a_w$ is the most fundamental single predictor of microbial stability.

### 3.3.1 Bound vs. Free Water

In any food, water exists in two states:
1. **Free water** — available for chemical reactions and microbial growth.
2. **Bound water** — tightly held by proteins, sugars, salts (unavailable).

The proportion of free water is quantified by $a_w$:

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

Where $p$ = vapor pressure of water in the food, and $p_0$ = vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature.

### 3.3.2 Microbial Growth Thresholds

| $a_w$ Range | Microbial Inhibition |
|---|---|
| $a_w \gt 0.95$ | All pathogens grow (most fresh foods) |
| $0.91 \le a_w \le 0.95$ | *Staphylococcus aureus* inhibited |
| $0.87 \le a_w \lt 0.91$ | Most spoilage bacteria inhibited |
| $0.75 \le a_w \lt 0.87$ | Halophilic bacteria; most yeasts inhibited |
| $0.60 \le a_w \lt 0.75$ | Xerophilic molds; honey, dried fruit |
| $a_w \lt 0.60$ | Microbial growth impossible (milk powder, biscuits) |

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Salt Addition to Cheese

> **Problem:** A cheese curd has $a_w = 0.98$. Salt is added, binding free water. The vapor pressure of the salted curd drops to $2.56$ kPa at $25^\circ C$ (where $p_0 = 3.17$ kPa). Is the new $a_w$ safe from *C. botulinum* growth?

**Step 1:** Compute new $a_w$.

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{2.56}{3.17} = 0.8076$$

**Step 2:** Compare to the *C. botulinum* growth threshold.

The minimum $a_w$ for *C. botulinum* growth is $\approx 0.94$.

Since $0.8076 \lt 0.94$:

> **Result:** Salt addition has brought the cheese into the *safer* region. *C. botulinum* cannot grow, dramatically extending safe storage life.

---

## 3.4 Respiration Rate and Shelf Life of Climacteric Fruits

### 3.4.1 Definition

The respiration rate $r$ is the rate at which a fruit consumes $O_2$ and produces $CO_2$ (mL per kg per hour). It is the **master clock** of fresh produce deterioration.

$$r_{CO_2} = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{m \cdot t}$$

A higher $r$ means a **shorter** shelf life — the fruit burns through its reserves faster.

### 3.4.2 Relation to Storage Temperature (Q10 of Respiration)

Typical $Q_{10}$ for respiration:
- **Apples:** $Q_{10} \approx 2.5$ to $3.5$
- **Bananas:** $Q_{10} \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$
- **Strawberries:** $Q_{10} \approx 3.0$ to $4.0$

> **Worked Example:** A banana respires at $r_1 = 35$ mL $CO_2$/(kg·h) at $13^\circ C$. Estimate the rate at $25^\circ C$ (room temperature) using $Q_{10} = 2.5$.

**Step 1:** Temperature rise = $25 - 13 = 12^\circ C$.

**Step 2:** General Q10 formula.

$$r_2 = r_1 \cdot Q_{10}^{(T_2 - T_1)/10} = 35 \cdot 2.5^{12/10} = 35 \cdot 2.5^{1.2}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the exponent.

$$2.5^{1.2} = e^{1.2 \cdot \ln 2.5} = e^{1.2 \cdot 0.9163} = e^{1.0996} = 3.003$$

**Step 4:** Final respiration rate.

$$r_2 = 35 \times 3.003 = 105.1 \text{ mL CO}_2 / (\text{kg} \cdot \text{h})$$

> The banana's respiration rate **triples** at room temperature — explaining why unrefrigerated bananas ripen and spoil in days.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Storage Decision Logic

The following table maps *food category → primary deterioration pathway → critical storage parameter → recommended technology*:

| Food Category | Dominant Deterioration Pathway | Critical Storage Parameter | Recommended Technology |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fresh fruits (climacteric) | Respiration / ethylene | Low $T$, low $O_2$, low $C_2H_4$ | CA / MAP + refrigeration |
| Fresh fruits (non-climacteric) | Mold growth, dehydration | Low $T$, high RH | Forced-air cooling, RH control |
| Leafy vegetables | Wilting, browning | $0^\circ C$, $>95\%$ RH | Hydro-cooling, MAP, ice |
| Root / tuber vegetables | Sprouting, weight loss | Cool, moist, dark | Curing + cool storage |
| Onions / garlic | Sprouting, rotting | Cool, *low* RH, ventilated | Ambient + low RH |
| Fresh red meat | Microbial spoilage (Pseudomonas) | $\le 1^\circ C$, $O_2$ exclusion | Vacuum packaging, chilling |
| Poultry | *Salmonella, Campylobacter* | $\le 1^\circ C$ | Ice-packing, MAP (high $CO_2$) |
| Cured meat | Lipid oxidation, *C. botulinum* | $3$ to $7^\circ C$, $a_w \lt 0.94$ | Vacuum / $N_2$ flush |
| Raw milk | Psychrotrophs (Pseudomonas) | $\le 4^\circ C$ | Plate cooler, insulated tank |
| Pasteurized milk | Post-pasteurization recontamination | $\le 4^\circ C$, short cold chain | Cold chain logistics |
| Butter / ghee | Lipid oxidation, light-induced off-flavor | $4^\circ C$ to $-18^\circ C$, dark, low $O_2$ | Foil wrap, $N_2$ flush |
| Cheese | Mold growth, proteolysis, $H_2O$ loss | $1$ to $8^\circ C$, $80$–$85\%$ RH | Wax coating, vacuum, gas flush |
| Ice cream | Recrystallization, texture loss | $\le -18^\circ C$, no temp cycling | Hard-frozen, $-25^\circ C$ storage |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define water activity ($a_w$) and explain its significance in determining the shelf life of stored foods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Water activity $a_w$ is defined as the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food ($p$) to the vapor pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

It ranges from 0 (bone-dry) to 1.0 (pure water).

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. **Microbial stability:** Each microbial group has a minimum $a_w$ below which it cannot grow. For example, most bacteria require $a_w > 0.91$, most yeasts $> 0.87$, and most molds $> 0.75$. By lowering $a_w$ below these thresholds (e.g., drying, salting, sugaring), microbial growth is arrested.
2. **Chemical reaction control:** Lipid oxidation, enzymatic browning, and non-enzymatic Maillard reactions all accelerate at higher $a_w$. Lowering $a_w$ slows these reactions and extends shelf life.
3. **Texture and stability:** Many crisp foods (chips, biscuits) require intermediate $a_w$ (~0.3–0.5) to remain crunchy; higher values make them soggy.
4. **Predictive tool:** $a_w$ is the single most reliable intrinsic indicator for predicting shelf life and selecting packaging requirements.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: What is the Q10 rule? How is it used to predict the shelf life of a stored food product?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Q10 rule states that the rate of a chemical, biochemical, or microbial reaction changes by a constant factor (the Q10 value) for every $10^\circ C$ change in temperature. It is a simplified form of the Arrhenius equation:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{R_{T+10}}{R_T}$$

The Q10 values for food deterioration reactions typically lie between **2 and 4**, with **3 being a common default** when experimental data is unavailable.

**Application (1.5 marks):**
- To predict shelf life at one temperature when known at another: $\theta_2 = \theta_1 / Q_{10}^{\Delta T/10}$.
- To estimate the *acceleration factor* for accelerated shelf-life testing (ASLT) — e.g., a 6-month ambient study can be compressed to weeks by holding samples at elevated temperatures and back-calculating to ambient using the Q10 rule.
- To design cold chain specifications: shows why even small temperature abuses (5–10°C) cause large reductions in shelf life.

**Example (bonus):** Milk with $Q_{10} = 3$ and $\theta_{4^\circ C} = 7$ days has $\theta_{14^\circ C} = 7/3 = 2.3$ days.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Two alternative questions (A or B), each with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**Q: (a) Discuss in detail the storage conditions for raw fruits. Differentiate between climacteric and non-climacteric fruits with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the concept of Controlled Atmosphere (CA) storage. Describe its principle, gas composition, and its applications for extending shelf life of fresh produce. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution: Part A(a) (7 marks)

**1. Introduction to Raw Fruit Storage (1 mark):**
Raw fruits are living, respiring tissues. They continue metabolic activity after harvest, consuming $O_2$, producing $CO_2$ and ethylene ($C_2H_4$), losing moisture, and undergoing softening. Storage aims to slow these processes to extend shelf life.

**2. Climacteric vs. Non-Climacteric Fruits (3 marks):**

| Property | Climacteric | Non-Climacteric |
|---|---|---|
| Respiration | Sharp rise (climacteric peak) at ripening | Low and steady throughout |
| Ethylene production | High, autocatalytic | Low |
| Ripening | Can continue after harvest | Must ripen on plant |
| Harvest stage | Mature-green | Fully ripe |
| Examples | Apple, banana, mango, tomato, pear, papaya | Citrus, grape, strawberry, pineapple, cherry, pomegranate |
| Storage approach | CA / MAP to delay ripening | Cool storage + mold control |

**3. Storage Conditions (2 marks):**
- **Temperature:** 0–13°C depending on chilling sensitivity. Bananas at 13–15°C (chilling injury below 12°C), apples at 0–1°C, mangoes at 12–13°C.
- **Relative Humidity:** 85–95% to prevent moisture loss and shriveling.
- **Atmosphere:** CA of 2–3% $O_2$, 1–2% $CO_2$ for apples extends storage to 6–12 months; for bananas, 2–5% $O_2$ + 2–5% $CO_2$ delays ripening.
- **Ethylene control:** Scrubbers (KMnO4-based) and exclusion of mixed storage.

**4. Conclusion (1 mark):** Effective storage of raw fruits requires matching temperature, humidity, and atmospheric composition to the specific fruit's physiology, with strict ethylene management.

---

#### Model Solution: Part A(b) (7 marks)

**1. Definition and Principle (2 marks):**
Controlled Atmosphere (CA) storage is a technique in which the **concentration of $O_2$, $CO_2$, and $N_2$ in the storage environment is precisely regulated** and maintained at non-atmospheric levels throughout the storage period. The principle is to **reduce $O_2$ and elevate $CO_2$** to suppress respiration, delay ripening, and inhibit microbial growth — thereby extending shelf life beyond what refrigeration alone can achieve.

**2. Typical Gas Composition (2 marks):**

| Food | $O_2$ (%) | $CO_2$ (%) | $N_2$ (%) | Temperature ($^\circ C$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apple | 1–3 | 1–2 | 95–98 | 0–1 |
| Pear | 2–3 | 0–1 | 96–98 | -1 to 0 |
| Cabbage | 2–5 | 2–5 | 90–96 | 0 |
| Kiwi | 1–2 | 3–5 | 93–96 | 0 |
| Avocado | 2–5 | 3–10 | 85–95 | 5–13 |

**3. Engineering Setup (2 marks):**
- Airtight, gas-tight cold room with **pressure-relief valves**.
- **$O_2$ scrubbers** (e.g., catalytic burners, molecular sieves) to maintain low $O_2$.
- **$CO_2$ absorbers** (activated carbon, water scrubbers) or controlled injection.
- Continuous **gas analyzers** (IR for $CO_2$, paramagnetic for $O_2$).
- High humidity (90–95% RH) maintained via humidifiers.

**4. Applications and Advantages (1 mark):**
- Apples: shelf life extended from 3 months (regular cold) to 6–12 months.
- Reduces respiration rate by 50–60%, ethylene action suppressed.
- Combined with refrigeration, allows year-round global supply chains.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**Q: (a) Explain the storage conditions for meat and meat products. Discuss the role of vacuum packaging and modified atmosphere packaging (MAP) in meat preservation. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the factors affecting the storage life of dairy products. What are the recommended storage conditions for fluid milk, cheese, and butter? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution: Part B(a) (7 marks)

**1. Why Meat is Highly Perishable (1 mark):**
Meat has $a_w \approx 0.99$, near-neutral pH (5.5–6.5), abundant nutrients, and high moisture — making it an ideal substrate for *Pseudomonas*, *Salmonella*, *Lactobacillus*, and *Clostridium* species.

**2. Storage Conditions by Product Type (3 marks):**

| Product | Temperature ($^\circ C$) | Method | Shelf Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fresh red meat | -1 to 1 | Chilled, RH 85–90% | 1–3 weeks |
| Fresh poultry | -1 to 1 | Chilled, ice-packed | 5–7 days |
| Frozen meat | -18 to -25 | Frozen storage | 6–12 months |
| Cured meat (ham) | 3 to 7 | Chilled, RH 70–75% | 2–6 weeks |
| Cooked sausage | 0 to 4 | Chilled | 2–4 weeks |
| Canned meat | Ambient | Hermetically sealed | 2–5 years |

**3. Vacuum Packaging (1.5 marks):**
- Removes air ($O_2$ drops to <1%) using an airtight film.
- Inhibits aerobic spoilage bacteria (*Pseudomonas*) and oxidative rancidity.
- Extends chilled shelf life 2–3× (e.g., beef from 1–2 weeks to 4–6 weeks).
- **Caution:** Anaerobic conditions favor *C. botulinum*; must keep temperature below $3.3^\circ C$ and/or $a_w < 0.94$ and/or $pH < 4.6$.

**4. Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) (1.5 marks):**
- Replaces air with a tailored gas mix — typically 60–80% $O_2$ (preserves red color via oxymyoglobin) for red meat, or high $CO_2$ (20–30%) + low $O_2$ for poultry.
- $CO_2$ is the key antimicrobial gas — it dissolves in the food's water phase, lowering surface pH and inhibiting Gram-negative spoilage bacteria.
- $N_2$ is used as an inert filler to prevent package collapse.

**5. Conclusion (1 mark):** Proper storage of meat requires integrated control of temperature, $O_2$ exclusion, and $a_w$/pH barriers, with packaging serving as the critical engineering intervention.

---

#### Model Solution: Part B(b) (7 marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Dairy products are perishable because milk has $a_w \approx 0.99$, near-neutral pH (6.6), and is rich in lactose, protein, and lipids — ideal for psychrotrophic bacteria (*Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus*) and pathogens (*Listeria*, *Salmonella*).

**2. Factors Affecting Storage Life of Dairy (3 marks):**

| Factor | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Temperature | Governs psychrotroph growth rate; $Q_{10} \approx 3$ for milk spoilage |
| Initial microbial load | Total Plate Count (TPC) right after pasteurization determines days to spoilage |
| Light | Riboflavin-sensitized photo-oxidation → "light-activated" off-flavor in milk |
| Oxygen | Lipid oxidation in butter, cheese |
| Packaging | Opaque vs. transparent bottles; $O_2$ barrier |
| $a_w$ and pH | Fermented dairy (yogurt, cheese) extends life via lowered pH and reduced $a_w$ |
| Time-temperature history | Each break in cold chain shortens remaining shelf life exponentially |

**3. Storage Conditions for Specific Products (2 marks):**

**Fluid Milk:**
- Raw milk: $\le 4^\circ C$, bulk tank, 1–2 days.
- Pasteurized milk: $\le 4^\circ C$ throughout cold chain, 7–14 days.
- UHT milk: ambient, aseptic packaging, 3–6 months.
- ESL (Extended Shelf Life): $\le 6^\circ C$, 14–21 days.

**Cheese:**
- Hard cheese (Cheddar, Parmesan): 4–8°C, RH 80–85%, 6–12 months.
- Soft cheese (Mozzarella, Brie): 1–4°C, 1–3 weeks.
- Vacuum or gas-flush packaging to prevent mold and moisture loss.

**Butter:**
- Salted butter: 4°C, 2–3 months; or -18°C for long term.
- Unsalted: 4°C, 1–2 weeks; -18°C for long-term.
- Wrapped in aluminum foil (light + O2 barrier); $N_2$ flush for industrial packs.

**4. Conclusion (1 mark):** Storage life of dairy is best protected by an unbroken cold chain (4°C), light-resistant opaque packaging, low $O_2$ headspace, and — for shelf-stable forms — UHT or drying.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **The "Temperature Only" Trap (most common mistake):** Students often list only the temperature for each food and forget to mention **RH, atmospheric composition, and packaging**. A 7-mark question on storage conditions *requires* all four parameters. Stating only temperature will earn at most **3–4 marks** out of 7.
>
> 2. **Confusing Climacteric Categories:** Mixing up climacteric vs. non-climacteric examples (e.g., calling grapes climacteric, or apple non-climacteric) is a **deduction of 1 mark per error**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Microbial Constraints:** In questions on meat and dairy, students write storage temperatures but **forget to mention *C. botulinum* control** ($a_w < 0.94$, $pH < 4.6$, $T < 3.3^\circ C$). This is a **frequently tested 2-mark sub-question**.
>
> 4. **Units Error in Q10 Calculation:** Temperatures in Q10/Arrhenius must be in **Kelvin** for Arrhenius, but in **°C** for Q10. Students frequently mix these and lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Generic Answers:** "Keep it cold and dry" is a **zero-mark answer**. Always quantify: state exact °C, exact % RH, exact % $O_2$/$CO_2$.
>
> 6. **Missing Ethylene:** Any question on mixed fruit storage that does not mention ethylene management is incomplete (1 mark deduction).
>
> 7. **Numerical Errors in Arrhenius:** A common mistake is computing $\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}$ with $T$ in °C instead of K, leading to **negative or impossible results**. Always convert first.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **A. Core Definitions (must memorize):**
> - **Storage condition** = controlled set of physical, chemical, and microbiological parameters.
> - **Water activity** ($a_w$) = $p / p_0$; key predictor of microbial stability.
> - **Q10** = factor of rate change per $10^\circ C$ rise; for foods, typically 2–4.
> - **Climacteric fruit** = shows respiratory burst + autocatalytic ethylene; can ripen off plant.
> - **Non-climacteric** = no respiratory burst; ripens only on plant.
> - **CA storage** = regulated low $O_2$ + elevated $CO_2$ throughout storage.
> - **MAP** = sealed package with one-time gas modification.
> - **Chilling injury** = damage to tropical produce stored below 10–13°C.
> - **D-value** = time for 90% kill of microbes at given T.
> - **Z-value** = °C change needed to change D-value by factor of 10.

> **B. Magic Numbers to Memorize for Exams:**
> - Most fresh fruits/vegetables: **0–1°C, 90–95% RH**.
> - Onions, garlic: **0°C, 60–70% RH** (low RH to prevent sprouting).
> - Banana: **13–15°C** (NOT below 12°C — chilling injury).
> - Mango: **12–13°C**.
> - Tomato (ripe): **12–15°C** (NOT below 10°C).
> - Apple CA: **2–3% $O_2$, 1–2% $CO_2$** → 6–12 months.
> - Fresh red meat: **-1 to 1°C**.
> - Frozen meat: **-18 to -25°C**.
> - Fluid pasteurized milk: **≤ 4°C**.
> - C. botulinum control: **$a_w < 0.94$, $pH < 4.6$, $T < 3.3°C$**.
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
> - Safe $a_w$ for mold inhibition: **< 0.70**.

> **C. The "Three Deterioration Routes" Framework (universal answer template):**
> 1. **Microbial** — controlled by temperature, $a_w$, pH, atmosphere, sanitation.
> 2. **Chemical/Enzymatic** — controlled by temperature, light exclusion, antioxidants, $O_2$ exclusion.
> 3. **Physical** — controlled by RH, protective packaging, mechanical handling.

> **D. The Five Must-List Parameters for *Any* Storage Question:**
> 1. Temperature (°C)
> 2. Relative Humidity (%)
> 3. Atmospheric gas composition (% $O_2$, % $CO_2$)
> 4. Packaging type
> 5. Shelf life result (in days/weeks/months)

> **E. Common Exam Question Patterns:**
> - *"Discuss storage conditions for X with examples"* → Use the master template (T, RH, $O_2$, $CO_2$, packaging, shelf life).
> - *"Explain Q10 / Arrhenius with an example"* → Show the derivation + a numerical problem.
> - *"Compare A and B storage methods"* → Tabulate differences, mention at least 3 quantitative parameters.
> - *"What happens if Y is stored at wrong temperature?"* → Chilling injury, accelerated respiration, microbial outgrowth.
> - *"Why is refrigeration the most important control?"* → It attacks all three deterioration routes simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Handling Equipment: Belt, Chain, Screw, Roller, Pneumatic Conveyors and Bucket Elevators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT633 - FOOD STORAGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Material Handling Equipment: Belt, Chain, Screw, Roller, Pneumatic Conveyors and Bucket Elevators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Material Handling Equipment (MHE) in Food Storage Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Material Handling Equipment (MHE)** refers to the mechanical, electromechanical, or pneumatic systems used to transport, lift, stack, load, unload, and store food materials — both in **bulk (granular/powder)** and **packaged (unit-load)** form — within storage godowns, silos, warehouses, processing plants, and distribution centers. As per KTU 2024 syllabus, the major classifications studied are **Belt Conveyors, Chain Conveyors, Screw Conveyors, Roller Conveyors, Pneumatic Conveyors, and Bucket Elevators.**

In the context of **Food Storage Engineering**, MHE is not just a convenience — it is the **circulatory system** of any storage facility. Without proper MHE, the integrity of stored grains, flours, sugar, pulses, and processed foods is compromised due to manual handling errors, spillage, contamination, and delay.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Logistics Skeleton" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine a hospital. The doctors, medicines, and equipment are useless if they cannot reach the patient in time. **Conveyors and elevators are the arteries of a food storage godown** — they ensure that a 50-tonne truck of paddy unloaded at the gate reaches the silo (via belt conveyor), gets elevated to the top of the silo (via bucket elevator), and the finished rice bag comes out the other end (via roller conveyor) — all without a single human lifting a grain. The choice of MHE depends on **WHAT** is being moved, **HOW FAR**, **AT WHAT ANGLE**, and **HOW CLEANLY** (hygienic standard).

### 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU 2024

| **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome** | **Why it Matters** |
|------------------------------|--------------------|
| LO-4.1: Classify MHE for grains/flour | Storage without transport is a "dead warehouse" |
| LO-4.2: Calculate capacity and power | Determines motor selection and electricity load (BEE audit) |
| LO-4.3: Select conveyor for food type | Prevents cross-contamination, breakage, dust explosion |
| LO-4.4: Design bucket elevators | Vertical lift = fundamental to silo loading |

### 1.4 Broad Classification Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Classification (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- **1. Continuous Conveyors**
  - 1.1 Belt Conveyors
  - 1.2 Chain Conveyors
  - 1.3 Screw Conveyors (Augers)
  - 1.4 Roller Conveyors
  - 1.5 Pneumatic Conveyors
  - 1.6 Bucket Elevators (technically discontinuous but grouped here)

- **2. Intermittent / Unit-Load**
  - 2.1 Forklifts (excluded from this module scope)
  - 2.2 Cranes, Hoists (excluded)

### 1.5 Standard Physical & Design Constants

- **Bulk density of wheat** : **780 kg/m³**
- **Bulk density of rice** : **750 kg/m³**
- **Bulk density of flour** : **500 kg/m³**
- **Angle of repose (wheat)** : **27°**
- **Standard conveyor belt speed** : **1.0 – 3.5 m/s**
- **Standard screw conveyor speed** : **50 – 150 rpm**
- **Standard bucket elevator speed** : **1.0 – 2.5 m/s**
- **Standard gravity (g)** : **9.81 m/s²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Most numerical problems will give you bulk density ρ, belt/pipe diameter D, and velocity v. You must calculate **mass flow rate Q (kg/s or TPH)** and **power P (kW)** as the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BELT CONVEYORS

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A belt conveyor is a continuous transport device where a **flexible endless belt** (rubber, PVC, food-grade PU) carries bulk material on its upper surface, supported by **idler rollers**, and is driven by a **head/drive pulley** with return over a **tail pulley**.

#### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
- The **drive pulley** imparts frictional traction on the belt.
- The belt forms a **troughed profile** (typically 20°, 35°, or 45° trough angle) using 3-idler sets to maximize load cross-section.
- Material is loaded via a **chute** at the tail end and discharged via a **head pulley scraper or throw-off**.

#### 2.1.2 Troughing Idler Geometry
- 3-Idler set: 2 outer idlers at 20° from horizontal, 1 horizontal center idler.
- This forms a **trough cross-section** with effective carrying surface wider than flat belt → higher capacity.

#### 2.1.3 Working Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. Material is fed through the loading chute at the tail.
2. The troughed belt forms a "U" shape, increasing load volume.
3. Drive pulley rotates → friction pulls belt → material travels horizontally or inclined.
4. At the head pulley, belt direction changes; material falls off due to gravity into a discharge chute.
5. Return idlers support the empty belt on the underside back to the tail.

#### 2.1.4 Capacity Formula
The mass flow rate of a belt conveyor is given by:

$$Q = A \times v \times \rho$$

Where:
- Q = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- A = cross-sectional area of load (m²)
- v = belt speed (m/s)
- ρ = bulk density of material (kg/m³)

#### 2.1.5 Troughed Belt Cross-Sectional Area

$$A = 0.5 \times \left[ (b + 0.4 \times b) + b \right] \times h = 0.5 \times b \times (1.4 + 1) \times h$$

But for **KTU standard**, the simplified area for a 3-idler troughed belt at 20° is:

$$A = 0.1036 \times b^2$$

Where **b** is belt width in meters. This empirical formula is valid for typical troughing angles and idler configurations used in food grain handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Maximum lump size = **b/5** (b in meters), for a 3-idler troughed belt carrying crushed grain or pulse.

#### 2.1.6 Belt Power Equation
The power required to drive a horizontal belt conveyor is:

$$P_h = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) \times v}{1000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

For an inclined belt, the net power becomes:

$$P = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) \times v}{1000} \pm \frac{Q \times g \times H}{1000}$$

Where:
- T₁ = tight-side tension (N)
- T₂ = slack-side tension (N)
- Q = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- H = vertical lift (m)
- "−" for downgrade, "+" for upgrade

#### 2.1.7 Effective Tension (Eytelwein / Lewis Formula)
For starting/breaking:

$$T_e = 33000 \times L \times \left( Q + Q_b \right) + \frac{Q \times H}{1000}$$

Where Q_b = belt weight factor, L = center-to-center length.

### 2.2 CHAIN CONVEYORS

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Chain conveyors use one or more **endless chains** with attached **flights, slats, or pushers** to drag material along a trough or guide. They are preferred over belt conveyors for **abrasive, hot, or oily** food materials (e.g., raw sugar, salt, oilseeds).

#### 2.2.1 Types
- **Drag (Flight) Conveyors** — single strand with flights in an enclosed trough.
- **Apron (Pans) Conveyors** — overlapping pans for hot/scalding materials.
- **En-Masse Conveyors** — chain moves through a closed casing filled with material.

#### 2.2.2 Capacity of Drag Conveyor
$$Q = 3600 \times A \times v \times \rho$$

For rectangular trough:

$$A = b \times h \quad \text{(m}^2\text{)}$$

Where:
- b = trough width (m)
- h = flight height (m)
- v = chain speed (typically 0.25 – 0.6 m/s)

### 2.3 SCREW CONVEYORS (AUGERS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A screw conveyor consists of a **helical screw blade (auger flight)** mounted on a central shaft, rotating inside a **U-trough or tubular casing**, to push material by **axial thrust** generated by the helical geometry.

#### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
- Each flight acts as a **continuous inclined plane**.
- Material is trapped between the flight and trough wall.
- The helix angle generates an **axial (forward) component** of velocity.

#### 2.3.2 Helix Angle (θ)
$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{S}{\pi D}$$

Where:
- S = screw pitch (m)
- D = screw diameter (m)
- θ = helix angle (typically 15° – 25°)

#### 2.3.3 Capacity of Screw Conveyor (KEY KTU FORMULA)
The volumetric capacity of a standard pitch screw is:

$$Q_{vol} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times S \times N \times 60 \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/hr)}$$

Where:
- D = screw diameter (m)
- S = pitch (m), typically S = D
- N = rotational speed (rpm)

The **mass flow rate** is:

$$Q_{mass} = Q_{vol} \times \rho \times \eta_f \quad \text{(kg/hr or TPH)}$$

Where:
- ρ = bulk density (kg/m³)
- η_f = **fill coefficient** (typically 0.15 – 0.45 for horizontal, decreasing for inclined)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fill Coefficient Table (KTU Standard):**
> - Horizontal (0°): **0.45**
> - 15° incline: **0.30**
> - 30° incline: **0.20**
> - 45° incline: **0.10**
> - Vertical: special design (screw lift)

#### 2.3.4 Power for Screw Conveyor
$$P = \frac{Q \times L \times F_o}{367} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

Where:
- Q = mass flow rate (TPH)
- L = conveyor length (m)
- F_o = material factor (1.0 – 4.0; e.g., wheat = 1.0, damp wheat = 2.0)

### 2.4 ROLLER CONVEYORS

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A roller conveyor consists of a series of **freely-rotating cylindrical rollers** mounted in a frame, supporting unit loads (boxes, bags, crates) on their outer surface. The load moves by **gravity (gravity roller)** or by a **motorized drive (powered roller)**.

#### 2.4.1 Classification
- **Gravity Roller Conveyor (GRC)** — slight downward slope (3°–5°), load moves by gravity.
- **Live (Powered) Roller Conveyor (LRC)** — chain, belt, or motorized rollers drive the load.
- **Accumulation Conveyor** — allows temporary stacking of loads.

#### 2.4.2 Roller Design Load
Maximum load per roller (W) is governed by bending stress:

$$W = \frac{\sigma_b \times \pi \times d^3}{32 \times L_s} \quad \text{(N per roller)}$$

Where:
- σ_b = allowable bending stress (N/m²)
- d = roller shaft diameter (m)
- L_s = span between bearings (m)

#### 2.4.3 Minimum Roller Spacing
To prevent sagging of bagged/unit loads:

$$s_{min} = \frac{L_{load}}{3}$$

Where L_load is the length of the unit load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Storage Context:** Roller conveyors are exclusively used in the **bagged goods section** of storage godowns (rice bags, flour bags, sugar bags). They are **NOT** used for bulk grains.

### 2.5 PNEUMATIC CONVEYORS

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A pneumatic conveyor transports **dry, granular food materials** (flour, sugar, starch, grain dust) suspended in a **gas (air) stream** through a closed pipeline by pressure difference.

#### 2.5.1 Operating Principle
- A **blower/compressor** creates air pressure difference.
- Material is **fluidized** — it behaves as a fluid.
- The air-material mixture flows through ducts.

#### 2.5.2 Two Operating Modes
| **Mode** | **Pressure** | **Application** |
|----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Dilute Phase** | Low pressure (vacuum or low positive) | Light, friable materials (flour, sugar) |
| **Dense Phase** | High pressure (up to 5 bar) | Heavy, abrasive materials (grain, pellets) |

#### 2.5.3 Air Velocity (Saltation Velocity)
The minimum conveying velocity to prevent particle settling:

$$v_{salt} = 1.3 \times \sqrt{\frac{g \times d_p \times (\rho_p - \rho_a)}{\rho_a}}$$

Where:
- d_p = particle diameter (m)
- ρ_p = particle density (kg/m³)
- ρ_a = air density (≈1.2 kg/m³)
- ρ_p - ρ_a ≈ ρ_p for solids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (Food Materials):**
> - Flour: v ≈ **18 – 22 m/s**
> - Wheat: v ≈ **25 – 32 m/s**
> - Rice: v ≈ **20 – 25 m/s**

#### 2.5.4 Pressure Drop (Darcy-Weisbach + Solids Loading)
Total pressure drop in a pneumatic line:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{air} + \Delta P_{solids} + \Delta P_{acceleration}$$

For dilute phase, the solids contribution is:

$$\Delta P_{solids} = \mu \times \Delta P_{air}$$

Where μ = **solids loading ratio** = (mass flow of solid / mass flow of air), typically 1–10 for food.

#### 2.5.5 Conveying Capacity
$$Q = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times v \times \rho_{mix}$$

Where ρ_mix is the mixture density (typically 2–50 kg/m³ for dilute phase).

#### 2.5.6 Power Requirement
$$P = \frac{Q_{air} \times \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}} \quad \text{(W)}$$

Where Q_air is volumetric air flow (m³/s) and η_fan is fan efficiency (0.6 – 0.85).

### 2.6 BUCKET ELEVATORS

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A bucket elevator is a vertical (or near-vertical) continuous conveyor where **buckets** fixed to a **belt or chain** scoop up bulk material at the **boot (bottom)** and discharge it at the **head (top)** by centrifugal or gravity action.

#### 2.6.1 Two Major Types
- **Centrifugal Discharge** — high speed (1.5 – 2.5 m/s), buckets throw material out at head pulley.
- **Continuous (Gravity) Discharge** — slow speed (0.5 – 1.0 m/s), material spills out at top of up-leg.

#### 2.6.2 Bucket Capacity Equation
$$Q = \frac{i \times V \times N \times \rho}{1000} \quad \text{(TPH or kg/s depending on units)}$$

Where:
- i = number of buckets per meter of belt
- V = volume of one bucket (m³ or litres)
- N = belt speed (m/s)
- ρ = bulk density (kg/m³)

#### 2.6.3 Spacing and Pitch
$$i = \frac{1}{a}$$

Where a = bucket spacing (m) along the belt/chain.

#### 2.6.4 Power for Bucket Elevator
$$P = \frac{Q \times H \times g}{3600 \times \eta} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

Where:
- Q = mass flow (kg/hr)
- H = lift height (m)
- g = 9.81 m/s²
- η = overall efficiency (0.7 – 0.85)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discharge Type Selection Rule:**
> - For **fine, free-flowing** material (flour, sugar) → **Centrifugal**
> - For **coarse, lumpy, fragile** material (grain, pulse, rice) → **Continuous (Positive)**

### 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Module 4. Memorize units carefully.

| **Equipment** | **Key Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Boundary / Typical Range** |
|---------------|-----------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Belt Conveyor | $Q = A \times v \times \rho$ | Q (kg/s), A (m²), v (m/s), ρ (kg/m³) | v = 1.0 – 3.5 m/s |
| Belt (Trough) | $A = 0.1036 \times b^2$ | b = belt width (m) | b = 0.4 – 1.6 m |
| Belt Power | $P = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) v}{1000}$ | T (N), v (m/s) | η_motor = 0.85 – 0.92 |
| Chain (Drag) | $Q = 3600 \times b \times h \times v \times \rho$ | b,h (m), v (m/s) | v = 0.25 – 0.6 m/s |
| Screw Helix | $\tan(\theta) = \frac{S}{\pi D}$ | S (m), D (m) | θ = 15° – 25° |
| Screw Capacity | $Q_{vol} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 S N \times 60$ | D,S (m), N (rpm) | m³/hr |
| Screw Mass | $Q_{mass} = Q_{vol} \times \rho \times \eta_f$ | ρ (kg/m³), η_f dimensionless | η_f = 0.10 – 0.45 |
| Screw Power | $P = \frac{Q \times L \times F_o}{367}$ | Q (TPH), L (m), F_o = 1–4 | kW |
| Roller Load | $W = \frac{\sigma_b \pi d^3}{32 L_s}$ | σ_b (N/m²), d (m), L_s (m) | N/roller |
| Pneumatic Velocity | $v_{salt} = 1.3 \sqrt{\frac{g d_p \rho_p}{\rho_a}}$ | d_p (m), ρ (kg/m³) | m/s |
| Pneumatic ΔP | $\Delta P = \Delta P_{air}(1 + \mu)$ | ΔP (Pa), μ = 1–10 | dilute phase |
| Bucket Capacity | $Q = i \times V \times N \times \rho / 1000$ | i (1/m), V (m³), N (m/s) | TPH |
| Bucket Power | $P = \frac{Q H g}{3600 \eta}$ | Q (kg/hr), H (m), η = 0.7–0.85 | kW |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on `|` symbol:** In all calculations, absolute values or "such that" conditions have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in handwritten work, e.g., $\tan \theta = S \div (\pi D)$ to avoid breaking the table syntax above.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Belt Conveyors** → Loading/unloading of bagged and bulk grain at ports and FCI godowns.
- **Chain Conveyors** → Bottom ash, hot clinker (in food: oilseed processing, sugar mills).
- **Screw Conveyors** → Flour mills, rice mills for in-plant transfer.
- **Roller Conveyors** → Warehousing of 50 kg rice/flour bags in storage godowns.
- **Pneumatic Conveyors** → Pneumatic ship unloaders for grain terminals; flour transfer in mills.
- **Bucket Elevators** → Vertical silo loading; rice mill paddy intake.
